verses.flow: The proverbs (mishlei) of Solomon, son of David, king of Israel:
verses.note: HEBREW: "Mishlei" (Strong's H4912) comes from the root mashal, meaning a comparison, parable, or pithy saying. The title links authorship to Solomon (Shelomoh), whose name derives from shalom, peace — itself a royal-messianic resonance. TYPOLOGY: Solomon as the paradigmatic wise king foreshadows the greater Son of David in whom "are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge" (Col 2:3; Matt 12:42).

verses.flow: To know wisdom (chokmah) and discipline (musar), to understand words of discernment (binah);
verses.note: HEBREW: "Chokmah" (H2451) denotes practical skill and mastery — wisdom as artisanship, not merely abstract knowledge. "Musar" (H4148) means discipline through instruction and sometimes correction by suffering. "Binah" (H998) carries the sense of discernment that separates and distinguishes between things. These three terms form the programmatic vocabulary of the entire book.

verses.flow: To receive instruction in wise dealing, in righteousness (tsedaqah) and justice and equity;
To give prudence (ormah) to the simple, knowledge and discretion to the youth—

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Let the wise hear and increase in learning, and the one of understanding acquire guidance (tachbulot),
To understand a proverb and a figure, the words of the wise and their riddles.
The fear of YHWH is the beginning (reshit) of knowledge; wisdom and discipline the foolish despise.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Reshit" (H7225) means beginning, first-fruits, or foundational principle — not merely the starting point chronologically but the governing root of all knowledge. The fear of YHWH is thus the epistemological foundation of the entire wisdom tradition. TYPOLOGY: The New Testament identifies Christ as the one "in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom" (Col 2:3), making the fear of YHWH inseparable from knowing the Son (John 17:3). THEOLOGICAL: The antithesis — that fools despise wisdom and discipline — establishes the two-ways structure that runs throughout Proverbs and connects to Psalm 1's two-paths motif.

verses.flow: Hear, my son, the discipline of your father, and forsake not the teaching (torah) of your mother;
verses.note: HEBREW: "Torah" (H8451) here carries its broader sense of instruction or teaching rather than the Mosaic law code specifically, though the same word is used for both. "Musar" (discipline) from the father and "torah" from the mother reflects the covenant household as the primary school of wisdom. HISTORICAL: In the ANE, both Mesopotamian and Egyptian wisdom traditions were transmitted through father-to-son pedagogical speech, giving Proverbs a form recognizable across cultures while filling it with distinctly Yahwistic content.

verses.flow: For they are a graceful wreath upon your head, and chains of ornament about your neck.

words.g: as/like Hades or the world of the dead

verses.flow: Let us swallow them alive like Sheol, and whole, like those who descend to the pit;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Sheol" (H7585) is the realm of the dead, the underworld — here invoked by the wicked as a boast of their devouring power. The imagery makes the gang of sinners a kind of anti-creation force, swallowing the living as Sheol swallows the dead. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Sheol in the Hebrew cosmology is a subterranean realm under divine oversight (Job 26:6; Ps 139:8), making the sinners' casual invocation of it a form of unconscious blasphemy.

verses.flow: For their feet run to evil, and they hasten to shed blood.
For in vain the net is spread in the eyes of any bird;
verses.note: HEBREW: The proverb in verse 17 functions as a parenthetical observation from nature: even a bird recognizes the net spread before it. The irony is stark — the sinner is portrayed as less discerning than a bird. STRUCTURE: This nature illustration interrupts the direct address to heighten the folly of the enticement before returning to its deadly conclusion in verse 18.

words.g: to/for blood of man or an animal

verses.flow: But they themselves lie in wait for their own blood, they ambush their own lives.
Such are the ways of everyone who gains unjust gain; it takes away the life of its possessors.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Betsa" (H1215) — unjust gain or greedy profit — is presented not merely as a moral failure but as a self-defeating trap. The life that is seized through violence or greed turns and seizes its pursuer. This is a fundamental moral principle in Proverbs: sin carries its own penalty within its own logic (cf. Gal 6:7-8).

verses.flow: Wisdom (chokmah) cries aloud outside; in the open squares she raises her voice;
verses.note: HEBREW: "Chokmah" is here personified (cf. Prov 8-9) and appears in a plural intensive form "chokhmot," sometimes translated "Wisdom herself" — a grammatical form that intensifies the majesty and fullness of the concept. THEOLOGICAL: Personified Wisdom calling in the public square is a striking literary device: divine wisdom is not hidden or esoteric but publicly proclaimed. TYPOLOGY: The loud, public cry of Wisdom anticipates the proclamation of the Word (John 1:1-14) and Christ's own public teaching (John 7:37, "If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink").

verses.flow: At the head of the noisy streets she calls out; at the entrance of the city gates she speaks her words:
"How long, O simple ones, will you love simplicity? And scoffers delight in their scoffing, and fools hate knowledge?
Turn to my reproof; behold, I will pour out my spirit (ruach) to you, I will make my words known to you.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ruach" (H7307) can mean spirit, breath, or wind. Wisdom's promise to "pour out my ruach" echoes prophetic language of divine Spirit-endowment (Joel 2:28-29; Isa 44:3). TYPOLOGY: This verse creates a remarkable typological link between Lady Wisdom and the divine Spirit who gives life and understanding. The Pentecost fulfillment of Joel's prophecy (Acts 2) stands as the eschatological outpouring of what Wisdom here promises to those who turn to her reproof.

verses.flow: Then they will call upon me, but I will not answer; they will seek me diligently but will not find me.
Because they hated knowledge and did not choose the fear of YHWH,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The refusal of the fear of YHWH is here identified as the root of all folly and the cause of divine abandonment. The sequence is instructive: hatred of knowledge leads to rejection of YHWH's fear, which leads to rejection of counsel, which leads to consuming the bitter fruit of one's own way. STRUCTURE: Verses 29-31 form an inverted echo of verses 2-4: the purposes for which Wisdom was given are precisely what the fool has refused.

verses.flow: They would not accept my counsel; they despised all my reproof.

verses.flow: But whoever listens to me shall dwell secure (betach), and shall be at ease from dread of harm."
verses.note: HEBREW: "Betach" (H983) means security, safety, trust — dwelling without anxiety or dread. The final word of the chapter ("harm," ra'ah) contrasts the fate of the wise with that of fools described in verse 32. THEOLOGICAL: The chapter closes with a covenant-shaped promise: those who hear Wisdom's voice receive shalom-like security, while those who reject her receive exactly what they chose. This is not arbitrary punishment but the natural consequence of wisdom or folly. TYPOLOGY: The one who "listens to me" echoes the royal-prophetic summons of Christ: "My sheep hear my voice" (John 10:27), and "whoever hears my word and believes him who sent me has eternal life" (John 5:24).

verses.flow: My son, if you receive my words and treasure up my commandments with you,
verses.note: HEBREW: The address "my son" (beni, H1121) frames the entire chapter as a father's formal instruction — a genre common in ANE wisdom literature, paralleling Egyptian texts like the Instruction of Amenemope. The verbs "receive" (laqach, H3947) and "treasure up" (tsaphan, H6845) imply active, deliberate retention — not passive hearing but intentional custody of the word.

yes, if you call out for discernment, and lift up your voice for understanding;

verses.flow: if you seek her as silver, and search for her as for hidden treasures;
then you will understand the fear of YHWH, and find the knowledge of God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The conditional structure of vv. 1-5 culminates here: the disciplined pursuit of wisdom leads not merely to intellectual gain but to knowing YHWH himself. "Fear of YHWH" (yirat YHWH) is the controlling epistemological framework of the entire wisdom tradition (cf. Prov 1:7; Job 28:28; Ps 111:10). Knowledge of God is the telos of the search.

verses.flow: For YHWH gives wisdom; from his mouth come knowledge and understanding;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse grounds all human wisdom in divine origin — YHWH is the source, not merely an endorser, of true wisdom. This has direct theological bearing on the identification of Wisdom with the Logos in the NT (John 1:1-3; 1 Cor 1:30). The phrase "from his mouth" (mipiv) suggests personal, spoken revelation, not merely general natural order.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: he stores up sound wisdom (tushiyyah) for the upright; he is a shield to those who walk in integrity,
verses.note: HEBREW: "Sound wisdom" translates tushiyyah (H8454), a rare and difficult term unique to wisdom literature (Job, Proverbs, Micah), possibly derived from a root meaning "substance" or "abiding success." It denotes practical, effective wisdom that produces real outcomes, not merely theoretical knowledge. "Integrity" (tom, H8537) conveys wholeness and moral completeness.

verses.flow: guarding the paths of justice, and watching over the way of his faithful ones (hasidim).
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "His faithful ones" renders hasidim (H2623), those bound to YHWH by covenant loyalty (hesed). The concept of YHWH "watching over" (shamar) a defined people group carries covenantal overtones analogous to the divine council's assigned guardianship of nations (Deut 32:8-9), with YHWH himself as guardian of Israel.

verses.flow: Then you will understand righteousness and justice and equity, every good path.
For wisdom will enter your heart, and knowledge will be pleasant to your soul (nephesh);
verses.note: HEBREW: "Soul" translates nephesh (H5315), the animate life-force or self — not merely the immaterial soul of later Greek-influenced thought, but the whole animated person. Wisdom entering the "heart" (lev, H3820) means it penetrates the seat of will, reason, and moral disposition in Hebrew anthropology.

verses.flow: delivering you from the way of evil, from the man who speaks perverse things,
from those who forsake the paths of uprightness to walk in the ways of darkness,
who rejoice in doing evil and delight in the perverseness of evil,
those whose paths are crooked, and who are devious in their ways.

verses.flow: Delivering you also from the forbidden woman, from the adulteress with her smooth words,
who forsakes the companion (allup) of her youth and forgets the covenant of her God;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "covenant of her God" (berit eloheyha) is most naturally read as the marriage covenant made before God — a binding oath with divine witness. This implies that adultery is not merely a social transgression but a covenant violation against YHWH himself (cf. Mal 2:14). The forsaking of the "companion of her youth" (allup) echoes the imagery of Israel's apostasy from YHWH.

verses.flow: for her house sinks down to death, and her paths to the shades (rephaim);
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Shades" translates rephaim (H7496), the ancient Hebrew term for the spirits of the dead inhabiting Sheol. The rephaim appear also in Ugaritic texts as a class of venerated ancestral spirits associated with the underworld, suggesting the foreign woman's path leads not only to physical death but to the realm of the spiritually disinherited. (cf. Isa 14:9; Ps 88:10)

verses.flow: none who go to her come back, nor do they attain the paths of life.
So you will walk in the way of the good, and keep to the paths of the righteous.
For the upright will inhabit the land, and those of integrity will remain in it;
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Inhabit the land" (yishkenu-arets) echoes the Deuteronomic promise of land inheritance conditioned on covenant faithfulness (Deut 28:1-14), and anticipates the Beatitude "the meek shall inherit the earth" (Matt 5:5). The righteous remnant's possession of the land ultimately finds its fulfillment in the new creation inheritance of Messiah's people (Rom 4:13; Rev 21:7).

verses.flow: but the wicked will be cut off from the land, and the treacherous will be uprooted from it.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Cut off" (yikkaretu, from karat, H3772) is the same root used in the covenant formula "cut off from the people" — signifying excision from the covenantal community. The parallel of the upright inheriting the land (v. 21) and the wicked being uprooted (yissachu) forms a deliberate antithetical chiasm, closing the chapter with the two-ways motif foundational to biblical ethics (cf. Ps 1:6; Deut 30:15-20).

verses.flow: My son, do not forget my Torah (torah), and let your heart keep my commandments,
verses.note: HEBREW: "Torah" (H8451, from yarah, "to instruct") is used here for a father's teaching, mirroring the use of the same word for the Mosaic Law. The parallelism with "commandments" (mitzvot, H4687) frames the father's instruction as carrying quasi-covenantal weight, reflecting the household as a microcosm of the covenant community.

verses.flow: for length of days and years of life and shalom (shalom) they will add to you.

verses.flow: Let not hesed (hesed) and faithfulness forsake you; bind them around your neck, write them on the tablet of your heart,
verses.note: HEBREW: "Hesed" (H2617) denotes covenant loyalty, steadfast love, and lovingkindness — it is one of the richest theological terms in the Hebrew Bible. "Faithfulness" (emet, H571) complements hesed as its pair throughout the Psalms and prophets (cf. Ps 85:10). Writing on the heart foreshadows the new covenant promise of Jer 31:33.

verses.flow: and you will find favor and good understanding in the eyes of Elohim (elohim) and man.
Trust in YHWH with all your heart, and do not lean upon your own understanding.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse stands as a paradigmatic statement of covenant epistemology — human understanding (binah, H998) is not rejected but subordinated to trust in YHWH. The verb "trust" (batach, H982) implies a leaning of the whole person's weight. The contrast with "your own understanding" (binatkha) warns against autonomous rationalism divorced from divine revelation.

verses.flow: In all your ways acknowledge him, and he will make straight your paths.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Acknowledge him" (da'ehu, from yada', H3045) carries relational and covenantal knowledge, not merely intellectual recognition. "Make straight" (yashar, H3474) implies YHWH actively smoothing and directing the path — a sovereign guidance motif found throughout the wisdom tradition and Psalms (cf. Ps 37:23).

verses.flow: Do not be wise in your own eyes; fear YHWH and turn away from evil.
It will be healing (refu'ah) to your navel and refreshment to your bones.

The discipline (musar) of YHWH, my son, do not despise, and do not loathe his rebuke,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This verse is quoted directly in Hebrews 12:5-6 as evidence that divine discipline is a mark of sonship. The author of Hebrews applies it to the suffering of believers under persecution, reading YHWH's fatherly correction as the pattern fulfilled in the relationship between God and the church as his children through Christ (Heb 12:5-11).

verses.flow: for whom YHWH loves he rebukes, even as a father the son in whom he delights.

verses.flow: Blessed is the man who finds wisdom (chokhmah), and the man who draws out understanding (tevunah),
verses.note: HEBREW: "Chokhmah" (H2451, wisdom) and "tevunah" (H8394, understanding, from bin, "to discern between") are the two primary wisdom terms of Proverbs. "Draws out" (yafik, from puk) suggests wisdom must be actively extracted, like water drawn from a well — wisdom does not come passively but requires diligent seeking.

verses.flow: for her gain is better than the gain of silver, and her profit than fine gold.
She is more precious than rubies (peninim), and all the things you desire cannot compare with her.
Length of days is in her right hand; in her left hand are riches and honor.
Her ways are ways of pleasantness, and all her paths are shalom.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal participle active + suffix

verses.flow: She is a tree of life to those who lay hold of her; those who hold her fast are called blessed.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Tree of life" (etz chayyim, H6086 + H2416) directly echoes Gen 2:9 and 3:22-24, and anticipates the restoration of the tree of life in Rev 22:2. Wisdom personified as the tree of life frames her as restoring what the Fall forfeited. Early Christian interpreters (Origen, Irenaeus) saw wisdom here as a type of Christ, in whom are hidden all treasures of wisdom (Col 2:3).

verses.flow: YHWH by wisdom founded the earth; by understanding he established the heavens;
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The role of wisdom in creation (cf. Prov 8:22-31) places her alongside YHWH at the cosmic founding. This verse closely parallels Jer 10:12 and Ps 104:24. Second Temple Jewish thought (cf. Sirach 24, 1 Enoch 42) developed wisdom's role as a hypostasis mediating between YHWH and creation, providing the conceptual background for the Logos theology of John 1:1-3.

verses.flow: by his knowledge the deeps were broken open, and the clouds drop down dew.
My son, let these not slip from your eyes; keep sound wisdom (tushiyyah) and discretion (mezimmah),
verses.note: HEBREW: "Tushiyyah" (H8454) is a rare and rich term often rendered "sound wisdom" or "abiding success" — its root is debated but may relate to yasha' (to save/deliver), suggesting wisdom that is effective and life-sustaining. "Mezimmah" (H4209, discretion/purposeful thought) is morally neutral in root but positive in wisdom contexts, referring to prudent scheming for good ends.

verses.flow: Do not contend with a man without cause, if he has done you no harm.
Do not envy a man of violence, and do not choose any of his ways,
for the devious are an abomination (to'evah) to YHWH, but his intimate counsel (sod) is with the upright.
verses.note: HEBREW: "To'evah" (H8441, abomination) is a term of cultic and moral horror — used for idolatry and gross sin in Leviticus and Deuteronomy — its application here to the "devious" (napalot, the crooked or deviant) shows that ethical perversity carries the same weight as ritual uncleanness. "Sod" (H5475) refers to the intimate council or inner circle — used of the divine council in Ps 89:7 and Jer 23:18, here applied to YHWH's fellowship with the upright.

verses.flow: The curse of YHWH is on the house of the wicked, but he blesses the dwelling of the righteous.
Toward the scornful he is scornful, but to the humble he gives grace (chen).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is quoted verbatim in both James 4:6 and 1 Peter 5:5, functioning as a foundational principle of the New Testament theology of grace. The word "scornful" (letz, H3887) describes one who mocks wisdom's call. "Chen" (H2580, grace/favor) given to the humble (anawim, H6035) anticipates the Beatitudes (Matt 5:3-5) and the theology of grace given to those who do not presume upon their own sufficiency.

verses.flow: The wise shall inherit honor, but fools bear away disgrace.

verses.flow: Hear, O sons, the instruction of a father, and give attention to know understanding (binah);
verses.note: HEBREW: "binah" (H998, from bin, "to discern") denotes perceptive understanding, the ability to distinguish between things. The plural "sons" (banim) may indicate this was a formal wisdom school setting in ancient Israel, not merely private fatherly counsel. STRUCTURE: Chapter 4 opens a series of three fatherly addresses (vv.1-9, 10-19, 20-27), each urging the son to pursue wisdom as the path to life.

verses.flow: for I give you good teaching (leqach); do not forsake my torah (torah).
verses.note: HEBREW: "leqach" (H3948) means "teaching received," what is taken in from instruction — emphasizing the receptive dimension of learning. "Torah" (H8451) here is used in its pre-Mosaic sense of authoritative parental instruction, though it carries covenantal resonance for readers familiar with Sinai. THEOLOGICAL: The father places his teaching on the same level of urgency as divine torah, reflecting the household as the primary covenantal transmission unit in ancient Israel.

verses.flow: For I was a son to my father — tender and the only one before my mother —

This pattern of obedience-as-life foreshadows Christ as the living Word who is himself the Way, the Truth, and the Life (John 14:6), in whom all wisdom is hidden (Col 2:3).

The beginning of wisdom (chokmah) is this: acquire wisdom, and with all your acquiring, acquire understanding (binah).
verses.note: HEBREW: "reshit chokmah" — "the beginning/chief thing of wisdom." The word "reshit" (H7225) can mean both "beginning" in time and "chief" in rank, as in Genesis 1:1 ("In the beginning") and Proverbs 8:22. THEOLOGICAL: The verse is paradoxical: to get wisdom, you must first acquire wisdom. This calls attention to the volitional act of valuing and pursuing wisdom before possessing it fully — an act of faith-like commitment.

Those who embrace divine Wisdom are adorned with honor — a theme developed in the NT with Christ as the believer's wisdom and righteousness (1 Cor 1:30).

For they eat the bread of wickedness and drink the wine of violence.
But the path of the righteous is like the light of dawn, shining brighter and brighter until the full day.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between the path of the righteous as increasing light and the way of the wicked as deep darkness (v.19) is one of the most developed light/darkness moral dualisms in the Hebrew Bible outside of apocalyptic literature. TYPOLOGY: The "shining brighter and brighter until the full day" anticipates the eschatological dawn motif fulfilled in Christ, the Light of the world (John 8:12; 2 Pet 1:19), and the consummate day of glory (Rev 22:5).

verses.flow: The way of the wicked is like deep darkness; they do not know over what they stumble.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Deep darkness" translates "afelah" (H653), the densest form of darkness in Hebrew, used of the plague darkness in Exodus 10:22. The wicked stumble without knowing the cause — a picture of moral blindness that is both judicial and experiential. DIVINE_COUNCIL: This moral dualism of light and darkness echoes cosmological themes found in texts like 1QS (Community Rule) from Qumran, which drew heavily on Proverbs' two-path theology to describe the cosmic battle between the Prince of Light and the Angel of Darkness.

words.m: verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: My son, give attention to my words; incline your ear to my sayings.

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Let them not depart from your eyes; keep them in the midst of your heart.

words.m: preposition + verb qal participle active + suffix

verses.flow: For they are life to those who find them, and healing (marpe) to all their flesh.
verses.note: HEBREW: "marpe" (H4832) means "healing" or "medicine," from the root rapha (to heal, the basis of the divine name YHWH Rapha). THEOLOGICAL: The claim that words of wisdom bring healing to "all their flesh" bridges the spiritual and physical — wisdom rightly received restores the whole person. This anticipates the NT integration of spiritual truth and bodily life (3 John 1:2).

verses.flow: Above all keeping, guard your heart, for from it flow the springs of life.
verses.note: HEBREW: "mishmar lev" — "keep/guard the heart." The heart (lev, H3820) in Hebrew thought is not merely the seat of emotion but of intellect, will, and moral direction — the integrating center of the person. "From it flow the springs (totsa'ot) of life" — "totsa'ot" (H8444) can mean "goings out" or "outflows," the same word used in boundary descriptions (Josh 17:9). THEOLOGICAL: This verse is foundational to a biblical anthropology: external behavior flows from an internal source. It is the OT foundation for Christ's teaching in Matthew 12:34 and 15:19 and undergirds the new covenant promise of a transformed heart (Ezek 36:26).

verses.flow: Put away from you a crooked mouth, and put perverse lips far from you.
Let your eyes look directly forward, and let your eyelids look straight before you.
Make level the track of your foot, and let all your ways be established.
Do not turn to the right hand or to the left; turn your foot away from evil.
verses.note: HEBREW: The prohibition against turning "to the right or the left" echoes a deuteronomic covenant formula (Deut 5:32; 17:11, 20; Josh 1:7), placing this wisdom instruction within the covenantal framework of Israel's national calling. THEOLOGICAL: The entire chapter closes with the image of a straight, guarded path — forming an inclusio of sorts with the opening call to instruction. Life is fundamentally a directed journey, and wisdom is the discipline of staying on course.

words.m: verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: My son, attend to my wisdom (chokmah); incline your ear to my understanding (tevunah),
that you may preserve discretion (mezimmah), and your lips may guard knowledge (da'at).
verses.note: HEBREW: "mezimmah" (Strong's H4209) derives from the root "zamam," meaning to plan or devise. In Proverbs it carries a positive sense of purposeful, guarded thinking — discretion that wards off moral danger. Contrast its negative use in Prov 12:2. "Da'at" (H1847) is relational knowledge, not merely intellectual information, tied to covenant fidelity and intimacy with YHWH.

verses.flow: For the lips of a foreign woman (zarah) drip honey, and her palate is smoother than oil;
verses.note: HEBREW: "zarah" (H2114) literally means "a strange woman" or one who is foreign — a term signaling someone outside the covenant community or outside the bounds of legitimate marriage. The image of honey-dripping lips echoes the seductive rhetoric of the foreign woman throughout Prov 1-9, forming a sustained literary foil to Lady Wisdom (Prov 8-9). TYPOLOGY: The seduction of the zarah functions typologically as an image of idolatry — Israel's unfaithfulness to YHWH portrayed as adultery (cf. Jer 3:1-10; Hos 1-3).

verses.flow: but her end is bitter as wormwood, sharp as a double-edged sword.

words.g: Hades or the world of the dead

verses.flow: Her feet go down to death; her steps take hold of Sheol.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Sheol" (H7585) is the Hebrew realm of the dead — not merely a metaphor for destruction, but the underworld conceived as a real domain. The path of the adulteress is portrayed as literally descending toward that realm, underscoring the mortal stakes of moral compromise. The two-step descent — "death" then "Sheol" — suggests progressive, irreversible ruin (cf. Prov 9:18).

verses.flow: She does not ponder the path of life; her ways are unstable — she does not know it.
And now, O sons, hear me, and do not turn aside from the words of my mouth.
Keep your way far from her, and do not go near the door of her house,
lest you give your honor to others, and your years to the cruel one;
lest strangers be filled with your strength, and your labors go to the house of a foreigner,

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: and you groan at your latter end, when your flesh and your body are consumed,

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: and you say, "How I hated discipline (musar), and my heart despised reproof!
verses.note: HEBREW: "musar" (H4148) is a rich term encompassing discipline, correction, chastisement, and instruction — often with the sense of corrective suffering that forms character. It is the dominant word in Proverbs for the formative education of the wise. The retrospective lament here (vv. 12-14) functions as a dramatic speech-within-a-speech, giving the reader a vicarious glimpse of future regret as a present warning.

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb piel participle active + suffix

verses.flow: I did not listen to the voice of my teachers, nor did I incline my ear to my instructors.
I was nearly in all evil in the midst of the assembly (qahal) and congregation."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Qahal" (H6951) and its synonym "edah" refer to the formal assembly of Israel — the covenant community gathered before YHWH. The shame of open moral failure "in the midst of the assembly" reflects the social and covenantal dimension of sin in ancient Israel; private vice had communal consequences and public disgrace.

verses.flow: Drink water from your own cistern, and flowing water from your own well.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The cistern/well imagery draws on a well-established Ancient Near Eastern metaphor for marital sexuality (cf. Song of Songs 4:15). The command to "drink from your own cistern" is a positive affirmation of the goodness and exclusivity of covenant marriage. TYPOLOGY: The faithful wife as life-giving water contrasts with the bitter wormwood of the adulteress (v. 4), forming a structural antithesis that mirrors the Wisdom/Folly contrast of chapters 8-9.

verses.flow: Should your springs be scattered abroad, streams of water in the open squares?
Let them be for yourself alone, and not for strangers with you.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Let your fountain be blessed, and rejoice in the wife of your youth.

words.m: verb piel jussive 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: A lovely doe, a graceful mountain goat — let her breasts satisfy you at all times; be intoxicated always in her love (ahavah).
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb "shagah" (H7686), translated "be intoxicated," elsewhere describes being led astray or wandering — its use here is deliberately ironic and erotic, sanctifying within marriage the kind of captivating, disorienting love that the foreign woman offers illegitimately (cf. v. 20 where the same verb recurs). "Ahavah" (H160) is the standard Hebrew word for love, rooted in deep affective attachment.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel imperfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Why should you be intoxicated, my son, with a foreign woman (zarah), and embrace the bosom of an adulteress (nokriyah)?
For the ways of a man are before the eyes of YHWH, and he watches all his paths.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse forms the theological anchor of the chapter: YHWH's omniscient moral governance underlies all the practical warnings. The father's counsel is not merely prudential wisdom but is grounded in divine accountability. The phrase "before the eyes of YHWH" (neged einei YHWH) echoes the covenantal framework in which all human conduct is evaluated (cf. Prov 15:3; Heb 4:13).

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: The iniquities of the wicked ensnare him, and he is held fast in the cords of his sin.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The image of iniquity as a cord or snare reflects the Hebrew understanding of sin as an entangling, enslaving power — not merely a moral failing but a force with quasi-personal agency that binds and destroys (cf. Ps 140:5; John 8:34). The passive construction "he is held fast" (yittamek) emphasizes the loss of freedom and agency that habitual sin produces.

verses.flow: He dies for lack of discipline (musar), and because of his great folly he goes astray.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Musar" reappears here closing the chapter, forming an inclusio with verse 12. The one who rejected discipline ends in death — the very consequence the father warned against. "Folly" here is "ivveleth" (H200), the ethical and moral foolishness of one who has rejected the fear of YHWH as the foundation of wisdom (Prov 1:7), not mere intellectual dullness.

verses.flow: My son, if you have put up surety for your neighbor, if you have struck your hands in pledge for a stranger —
verses.note: HEBREW: "Struck your hands" (taqaʿ kaph, Strong's H8628 + H3709) refers to the ancient Near Eastern practice of sealing a financial pledge with a handshake or clap, legally binding the guarantor. The warning addresses the real danger of becoming financially liable for another's debt — a recurring theme in Proverbs (Prov 11:15; 17:18; 22:26).

verses.flow: you are snared by the words of your mouth, you are caught by the words of your mouth.

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal imperative 2nd masc sing

words.g: in/with the hollow hand or palm

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Do this, then, my son, and deliver yourself, for you have come into the hand of your neighbor: go, humble yourself (hithrappes), and press urgently upon your neighbor.
Give no sleep to your eyes, nor slumber to your eyelids.
Deliver yourself like a gazelle from the hand of the hunter, and like a bird from the hand of the fowler.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The urgent imagery of self-deliverance — like a gazelle (tsvi, Strong's H6643) fleeing a snare — foreshadows the NT call to flee the entanglements of sin and the world (2 Tim 2:22; Heb 12:1). The double animal metaphor intensifies the imperative of decisive, undelayed action.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Go to the ant, O sluggard; consider her ways, and be wise.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The ant (nemalah, Strong's H5244) as a wisdom exemplar reflects ancient ANE nature wisdom, paralleled in Egyptian wisdom texts. Ants were observed in the agricultural landscape of ancient Canaan storing grain before winter. The address "O sluggard" (atsel, Strong's H6102) is a distinctive Proverbs character type appearing seven times in chapters 6, 10, and 26.

verses.flow: Without having a chief, an officer, or a ruler,
she prepares her bread in summer, she gathers her food in harvest.
How long will you lie there, O sluggard? When will you arise from your sleep?
A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest —

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: and your poverty will come upon you like a vagrant, and your want like an armed man.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Vagrant" (mehallek, a Piel participle meaning "one who walks about") and "armed man" (ish magen, "man of shield/weapon") paint poverty as an aggressive, inevitable invader. The image underscores that laziness is not passive — its consequences are violent and swift.

verses.flow: A worthless person (adam beliyyaal), a wicked man, walks with a crooked mouth;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Adam beliyyaal" (Strong's H1100) — literally "a man of worthlessness/nothingness" — is a significant moral category in Wisdom and historical literature (cf. 1 Sam 25:17; 2 Sam 16:7). Beliyyaal later becomes a proper demonic name in Second Temple literature and 2 Cor 6:15. The portrait here is of a character whose entire manner of communication — body and speech — is corrupted.

verses.flow: he winks with his eyes, scrapes with his feet, points with his fingers;
with a perverted heart he devises evil continually; he sows discord (midyanim).
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Sows discord" (midyanim, Strong's H4090, from riv, "strife/contention") is listed twice in this chapter (vv. 14, 19) as among the chief abominations YHWH hates. Strife-sowing has a cosmic dimension — Satan as the accuser and divider mirrors this human pattern at the heavenly level (Job 1-2; Rev 12:10).

verses.flow: Therefore his calamity will come suddenly; in an instant he will be broken beyond healing.
There are six things YHWH hates, seven that are an abomination (toevah) to him:
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Verses 16-19 form a classic Hebrew numerical proverb (x, x+1), a literary device appearing also in Amos 1-2 and Prov 30. The structure "six... seven" (shesh... sheva) is not arithmetic but rhetorical — emphasizing completeness and building to a climactic final item. The list culminates (v. 19b) in the same sin as v. 14, forming a literary bracket.

words.g: blood of man or an animal

verses.flow: haughty eyes, a lying tongue, and hands that shed innocent blood,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Innocent blood" (dam naqiy, Strong's H1818 + H5355) is a covenantal category — shedding it defiles the land (Num 35:33) and cries out to YHWH (Gen 4:10). "Haughty eyes" (eynayim ramot, literally "high/lifted eyes") heads the list as the root vice, reflecting the consistent biblical axiom that pride is the genesis of all moral disorder (Prov 16:18; Isa 2:11).

verses.flow: a heart that devises wicked plans, feet that are swift to run to evil,
a false witness who breathes out lies, and one who sows discord among brothers.
My son, keep the commandment (mitzvah) of your father, and forsake not the teaching (torah) of your mother.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Commandment of your father... teaching (torah) of your mother" echoes the Shema's call to parental transmission of divine instruction (Deut 6:6-9). In its canonical context, this parental torah ultimately points to the divine Father's instruction embodied in Wisdom — and in Christ, who is the wisdom of God (1 Cor 1:24; Col 2:3).

verses.flow: Bind them upon your heart continually; tie them around your neck.

verses.flow: When you walk, it will lead you; when you lie down, it will watch over you; and when you awake, it will speak with you.
For the commandment is a lamp (ner) and the teaching is a light (or), and the reproofs of discipline are the way of life,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "Commandment is a lamp... teaching is a light" (ner... or) directly anticipates Ps 119:105 ("Your word is a lamp to my feet and a light to my path") and NT affirmations of Scripture as illuminating (2 Pet 1:19). The progression lamp → light → way of life suggests a covenantal hermeneutic: instruction received leads to life.

words.m: preposition + verb qal infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: to keep you from the evil woman, from the smooth tongue of the foreign woman (nokriyyah).

words.m: verb qal jussive 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Do not desire her beauty in your heart, and do not let her capture you with her eyelids.

words.g: in up to or over against

verses.flow: For the cost of a prostitute is only a loaf of bread, but a married woman hunts down a precious life.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verse is syntactically difficult in the MT. "A loaf of bread" (kikkar lechem) may indicate the prostitute's fee is ruinously cheap — implying a man reduces himself to commodity — while the adulteress (eshet ish, "woman of a man/wife") hunts the precious soul (nefesh yeqarah). The contrast is poverty of the body versus destruction of the soul.

words.m: particle + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Can a man carry fire in his bosom and his garments not be burned?
Or can a man walk on hot coals and his feet not be scorched?
So is he who goes in to his neighbor's wife; none who touches her will go unpunished.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "None who touches her will go unpunished" (lo yinnaqeh, Strong's H5352 Niphal, literally "will not be held innocent/clean") uses the same root as the Decalogue's warning that YHWH "will not hold guiltless" (lo yenaqeh) the one who takes His name in vain (Exod 20:7). The moral grammar of adultery is mapped onto covenant violation language.

words.g: to/for to fill or be full of

verses.flow: People do not despise a thief if he steals to satisfy his appetite when he is hungry;

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: yet if he is caught, he will pay sevenfold; he will give all the wealth of his house.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: He who commits adultery with a woman lacks heart (chasar lev); he who does it destroys his own soul.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Lacks heart" (chasar lev, Strong's H2638 + H3820) is a uniquely Hebraic idiom meaning deficient in moral understanding and self-governance, not merely emotional feeling. The lev (heart) in Hebraic anthropology is the seat of the will, intellect, and character. This is the strongest condemnation in the pericope — adultery is not weakness but the mark of a fundamentally disordered inner man.

verses.flow: Wounds and dishonor he will get, and his reproach will not be wiped away.
For jealousy (qinah) is the rage of a man, and he will not spare in the day of vengeance.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Jealousy (qinah, Strong's H7068) is the rage of a man" — this term is the same word used of YHWH's own divine jealousy (Exod 20:5; Deut 4:24), grounding the severity of marital betrayal in covenantal categories. Marriage in the OT is a covenant (Mal 2:14), and its violation provokes a response analogous to covenant treachery against YHWH Himself.

verses.flow: He will not accept the face of any ransom, nor will he be content though you multiply gifts.
My son, keep my words, and treasure up my commandments with you.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing

words.g: as/like the little man of the eye

verses.flow: Keep my commandments and live, and my teaching (torah) as the apple of your eye.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Apple of your eye" renders 'ishon einekha' (H380/H5869), literally "little man of your eye" — the pupil, reflecting one's image within it. The term suggests extreme preciousness and careful protection. Torah here (H8451) functions as parental instruction, not merely Mosaic law, but the full wisdom tradition.

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix
verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Bind them on your fingers; write them on the tablet of your heart.
Say to wisdom, "You are my sister," and call insight your intimate friend,
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Calling Wisdom "sister" (achot, H269) and "intimate friend" (moda, H4129) frames the relationship with wisdom as covenantal kinship — a counterpoint to the seductive woman who also speaks of intimate union. The personification of Wisdom here anticipates Proverbs 8 and its christological resonances (John 1:1-14; Col 2:3).

words.m: preposition + verb qal infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: to keep you from the forbidden woman, from the adulteress with her smooth words.

words.g: in up to or over against

verses.flow: For at the window of my house I looked out through my lattice,

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: and I saw among the simple ones, I discerned among the youths, a young man lacking heart (lev),
verses.note: HEBREW: "Lacking heart (lev)" renders chasar-lev (H2638/H3820), literally "void of heart." In Hebrew anthropology the heart is the seat of intellect, will, and moral discernment, not merely emotion. The youth's deficiency is fundamentally one of wisdom and moral understanding, not feeling.

verses.flow: passing through the street near her corner, and he went the way to her house,

words.g: in/with the little man of the eye

verses.flow: in the twilight, in the evening of the day, in the middle of the night and in the darkness.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The progression of darkness — twilight, evening, midnight, blackest darkness — is morally loaded. The Hebrew idiom "in the apple of the eye of night" ('ishon laylah, H380/H3915) for deepest darkness inverts the precious "apple of the eye" of v.2, suggesting that folly is the corruption of what should be most treasured.

words.m: preposition + verb qal infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: And behold, a woman comes to meet him, dressed as a prostitute and cunning of heart.
She is loud and wayward; her feet do not abide in her house.
Now in the street, now in the squares, and at every corner she lies in wait.

verses.flow: And she seizes him and kisses him, and with a brazen face she says to him:
"Sacrifices of peace offerings (shelem) were upon me; today I have paid my vows.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The woman's reference to peace offerings (shelem, H8002) is significant: after fulfilling a vow, the offerer would receive back portions of the sacrificial animal to be eaten within a specified time. The meat had to be consumed quickly or burned, creating social pressure to invite guests — here used as a seductive pretext. This reflects precise knowledge of Levitical law (Lev 7:15-18).

words.m: preposition + verb qal infinitive construct + suffix
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: So I have come out to meet you, to seek your face earnestly, and I have found you.
I have spread my couch with coverings, with colored linens of Egypt.
I have sprinkled my bed with myrrh, aloes, and cinnamon.

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Come, let us drink deeply of love (dodim) until the morning; let us delight ourselves with love.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Love" here is dodim (H1730), plural of dod, meaning physical love or lovemaking. The same term appears in Song of Solomon (1:2, 4; 4:10; 7:12) where its use is sanctioned within covenant. Here it is illicit appropriation of what belongs within the marriage bond, a counterfeit of true eros.

verses.flow: For the man is not in his house; he has gone on a long journey.
He took the bag of silver in his hand; at the full moon he will come home."

words.m: verb hiphil perfect 3rd fem sing + suffix
verb hiphil imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: With her much speech she leads him astray; with the smoothness of her lips she compels him.
He goes after her immediately, as an ox goes to the slaughter, and as a stag steps into a noose,
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The ox going to slaughter and the stag to the noose evoke sacrificial and hunting imagery respectively. The man who abandons wisdom becomes prey, the reversal of the human calling to exercise dominion (Gen 1:28). Death as the consequence of folly mirrors the Eden narrative, where the serpent's smooth words led to mortality (Gen 3:1-6; Rom 5:12).

verses.flow: till an arrow pierces his liver; as a bird rushes into a snare, and he does not know that it will cost him his life.

verses.flow: And now, O sons, listen to me, and be attentive to the words of my mouth.
Let not your heart turn aside to her ways; do not go astray in her paths.
For many are the wounded she has brought down, and all her slain are a mighty host.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Mighty host" renders 'atsumim (H6099), the same word used of powerful armies and nations. The forbidden woman is thus portrayed as a military commander whose casualties constitute a battlefield — an inversion of holy warfare. Where the wise fight for life, folly wages war for death.

words.g: Hades or the world of the dead

verses.flow: Her house is the way to Sheol, going down to the chambers of death.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Sheol (H7585) in Hebraic cosmology is the realm of the dead beneath the earth, conceived as the anti-creation space opposed to YHWH's life-giving order. "Chambers of death" (chadrei mavet, H2315/H4194) parallels ancient Near Eastern underworld imagery found in Ugaritic texts (the house of Mot). The forbidden woman's house is thus mapped onto cosmic geography as an entrance to the anti-divine realm, inverting the house of Wisdom (Prov 9:1). (Rev 1:18; Matt 16:18)

verses.flow: Does not Wisdom call out, and Understanding lift up her voice?
verses.note: HEBREW: The Hebrew verb qara' (to call out, Strong's H7121) is the same root used for the summoning of divine assembly members. Wisdom's call here is universal and public — a royal proclamation, not a private invitation. The pairing of "Wisdom" (chokmah, H2451) and "Understanding" (binah, H998) appears frequently in wisdom literature as complementary divine attributes.

verses.flow: On the top of the heights along the way, at the crossroads she takes her stand;
Beside the gates, at the entrance of the city, at the coming in of the doors, she cries aloud:
verses.note: HISTORICAL: City gates in the ancient Near East (ANE) were the primary sites of commerce, legal judgment, and public discourse. Wisdom's positioning at the gates places her at the very center of civic and judicial life, suggesting her authority over all human governance and decision-making. Compare Ruth 4:1-12 and Amos 5:15.

verses.flow: "To you, O men, I call, and my voice is to the sons of man (adam).
O simple ones, learn prudence (ormah); O fools, take to heart understanding.
Hear, for I will speak noble things, and the opening of my lips will be upright things.
For my mouth will utter truth, and wickedness is an abomination to my lips.
All the words of my mouth are in righteousness; there is nothing twisted or crooked in them.
They are all straightforward to him who understands, and right to those who find knowledge.
Take my instruction and not silver, and knowledge rather than choice gold.
For Wisdom is better than corals (peninim), and all that can be desired cannot compare with her.
verses.note: HEBREW: Peninim (H6443) is likely a reference to rubies, corals, or precious pearls — the exact identity is debated, but the term consistently denotes the most prized jewelry in the Hebrew world. The comparison elevates Wisdom above any earthly treasure. Compare Proverbs 3:15 and Job 28:18.

verses.flow: I, Wisdom, dwell with prudence (ormah), and I find out knowledge and discretion.
The fear (yirah) of YHWH is hatred of evil; pride and arrogance and the way of evil and perverted speech I hate.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The fear of YHWH (yirat YHWH, H3374) is not mere terror but a covenantal orientation of reverent submission and moral allegiance to YHWH. Its equation here with the hatred of evil reveals a deeply ethical dimension: to fear YHWH is necessarily to oppose what he opposes. This verse also connects Wisdom directly to YHWH's own moral character.

verses.flow: Counsel and sound wisdom are mine; I am understanding; strength is mine.
By me kings reign, and rulers decree what is just.
By me princes rule, and nobles — all who judge rightly.

verses.flow: I love those who love me, and those who seek me diligently will find me.
Riches and honor are with me, enduring wealth and righteousness.
My fruit is better than gold, even fine gold, and my yield than choice silver.
I walk in the way of righteousness, in the midst of the paths of justice,

verses.flow: YHWH possessed (qanah) me at the beginning of his work, the first of his acts of old.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The verb qanah (H7069) is disputed: it may mean "possessed," "created," or "acquired." The LXX renders it ektisen (created), which influenced early Christological debates. In the context of Proverbs, Wisdom is presented as preexistent and intimately associated with YHWH's creative work. TYPOLOGY: This verse became a major locus in the Arian controversy. Orthodox theologians from Athanasius onward argued that "Wisdom" here is a literary personification anticipating the Logos of John 1:1-3, not a statement that the eternal Son had a temporal origin. The NT identifies Christ as "the wisdom of God" (1 Cor 1:24, 30; Col 2:3), making this passage a profound messianic type.

verses.flow: From everlasting I was set up (nassak), from the beginning, before the earth existed.
verses.note: HEBREW: Nassak (H5258) means "to be woven" or "to be set/installed" — it is used of the anointing/installation of a king (cf. Ps 2:6, where YHWH installs his Anointed). The use of this term for Wisdom's pre-creation establishment carries regal and priestly overtones. TYPOLOGY: Psalm 2:6 uses the cognate root for the installation of the Messiah: "I have set (nassak) my King on Zion." The lexical connection implies that Wisdom's preexistent installation foreshadows the Anointed One's eternal appointment as King.

verses.flow: When there were no depths (tehomot) I was brought forth, when there were no springs abounding with water.
verses.note: HEBREW: Tehomot (H8415, plural of tehom) refers to the primordial deep waters, the same word used in Genesis 1:2 ("the deep"). The term has ANE cognates in the Babylonian Tiamat (the chaos dragon), though in the Hebrew Bible tehom is desacralized — it is creation, not a deity. Wisdom's existence before the tehomot asserts her priority over all created matter.

words.g: a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: Before the mountains had been shaped, before the hills, I was brought forth —
before he had made the earth and its open spaces, and the first of the dust of the world.

verses.flow: When he established the heavens, I was there; when he drew a circle upon the face of the deep (tehom),
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "circle upon the face of the deep" likely refers to the cosmic horizon, reflecting an ANE cosmological conception of the earth as a disc surrounded by water. Similar imagery appears in Isaiah 40:22 ("he who sits above the circle of the earth"). This verse highlights Wisdom's witness to the act of cosmic ordering — she was present as YHWH imposed structure on chaos.

verses.flow: then I was beside him, like a master craftsman (amon); and I was daily his delight, rejoicing before him at all times,
verses.note: HEBREW: The word amon (H0542) is one of the most debated terms in Proverbs. It may mean "craftsman" or "architect" (cf. Jer 52:15), "ward/nursling" (beloved child), or possibly "faithful one." The LXX renders it harmozousa (one who fits together/harmonizes). The sense of "master craftsman" or "artisan" best fits the context of creation and aligns with John 1:3 and Colossians 1:16-17, where the Logos is the agent of all creation. TYPOLOGY: Wisdom as the amon (master craftsman) present at creation is the closest OT anticipation of the Johannine Logos Christology (John 1:1-3). The early church fathers consistently read this verse as a veiled revelation of the Son's eternal co-creative role. (Col 1:15-17, Heb 1:2)

verses.flow: rejoicing in his inhabited world, and my delight was with the sons of man (adam).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Wisdom's delight in the "sons of man" (bene adam) reveals the relational and redemptive heart of divine wisdom. YHWH's creative work is not impersonal or mechanical — it is oriented toward love for humanity. This verse forms an important bridge to the NT teaching that the Son became flesh (John 1:14) precisely because divine Wisdom delights in the human race.

verses.flow: "And now, O sons, listen to me: blessed are those who keep my ways.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc plur

verses.flow: Hear instruction and be wise, and do not neglect it.
Blessed is the man who listens to me, watching daily at my gates, waiting at my doorposts.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: For whoever finds me finds life (chayyim), and obtains favor (ratzon) from YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The one who "finds" Wisdom finds life (chayyim, H2416) — the same word used for the living God and the tree of life in Genesis. To embrace Wisdom is to be restored to the life forfeited in the Fall. Favor (ratzon, H7522) from YHWH is covenantal goodwill, the opposite of divine wrath. TYPOLOGY: Jesus identifies himself as the one through whom life is found: "I am the way, the truth, and the life" (John 14:6). Wisdom's promise here ("whoever finds me finds life") is fulfilled in the incarnate Wisdom of God.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal participle active + suffix

verses.flow: But he who sins against me wrongs his own soul (nephesh); all who hate me love death."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The stark inversion — hating Wisdom is loving death — frames the entire chapter as a cosmic binary: alignment with YHWH's Wisdom is alignment with life, and rejection of her is self-destruction. The word nephesh (H5315, "soul/self") underscores that the harm of rejecting Wisdom is not merely external but ontological — it damages the very essence of the person. This echoes the death-threat of Genesis 2:17 and the two-ways theology of Deuteronomy 30:15-20.

verses.flow: Wisdom has built her house; she has hewn out her seven pillars.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Hewn out her seven pillars" — the verb חָצְבָה (chatsavah, H2672) means to cut or hew from stone, implying permanent, costly construction. The seven pillars (עַמּוּדִים, ammudim) are debated: some see a cosmic temple image, others a literal house of banquet. The number seven signals completeness and divine perfection throughout the Hebrew Bible. TYPOLOGY: Personified Wisdom building her house is a profound type of Christ, the Logos and eternal Wisdom of God, who "tabernacled among us" (John 1:14) and through whom all things were made (1 Cor 1:24, Col 1:16).

verses.flow: She has slaughtered her slaughter; she has mixed her wine; she has also set in order her table.
verses.note: HEBREW: "She has slaughtered her slaughter" (טָבְחָה טִבְחָהּ, tavchah tivchah) is a cognate accusative construction emphasizing the lavishness and completeness of the feast prepared. Mixing wine (מָסְכָה יֵינָהּ) involved blending wine with spices or water, producing a refined drink for formal feasting. TYPOLOGY: The feast of Wisdom anticipates the Messianic banquet (Isa 25:6; Matt 22:1-14; Rev 19:9), where Christ spreads the table of salvation for all who will come.

verses.flow: She has sent out her servant-girls; she calls from the highest places of the city:
"Whoever is simple (peti), let him turn aside here!" To him who lacks heart (lev) she says,
verses.note: HEBREW: "Simple" (פֶּתִי, peti, H6612) refers to one who is open, naive, or easily swayed — not yet committed to either wisdom or folly. "Lacks heart" (חֲסַר-לֵב, chasar-lev) literally means deficient in the organ of thought and will; in Hebrew anthropology the heart (לֵב, lev) is the seat of reason, intention, and moral direction. This invitation targets the morally undecided, those still at a crossroads.

verses.flow: "Come, eat of my bread, and drink of the wine I have mixed.

verses.flow: Leave behind simpleness (peti), and live; and walk in the way of understanding (binah)."
He who corrects a scoffer takes to himself dishonor; and he who reproves a wicked man — it is his blemish.

verses.flow: Do not reprove a scoffer, lest he hate you; reprove a wise man, and he will love you.

verses.flow: Give to a wise man, and he will be yet wiser; instruct a righteous man, and he will increase in learning.
The fear of YHWH is the beginning (reshit) of wisdom (chokmah), and knowledge of the Holy One is understanding (binah).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The fear of YHWH is the beginning (רֵאשִׁית, reshit) of wisdom" — reshit can mean "beginning" in both temporal and positional senses (cf. Gen 1:1). True wisdom does not start from neutral human reason but from a covenantal, reverent relationship with YHWH. This verse is the thematic climax of the entire first section of Proverbs (chapters 1-9). HEBREW: "Knowledge of the Holy One" (קְדֹשִׁים, qedoshim) uses a Hebrew plural of majesty or intensity — "the Holy Ones" — likely referring to YHWH in His transcendent holiness, though some see a divine council echo (Ps 89:5-7). The pairing of "wisdom" (chokmah, H2451) and "understanding" (binah, H998) as twin gifts rooted in YHWH-relationship is programmatic for all of Proverbs.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: For by me your days will be multiplied, and the years of your life will be added to you.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: If you are wise, you are wise for yourself; and if you scoff, you alone will bear it.
The woman of Folly is boisterous; she is simple (peti), and knows nothing.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Woman of Folly" (אֵשֶׁת כְּסִילוּת, eshet kesîlut) — kesîlut (H3687) denotes a moral and intellectual dullness, a stubbornness of heart that rejects instruction. She is personified as the dark counterpart to Lady Wisdom, a structuring device the author uses deliberately throughout chapters 1-9. THEOLOGICAL: The two women — Wisdom and Folly — represent the two ultimate paths set before every human being, the way of life in covenant with YHWH and the way of death in rebellion against Him (Deut 30:15-19).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: And she sits at the door of her house, upon a seat at the highest places of the city,

verses.flow: "Whoever is simple (peti), let him turn aside here!" And to him who lacks heart (lev) she says,
"Stolen waters are sweet, and bread eaten in secret is pleasant."

words.g: Hades or the world of the dead

verses.flow: But he does not know that the dead (rephaim) are there, that her guests are in the depths of Sheol.
verses.note: HEBREW: "The dead" (רְפָאִים, rephaim, H7496) can refer to the shades of the dead in Sheol, the underworld realm of the deceased in ancient Israelite cosmology. Sheol (שְׁאוֹל, H7585) is the place of the dead, a dim subterranean realm in Hebrew thought. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The rephaim appear in Ugaritic texts as a class of deified or semi-divine dead warriors, the ancient shadowy inhabitants of the underworld; in biblical usage the term is demythologized to describe the powerless shades of deceased humans (Isa 14:9; Ps 88:10). TYPOLOGY: Folly's house leading to Sheol contrasts sharply with Wisdom's house as a place of life — a contrast resolved eschatologically in Christ, who holds "the keys of Death and of Hades" (Rev 1:18) and abolishes the power of the grave for those united to Him.

verses.flow: The proverbs of Solomon. A wise son makes a father glad, but a foolish son is a grief to his mother.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Proverbs 10:1 opens the second major section of the book, shifting from the extended discourses of chapters 1-9 to the collection of individual antithetical couplets (Hebrew mashal, pl. meshalim). The superscription "proverbs of Solomon" (mishle Shelomoh) mirrors 1:1, demarcating a new literary unit. TYPOLOGY: Solomon as the archetypal wise son points forward to the greater Son of David, Jesus the Messiah, who is called the wisdom of God (1 Cor 1:24).

verses.flow: Treasures of wickedness profit nothing, but righteousness (tsedaqah) delivers from death.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Tsedaqah" (H6666) denotes righteousness as conformity to a covenant standard; here it functions as a rescuing agent. The verb "delivers" (natsal, H5337) implies being snatched or drawn out from danger. THEOLOGICAL: Righteousness delivering from death anticipates the NT teaching that the righteous live by faith (Hab 2:4; Rom 1:17), and ultimately points to Christ's righteousness imputed to believers.

verses.flow: YHWH does not let the soul (nephesh) of the righteous go hungry, but the craving of the wicked He thrusts away.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's active provision for the nephesh (soul/life-breath) of the righteous reflects covenant faithfulness (hesed). The verb "thrust away" (hadaph, H1920) is forceful — YHWH actively repels the cravings of the wicked. TYPOLOGY: The contrast between hunger satisfied and craving repelled foreshadows the Beatitudes: "Blessed are those who hunger and thirst for righteousness, for they shall be satisfied" (Matt 5:6).

verses.flow: A slack hand makes poor, but the hand of the diligent makes rich.
He who gathers in summer is a prudent son, but he who sleeps in harvest is a son who brings shame.
Blessings are upon the head of the righteous, but the mouth of the wicked conceals violence.
The memory of the righteous is a blessing, but the name of the wicked will rot.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Memory" (zeker, H2143) carries the sense of one's name being invoked in blessing by future generations. "Will rot" (yirqav, from raqav H7537) is a visceral image of organic decay applied to reputation. HISTORICAL: In ANE culture, the perpetuation of one's name after death was of supreme importance; to have one's name "rot" was the ultimate social death. THEOLOGICAL: Contrast with Ps 112:6 — the righteous will be remembered forever.

verses.flow: The wise of heart receives commandments, but a babbling fool will be thrown down.
He who walks in integrity walks securely, but he who makes his ways crooked will be found out.
He who winks the eye causes pain, and the babbling fool will be thrown down.
The mouth of the righteous is a fountain of life (chayyim), but the mouth of the wicked conceals violence.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Fountain of life" (meqor chayyim, H4726 + H2416) is a significant wisdom metaphor in Proverbs, also appearing in 13:14; 14:27; 16:22. The image evokes a spring of fresh, flowing water as opposed to a stagnant or poisoned source. TYPOLOGY: Jesus appropriates this imagery directly in John 4:14 and 7:38, declaring Himself the true source of living water, the fulfillment of wisdom's promise.

verses.flow: Hatred stirs up strife (madon), but love (ahavah) covers all transgressions.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Covers" (kasah, H3680) implies a deliberate act of concealment — love does not merely overlook transgression but actively covers it. This is quoted in 1 Pet 4:8 (Greek: kalyptei). THEOLOGICAL: The proverb undergirds the NT theology of forgiveness as a covering (Ps 32:1; Rom 4:7), pointing ultimately to the atoning work of Christ whose blood covers transgression.

verses.flow: On the lips of him who has understanding wisdom is found, but a rod is for the back of him who lacks heart.
The wise store up knowledge, but the mouth of the fool is imminent ruin.
The wealth of a rich man is his strong city; the poverty of the poor is their ruin.
The wages of the righteous lead to life, the gain of the wicked to sin.
He who keeps discipline is on the path of life, but he who forsakes reproof leads others astray.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Discipline" (musar, H4148) is the same term used throughout chapters 1-9 for the father's instruction; it encompasses correction, warning, and moral formation. "Leads others astray" (mat'eh, from ta'ah H8582) suggests the one who rejects reproof does not merely err himself but becomes a cause of wandering for others. TYPOLOGY: The path of life motif connects to Jesus as the Way (John 14:6).

verses.flow: He who conceals hatred has lying lips, and he who utters slander is a fool.
In the abundance of words transgression is not absent, but he who restrains his lips is prudent.
The tongue of the righteous is choice silver; the heart of the wicked is of little worth.
The lips of the righteous shepherd many, but fools die for lack of heart.
The blessing of YHWH — it makes rich, and He adds no sorrow with it.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The blessing of YHWH is here presented as the sole sufficient cause of wealth — human effort is implicitly subordinated. "He adds no sorrow with it" (lo-yosif etsev immah) is a pointed contrast with the toil-cursed labor of the Fall (Gen 3:17). THEOLOGICAL: The verse affirms divine sovereignty in material provision and echoes the Deuteronomic blessing theology (Deut 28:2-8).

verses.flow: Doing wickedness is like sport to a fool, but wisdom is pleasure to a man of understanding.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: What the wicked fears will come upon him, but the desire of the righteous will be granted.
When the storm wind passes, the wicked is no more, but the righteous is an everlasting foundation.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Storm wind" (suphah, H5492) denotes a whirlwind or tempest — an image of sudden, catastrophic judgment. "Everlasting foundation" (yesod olam) uses yesod (H3245), the word for a founding base or cornerstone. TYPOLOGY: The image of the righteous as an unshakeable foundation is echoed in the parable of the wise and foolish builders (Matt 7:24-27) and in Christ as the true cornerstone (1 Pet 2:6; Isa 28:16).

words.m: preposition + verb qal participle active + suffix

verses.flow: Like vinegar to the teeth and like smoke to the eyes, so is the sluggard to those who send him.
The fear of YHWH (yir'at YHWH) adds days, but the years of the wicked will be cut short.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Yir'at YHWH" (fear/reverence of YHWH, H3374 + H3068) is the foundational theme of the entire book (cf. 1:7; 9:10). "Adds days" (tosif yamim) reflects the covenantal promise of long life tied to obedience (Deut 5:33). THEOLOGICAL: The shortening of the wicked's years is not merely a moral observation but reflects YHWH's judicial sovereignty over human life and death.

verses.flow: The hope of the righteous is gladness, but the expectation of the wicked will perish.
The way of YHWH is a stronghold to the blameless, but ruin to those who do evil.
The righteous will never be moved, but the wicked will not dwell in the land.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Will not dwell in the land" (lo-yishkon erets) evokes the covenantal land promise — inhabiting the land is a sign of covenant blessing; expulsion is covenant curse (Deut 28:63; Ps 37:9, 11). TYPOLOGY: The promise that the righteous shall inherit the earth (Ps 37:11) is directly quoted by Jesus in the Beatitudes (Matt 5:5), reframing the land promise in eschatological and cosmic terms.

verses.flow: The mouth of the righteous brings forth wisdom (chokmah), but the perverse tongue will be cut off.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Brings forth" (yaniv, from nub H5107) means to bear fruit or produce abundantly, as a tree yields its produce. "Perverse tongue" (tahpukot, H8419) denotes that which is turned upside down — moral inversion. THEOLOGICAL: The righteous mouth as fruit-bearing organ connects to the NT image of good trees bearing good fruit (Matt 7:17-18) and to Christ as the true Vine (John 15:5).

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: The lips of the righteous know what is acceptable, but the mouth of the wicked speaks perversities.
A false balance is an abomination (toevah) to YHWH, but a full weight is his delight.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Toevah" (Strong's H8441) — "abomination," a term denoting that which is ritually or morally repugnant to YHWH. A "false balance" (moznei mirmah, lit. "balances of deceit") evokes Levitical standards of honest commerce (Lev 19:35-36). The pairing of commercial ethics with divine response reflects the OT's integration of worship and marketplace conduct. THEOLOGICAL: Dishonest commerce is placed in the same category as idolatry in Deuteronomy; YHWH's "delight" (ratson, H7522) in honest weights mirrors his pleasure in righteous sacrifice.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: When pride comes, then comes shame, but with the humble is wisdom.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix
verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: The integrity (tummah) of the upright guides them, but the crookedness of the treacherous destroys them.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Tummah" (H8538) — integrity, wholeness, completeness; from the root tamam, meaning to be complete or finished. The same root yields "tam" (blameless) used of Job (Job 1:1). THEOLOGICAL: Moral integrity is here portrayed as a navigational guide; the "crookedness" (selef, H5558) of the treacherous is their own undoing, a recurring wisdom motif that sin carries inherent self-destructive consequences.

verses.flow: Riches do not profit in the day of wrath, but righteousness (tsedaqah) delivers from death.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The day of wrath" (yom evrah) anticipates the eschatological day of divine judgment, a theme developed extensively in the Prophets (Zeph 1:15, 18; Isa 13:13). Wealth cannot serve as an atonement or ransom on that day. TYPOLOGY: "Righteousness delivers from death" — this principle finds its ultimate fulfillment in the imputed righteousness of Messiah, through whom the righteous are delivered from the second death (Rom 5:9; Rev 20:6).

verses.flow: The righteousness of the blameless makes straight his way, but the wicked falls by his own wickedness.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: The righteousness of the upright delivers them, but the treacherous are taken captive by their own desire.
When the wicked man dies, his hope perishes, and the expectation of strength perishes.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: The righteous is delivered from trouble, and the wicked enters in his place.
With his mouth the godless man destroys his neighbor, but through knowledge the righteous are delivered.

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb qal infinitive construct

verses.flow: When the righteous prosper, the city rejoices, and when the wicked perish there is jubilation.

words.g: to pull down or in pieces

verses.flow: By the blessing of the upright a city is exalted, but by the mouth of the wicked it is overthrown.
Whoever belittles his neighbor lacks sense, but a man of understanding keeps silent.
Whoever goes about as a slanderer (rakil) reveals secrets, but one who is faithful of spirit keeps a matter covered.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Rakil" (H7400) — slanderer, talebearer, one who goes about spreading reports; possibly related to the root ragal (to go about on foot). The same term appears in Lev 19:16: "You shall not go about as a slanderer among your people." THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between the gossip who reveals secrets and the faithful spirit who covers them anticipates the NT virtue of discretion and love that "covers a multitude of sins" (1 Pet 4:8; Prov 10:12).

verses.flow: Where there is no guidance, a people falls, but in an abundance of counselors there is safety (teshuah).
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The concept of a council of advisors was standard in ANE royal courts; the king's cabinet of counselors (yo'atsim) was considered essential for military and political stability. THEOLOGICAL: "Teshuah" (H8668) — deliverance, safety, victory; the same word used for divine salvation in battle contexts. The wisdom ideal projects this royal advisory model onto civic and personal life, echoing the divine council pattern wherein even YHWH deliberates among his assembly (1 Kgs 22:19-22). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The image of plural counselors yielding "teshuah" resonates with the heavenly council where YHWH's decrees are established through deliberation (Ps 82:1; Job 1:6).

verses.flow: Whoever puts up security for a stranger will surely suffer for it, but whoever hates striking hands in pledge is secure.
A gracious woman obtains honor, and violent men obtain riches.
A man of kindness (hesed) benefits his own soul, but a cruel man harms his own flesh.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Hesed" (H2617) — lovingkindness, steadfast covenant-love, mercy; one of the most theologically rich terms in the Hebrew Bible, denoting faithful loyalty within a relational bond. Here its first parenthetical use marks its significance. THEOLOGICAL: The reciprocity principle — that kindness benefits one's own soul — reflects the OT wisdom conviction that the moral order is woven into creation; virtue returns to the virtuous, and cruelty harms the cruel (Gal 6:7-8).

verses.flow: The wicked earns deceptive wages, but one who sows righteousness — a true reward.
Truly righteous leads to life, but whoever pursues evil — to his own death.
Those of crooked heart are an abomination to YHWH, but those blameless in their way are his delight.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The verse forms a precise antithetical couplet contrasting YHWH's moral response to inner disposition — "crooked heart" versus "blameless way." "Abomination to YHWH" and "his delight" (ratson, H7522) are the strongest possible poles of divine evaluation. TYPOLOGY: Messiah alone is perfectly "blameless in the way" (tamim derek); his righteousness is the basis upon which believers become YHWH's delight (2 Cor 5:21).

verses.flow: Be assured, the evil man will not go unpunished, but the offspring of the righteous will be delivered.
Like a gold ring in the snout of a pig is a beautiful woman who turns aside from discretion.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Gold nose-rings were common adornments in the ANE, particularly for women of status and bridal gifts (cf. Gen 24:47, Rebekah's nose ring). The image is deliberately grotesque — placing a costly ornament on an unclean animal renders it absurd. THEOLOGICAL: The proverb targets the disconnect between external beauty and inner moral character (discretion, ta'am — H2940, taste, discernment). True beauty in the OT wisdom tradition is inseparable from moral wisdom (Prov 31:30).

words.g: in/with good in the widest sense

verses.flow: The desire of the righteous is only good, but the expectation of the wicked is wrath.
There is one who scatters and yet increases still, and there is one who withholds more than is right, only to come to want.
A soul (nephesh) of blessing will be enriched, and whoever waters others will himself be watered.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Nephesh" (H5315) — soul, living being, person, life-breath; one of the most theologically laden anthropological terms in Hebrew. Its use here ("soul of blessing") personifies the generous person as embodying blessing itself. THEOLOGICAL: The principle of reciprocal generosity — "whoever waters others will himself be watered" — reflects a creation-order conviction that aligns with NT teaching on sowing and reaping (2 Cor 9:6-8; Luke 6:38).

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: Whoever withholds grain, the people will curse him, but blessing be upon the head of him who sells it.

words.g: in/with good in the widest sense

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: Whoever diligently seeks good seeks favor, but whoever searches for evil — it will come upon him.

words.g: to break forth as a bud

verses.flow: Whoever trusts in his riches will fall, but like green foliage the righteous will flourish.
Whoever troubles his own household will inherit the wind, and the fool will be servant to the wise of heart.
The fruit of the righteous is a tree of life, and whoever captures souls (naphshot) is wise.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Tree of life" (ets hayyim) — a direct allusion to the primordial tree in the Garden of Eden (Gen 2:9; 3:22). Its reappearance in Proverbs (also 3:18; 13:12; 15:4) signals that wisdom and righteousness restore access to what was lost in the Fall. TYPOLOGY: The "tree of life" reappears in Rev 22:2 as the eschatological restoration of Eden, pointing to Messiah as the one who restores life forfeited by Adam. THEOLOGICAL: "Whoever captures souls (naphshot) is wise" — the fruit of righteousness extends to others; the righteous person draws others into life, anticipating the NT missionary vocation.

verses.flow: Behold, the righteous is repaid on the earth — how much more the wicked and the sinner!
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The argument runs from lesser to greater (qal va'homer): if the righteous themselves experience recompense on earth, how inescapably more certain is the judgment upon the wicked. TYPOLOGY: Peter quotes a close variant (LXX of Prov 11:31) in 1 Pet 4:18 — "If the righteous is scarcely saved, what will become of the ungodly and the sinner?" — applying this wisdom axiom to the eschatological judgment inaugurated by Messiah's first coming and perfected at his return.

verses.flow: Whoever loves discipline loves knowledge, but he who hates reproof is brutish (ba'ar).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Brutish" translates ba'ar (H1198), meaning animal-like or stupid, derived from the root meaning to be consumed or to be like cattle.
