The contrast is stark: disciplined pursuit of knowledge is the mark of genuine humanity, while rejection of correction degrades one to a sub-rational level. This sets the epistemological foundation for the chapter.

verses.flow: A good man obtains favor (ratson) from YHWH, but a man of wicked schemes He condemns.
A man is not established by wickedness, but the root of the righteous will not be moved.
A wife of valor (eshet chayil) is the crown of her husband, but she who brings shame is like rottenness in his bones.
verses.note: HEBREW: Eshet chayil (H2428) — literally "woman of strength/valor/excellence," the same phrase used of the ideal wife in Proverbs 31:10. Chayil carries military, economic, and moral dimensions simultaneously. TYPOLOGY: The crown imagery anticipates the honor given to wisdom personified in Proverbs 8 and finds NT resonance in the Bride of Christ who brings glory rather than shame (Eph 5:27; Rev 19:7-8).

verses.flow: The thoughts (machshevot) of the righteous are just, but the counsels of the wicked are deceit.

words.g: blood of man or an animal

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: The words of the wicked are a lying-in-wait for blood, but the mouth of the upright delivers them.
The wicked are overthrown and are no more, but the house of the righteous will stand.
A man is praised according to his insight (sekhel), but one who is twisted of heart is despised.
Better is one who is lightly esteemed and has a servant than one who honors himself and lacks bread.
A righteous man knows the life of his animal, but the mercies (rachamim) of the wicked are cruel.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The righteous man's concern for his animal reflects the creational mandate of stewardship (Gen 1:28; 2:15) and the broader covenantal ethic that spans human and animal welfare (Deut 25:4). That even the "mercies" (rachamim, H7356 — lit. womb-compassion) of the wicked are cruel reveals that wickedness corrupts even the capacity for genuine compassion. TYPOLOGY: The Good Shepherd image (John 10:11) is rooted in this wisdom tradition.

verses.flow: Whoever works his land will be satisfied with bread, but whoever pursues empty things lacks understanding (lev).
The wicked desires the net of evil men, but the root of the righteous yields fruit.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: In the transgression of the lips is a snare to the evil man, but the righteous comes out from distress.

words.g: in/with good in the widest sense

verses.flow: From the fruit of the mouth of a man he is satisfied with good, and the recompense of a man's hands is returned to him.
The way of a fool is right in his own eyes, but a wise man listens to counsel (etsah).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The self-referential certainty of the fool is a key theme in Proverbs. The phrase "right in his own eyes" echoes the refrain of Judges ("every man did what was right in his own eyes," Judg 21:25), linking moral autonomy to social chaos. True wisdom is always communal and correctable.

verses.flow: The vexation (ka'as) of a fool is known at once, but a prudent man covers dishonor.
A truthful witness breathes out righteousness, but a false witness breathes out deceit.
There is one who speaks like the piercings of a sword, but the tongue of the wise brings healing (marpe).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Piercings" (mivtach, from batach) — the word used here, midqarot (H4094), literally means stabbings or thrusts, from the root daqa meaning to pierce. "Healing" is marpe (H4832), a term also translated "medicine" or "cure," related to the verb rapha (to heal). TYPOLOGY: The tongue as a weapon or instrument of healing anticipates James 3:1-12 and Christ as the living Word whose speech either wounds or restores (Isa 53:5; Rev 1:16).

verses.flow: A lip of truth is established forever, but a tongue of falsehood is only for a moment.

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb qal participle active

verses.flow: Deceit is in the heart of those who devise evil, but those who counsel peace (shalom) have joy.
verses.note: HEBREW: Shalom (H7965) carries its full freight here — not merely absence of conflict but complete well-being, wholeness, and covenantal flourishing. THEOLOGICAL: Those who "counsel shalom" are aligned with the divine purpose; YHWH is described in Isaiah 9:6 as the "Prince of Shalom," and peacemakers are called children of God (Matt 5:9).

words.g: to fill or be full of

verses.flow: No harm (aven) befalls the righteous, but the wicked are filled with calamity.
Lips of falsehood are an abomination (to'evah) to YHWH, but those who deal faithfully are His delight.
verses.note: HEBREW: To'evah (H8441) — "abomination" — is a strong cultic term, used repeatedly in Deuteronomy for things utterly incompatible with YHWH's holy character. Its use here for false lips elevates ethical speech to a matter of worship and covenant loyalty. THEOLOGICAL: This verse creates a direct parallel between false worship and false speech, grounding the ethics of honesty in the very nature of YHWH as the God of truth (Deut 32:4).

verses.flow: A prudent man conceals knowledge, but the heart of fools proclaims folly.
The hand of the diligent will rule, but the slack hand will be put to forced labor (mas).
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Mas (H4522) refers to corvee — forced or tributary labor imposed by a conquering power or ruling class on subjugated peoples (cf. 1 Kgs 5:13). The contrast between ruling and serving under compulsion maps onto the Wisdom tradition's warning that sloth leads to social diminishment. TYPOLOGY: Diligent labor as a form of dominion connects to the creation mandate and the parable of the talents (Matt 25:14-30).

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
verb piel imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Anxiety (de'agah) in the heart of a man weighs it down, but a good word makes it glad.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: A righteous man searches out his neighbor, but the way of the wicked leads them astray.

words.g: to braid or catch in anet

verses.flow: The slack man does not roast his prey, but the wealth of a diligent man is precious.
In the path of righteousness is life (chayyim), and in its pathway there is no death.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The climactic verse of the chapter declares that the path of righteousness is the path of life (chayyim, H2416 — abundant, living life). The phrase "no death" (al mavet) is striking and has been interpreted as immortality or at minimum as the fullness of life that avoids premature destruction. TYPOLOGY: Jesus identifies Himself as "the way, the truth, and the life" (John 14:6), directly embodying the righteous path of Proverbs. The connection between righteousness, wisdom, and eternal life is a thread running from Proverbs through the Psalms to the NT (Ps 16:11; John 17:3).

verses.flow: A wise son — the discipline (musar) of a father; but a scoffer does not listen to rebuke.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verse opens with an elliptical construction — the subject "a wise son" hangs without a predicate, implying "heeds" or "accepts." The term musar (Strong's H4148) denotes instruction, correction, and discipline with covenantal weight; it appears throughout Proverbs as the path of formation into wisdom. The contrast with the scoffer (letz, H3887) who refuses rebuke introduces the two-way anthropology foundational to the entire book.

words.g: in/with good in the widest sense

verses.flow: From the fruit of a man's mouth he eats what is good, but the desire of the treacherous is for violence.
He who guards his mouth preserves his life; he who opens wide his lips comes to ruin.
The soul (nephesh) of the sluggard craves and gets nothing, but the soul of the diligent is made fat.
verses.note: HEBREW: Nephesh (Strong's H5315) is here the animating desire of the inner person, not merely "soul" in a Greek sense but the full appetitive self — the craving, longing self. The sluggard's nephesh craves but his hands refuse to act, while the diligent's nephesh is "made fat" (dashen, H1878), an agricultural image of rich abundance. THEOLOGICAL: This verse anticipates Paul's tension in Romans 7 between desire and action, though here the diagnosis is moral rather than forensic.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: A righteous man hates a word of falsehood, but a wicked man acts shamefully and disgracefully.
Righteousness guards the one whose way is blameless, but wickedness overthrows the sinner.
There is one who makes himself rich, yet has nothing; there is one who makes himself poor, yet has great wealth.
The ransom of a man's life is his riches, but the poor man hears no threat.
The light of the righteous rejoices, but the lamp of the wicked will be put out.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The imagery of light and extinguished lamp carries covenantal resonance: YHWH is Israel's light (Ps 27:1), and the lamp of the wicked being quenched echoes the fate of the house of Eli (1 Sam 3:3) and the warnings of Proverbs 20:20 and 24:20. TYPOLOGY: Christ declares himself the Light of the world (John 8:12); those united to him share in undying light (Matt 5:14-16), while the wicked face outer darkness (Matt 25:30).

verses.flow: By insolence comes nothing but strife, but with those who take counsel is wisdom.
Wealth gained hastily will dwindle, but whoever gathers little by little will increase it.
Hope deferred makes the heart sick, but a desire fulfilled is a tree of life.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Tree of life" (etz hayyim, H6086 + H2416) is one of only four uses of this phrase in Proverbs (3:18; 11:30; 13:12; 15:4), each time describing the fruit of wisdom or fulfilled hope. The phrase echoes Eden (Gen 2:9; 3:22) and anticipates its eschatological restoration (Rev 22:2). Hope deferred as sickness of heart speaks to the embodied experience of longing — wisdom is not merely cognitive but deeply affective.

verses.flow: Whoever despises the word brings destruction on himself, but he who fears the commandment will be rewarded.
The teaching (torah) of the wise is a fountain of life, turning one away from the snares of death.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word torah (H8451) here does not refer to the Mosaic Law per se but to the authoritative instruction of a wisdom teacher — yet the word is deliberately the same, linking wisdom teaching to covenantal revelation. "Fountain of life" (meqor hayyim, H4726 + H2416) is a recurring phrase (Ps 36:9; Prov 10:11; 14:27) denoting the life-giving source found in YHWH himself. TYPOLOGY: Christ is the ultimate Wisdom-Teacher whose words are spirit and life (John 6:63; 7:38).

verses.flow: Good insight gains favor, but the way of the treacherous is their ruin.
Every prudent man acts with knowledge, but a fool spreads folly.
A wicked messenger falls into trouble, but a faithful envoy brings healing (shalom).
verses.note: HEBREW: Shalom (H7965) is rendered "healing" here in the sense of wholesomeness and restoration brought by a trustworthy envoy — the full semantic range includes peace, completeness, and welfare. The contrast between the wicked and faithful messenger (malak, the same word used for divine messengers/angels) may carry deliberate overtones of reliability in divine service. THEOLOGICAL: Faithfulness in representation — whether of a king or of YHWH — is itself a form of covenant loyalty (hesed).

verses.flow: Poverty and disgrace come to him who ignores discipline (musar), but whoever heeds reproof is honored.
A desire fulfilled is sweet to the soul, but it is an abomination to fools to turn away from evil.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing

words.g: and + and to tend a flock

verses.flow: Whoever walks with the wise becomes wise, but the companion of fools will suffer harm.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse encapsulates the social and formative dimension of wisdom in Proverbs — wisdom is not merely individual attainment but is acquired through covenant community and deliberate association. The verb "walks with" (yalak, H1980) is the same covenantal walking language used of Enoch (Gen 5:24) and Noah (Gen 6:9). TYPOLOGY: Discipleship in the New Testament is structurally identical — Jesus calls followers to "walk with" him (Matt 4:19; John 10:27).

words.g: in/with good in the widest sense

verses.flow: Disaster pursues sinners, but the righteous are rewarded with good.
A good man leaves an inheritance to his children's children, but the sinner's wealth is laid up for the righteous.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The transfer of the sinner's accumulated wealth to the righteous is not merely proverbial observation but a covenantal principle seen in the Exodus plunder of Egypt (Exod 12:36) and anticipated eschatologically (Isa 60:5; Rev 21:24). The multigenerational inheritance affirms YHWH's covenantal faithfulness across generations, reflecting the Abrahamic promise structure (Gen 17:7).

verses.flow: The fallow ground of the poor yields much food, but it is swept away through injustice.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal participle active + suffix
verb piel perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Whoever spares the rod hates his son, but he who loves him is diligent to discipline him.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "rod" (shevet, H7626) is the shepherd's staff — an instrument of guidance, protection, and correction. ANE parental wisdom literature (Egyptian Instruction texts, Sumerian proverb collections) similarly commends corporal correction as formative. THEOLOGICAL: The verse ties love explicitly to discipline, anticipating Proverbs 3:12 and its New Testament citation in Hebrews 12:6, where YHWH's fatherly discipline is the proof of sonship, not its contradiction.

verses.flow: The righteous has enough to satisfy his appetite (nephesh), but the belly of the wicked suffers want.
verses.note: HEBREW: Nephesh (H5315) appears again, here as "appetite" — the living, craving self that in the righteous finds genuine satisfaction. The contrast with the "belly" (beten, H990) of the wicked suffering want creates a bodily register: the righteous person is whole and satisfied at the level of creaturely need, while the wicked remain perpetually deficient. THEOLOGICAL: This verse functions as a benediction on the chapter — the path of wisdom leads to shalom, the path of wickedness to perpetual lack.

words.g: to pull down or in pieces
words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: The wisest of women builds her house, but folly with her own hands tears it down.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verse opens with a construct form, "chakhmot nashim" (literally "the wisest of women"), using a plural of excellence for wisdom. The contrast between building and tearing down (haras) with one's own hands is emphatic — folly is self-destructive by nature. TYPOLOGY: The wise woman building her house anticipates Wisdom personified in Proverbs 9:1, who also builds her house, a figure the NT connects to Christ as the wisdom of God (1 Cor 1:24).

verses.flow: Whoever walks in uprightness fears YHWH, but one who is devious in his ways despises him.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: In the mouth of a fool is a rod of pride, but the lips of the wise will preserve them.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Shoot" or "rod" (choter, H2415) in the mouth of a fool refers to speech that wounds or acts as an instrument of pride and punishment. Contrast with the lips of the wise that "guard" (shomer) them — the same root used for covenant keeping. THEOLOGICAL: The tongue's power to elevate or destroy is a consistent Proverbs theme, rooted in the idea that speech reflects the condition of the heart (cf. Matt 12:34).

verses.flow: Where there are no oxen, the manger is clean, but abundant produce comes by the strength of the ox.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: A faithful witness does not lie, but a false witness breathes out lies.
A scoffer seeks wisdom and finds none, but knowledge is easy for a man of understanding.
Go from the presence of a foolish man, for you will not find lips of knowledge there.
The wisdom of the prudent is to discern his way, but the folly of fools is deceit.
Fools mock at the guilt offering, but among the upright there is favor (ratson).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Asham" (H817) is the technical term for the guilt offering under Mosaic law (Lev 5–7). Fools mock the very mechanism of atonement — treating sin as trivial. "Ratson" (H7522) means favor, goodwill, or delight, often describing divine acceptance. TYPOLOGY: The guilt offering prefigures Christ's atoning work as the one who bore our asham (Isa 53:10), making his sacrifice the ultimate object of either faith or mockery.

verses.flow: The heart knows the bitterness of its own soul (nephesh), and no stranger shares in its joy.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Nephesh" (H5315) here carries its full range — soul, self, inner life. The verse asserts an irreducible interiority: no outsider (zar, stranger/foreigner) can fully enter another's grief or joy. THEOLOGICAL: This anthropological observation grounds the necessity of a mediator who can truly know human suffering — a theme the NT applies to Christ as the great High Priest who sympathizes with our weaknesses (Heb 4:15).

words.g: to break forth as a bud

verses.flow: The house of the wicked will be destroyed, but the tent of the upright will flourish.
There is a way that seems right to a man, but its end is the ways of death.
verses.note: HEBREW: This proverb (repeated verbatim in 16:25) uses "yashar" (straight, right) — the same root as "Israel's" ethical ideal of uprightness. The phrase "ways of death" (darkei mavet) is plural, implying many roads lead to destruction. THEOLOGICAL: The verse is a foundational warning against self-derived morality and human autonomy as a guide for life, anticipating Christ's declaration that he alone is "the Way" (John 14:6).

verses.flow: Even in laughter the heart may be in pain, and the end of joy may be grief.
The backslider in heart will be filled with the fruit of his ways, and a good man will be satisfied from himself.
The simple believes every word, but the prudent gives thought to his steps.
A wise man fears and turns away from evil, but a fool throws off restraint and is reckless.
A man of quick temper acts foolishly, and a man of evil schemes is hated.
The simple inherit folly, but the prudent are crowned with knowledge.
The evil bow down before the good, and the wicked at the gates of the righteous.
Even by his neighbor a poor man is hated, but the friends of a rich man are many.
Whoever despises his neighbor is a sinner, but blessed is he who is gracious to the humble.

words.g: in/with good in the widest sense

verses.flow: Do they not go astray who devise evil? But lovingkindness (hesed) and faithfulness (emet) belong to those who devise good.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Hesed" (H2617) is covenant lovingkindness, loyalty, and mercy — one of the most theologically rich terms in the Hebrew Bible. Paired here with "emet" (H571, faithfulness/truth), these two terms form the classic covenantal hendiadys found across the Psalms and Pentateuch. THEOLOGICAL: The one who devises good is rewarded with the very character of YHWH himself — hesed and emet are divine attributes that are reflected in the righteous.

verses.flow: In all toil there is profit, but mere talk of the lips leads only to poverty.
The crown of the wise is their wealth, but the folly of fools is folly.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: A truthful witness saves lives, but one who breathes out lies is deceitful.
In the fear of YHWH one has strong confidence, and his children will have a refuge.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Mibtach oz" (strong confidence/secure trust) is the fruit of "yirat YHWH" (fear of YHWH), a central organizing concept of Proverbs (cf. 1:7). The term "mibtach" (H4009) implies a place of refuge or security, not merely a feeling. THEOLOGICAL: The generational dimension — "his children will have a refuge" — shows that covenant faithfulness produces communal blessing, consistent with Deuteronomic theology (Deut 7:9).

verses.flow: The fear of YHWH is a fountain of life, that one may turn away from the snares of death.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Fountain of life" (meqor chayyim) is a powerful metaphor for YHWH's life-giving wisdom and presence (cf. Ps 36:9; Jer 2:13). The "snares of death" (moqeshei mavet) echoes the trap imagery used of spiritual danger throughout Proverbs. TYPOLOGY: Jesus applies "living water" language to himself in John 4:10–14 and 7:38, drawing on this "fountain of life" tradition. THEOLOGICAL: Fear of YHWH is not servile terror but an orientation of the whole person that leads away from death-entanglements.

verses.flow: In a multitude of people is the glory of a king, but without people a prince is ruined.
Whoever is slow to anger has great understanding, but one who is hasty of spirit exalts folly.
A heart of tranquility is life to the body, but envy is rottenness to the bones.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel participle active + suffix

verses.flow: Whoever oppresses a poor man insults his Maker, but whoever is gracious to the needy honors him.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Oppresses" (ashaq, H6231) describes exploitative mistreatment of the vulnerable. The theological grounding is striking — to oppress the poor is to insult (cherpah, to reproach/taunt) their "Maker" (oseh, the one who made them). THEOLOGICAL: Human dignity is grounded in creation theology: all persons bear the mark of their divine Maker. This verse anticipates the NT principle that what is done to the least is done to Christ (Matt 25:40–45).

verses.flow: A wicked man is overthrown through his evildoing, but the righteous finds refuge in his death.
Wisdom rests in the heart of a man of understanding, but what is in the heart of fools makes itself known.
Righteousness exalts a nation, but sin is a reproach to any people.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Tsedaqah" (H6666, righteousness/justice) and "chesed" both elevate nations in Proverbs' political theology. "Reproach" (cherpah, H2781) is the same word used of shame that attaches to covenant infidelity. THEOLOGICAL: National exaltation is covenantal — it flows from alignment with YHWH's moral order. This has direct implications for political ethics and the theology of nations under divine governance.

verses.flow: A servant who acts wisely has the favor of a king, but his wrath falls on one who acts shamefully.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "servant who acts wisely" (maskil, one who has insight/prudence) in the royal court setting reflects the ANE wisdom tradition of court sages whose practical wisdom earned them advancement. ANE texts from Egypt and Mesopotamia (e.g., the Instruction of Amenemope) similarly stress prudent service before rulers. TYPOLOGY: Joseph and Daniel stand as canonical examples of the "wise servant" whose wisdom wins royal favor — both typologically pointing to Christ as the supremely wise servant exalted to the right hand of the Father.

verses.flow: A soft answer turns away wrath, but a word of pain (etsev) stirs up anger.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Soft answer" — the adjective rach (H7390) means tender, gentle, delicate. The noun for "wrath" is chemah (H2534), meaning burning heat or fury, often used of both human and divine anger. The contrast is stark: a gentle response literally "turns back" (yashuv, H7725) destructive fire.

verses.flow: The tongue of the wise makes knowledge good, but the mouth of fools pours out folly.

words.g: and good in the widest sense

verses.flow: The eyes of YHWH are in every place, keeping watch over the evil and the good.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse affirms the omniscience of YHWH in the most comprehensive terms. The Hebrew tsophot (from H6822, to watch or keep watch) implies active surveillance, not passive knowledge. YHWH's eyes are not merely aware of deeds but are engaged in moral evaluation of all human conduct. (Ps 139:1-12; Heb 4:13)

verses.flow: A wholesome (marpe) tongue is a tree of life, but perverseness in it is a breaking of the spirit.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Wholesome tongue" — marpe (H4832) means healing or health; it can describe both physical and spiritual restoration. "Tree of life" (ets chayyim, H6086/H2416) is a direct echo of Eden (Gen 2:9) and recurs in Proverbs (3:18; 11:30; 13:12), suggesting that wisdom language participates in the creational order. TYPOLOGY: The "tree of life" imagery anticipates Christ as the source of life (John 11:25; Rev 2:7), whose words are spirit and life (John 6:63).

verses.flow: A fool despises the discipline (musar) of his father, but whoever heeds reproof is prudent.
In the house of the righteous there is much treasure, but in the revenue of the wicked there is trouble.
The lips of the wise scatter knowledge, but the heart of fools is not so.
The sacrifice of the wicked is an abomination (toevah) to YHWH, but the prayer of the upright is his delight.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The term toevah (H8441), "abomination," is covenantal language of the strongest kind — it describes what YHWH finds utterly repugnant and incompatible with his holiness. Remarkably, it is here applied not to an act of gross immorality but to religious ritual performed by the wicked. Outward sacrifice without inner righteousness is not merely inadequate — it is loathsome. (Isa 1:11-17; Amos 5:21-24)

verses.flow: The way of the wicked is an abomination to YHWH, but he loves the one who pursues righteousness.
Discipline is grievous to him who forsakes the path; he who hates reproof shall die.

words.g: Hades or the world of the dead

verses.flow: Sheol and Abaddon lie open before YHWH — how much more the hearts of the children of man.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "Abaddon" (H11, from abad, to perish) appears here alongside Sheol as the realm of the dead or destruction; it is used later in Job 26:6 and Rev 9:11 where it is the name of the angel of the abyss. The argument moves from the greatest hiddenness (the underworld) to the lesser (human hearts) — if even the cosmic depths of death are transparent to YHWH, human interiority is fully known. This reflects the ancient Hebrew understanding of YHWH's sovereignty extending over all cosmic realms. (Job 26:6; Ps 139:8; Rev 9:11)

verses.flow: A scoffer does not love the one who reproves him; he will not go to the wise.
A glad heart makes the face cheerful, but by sorrow of heart the spirit is crushed.
The heart of the understanding one seeks knowledge, but the mouth of fools feeds on folly.

words.g: and good in the widest sense

verses.flow: All the days of the afflicted are evil, but a good heart has a continual feast.
Better is a little with the fear (yirat) of YHWH than great treasure and turmoil with it.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Fear of YHWH" — yirat YHWH (H3374 + H3068) is the foundational concept of wisdom literature, appearing in Proverbs 1:7 as the very beginning of wisdom. It denotes reverent awe, covenantal loyalty, and moral submission to YHWH — not servile dread. THEOLOGICAL: The "better than" (tov min) formula appears frequently in Proverbs and reflects a wisdom genre of comparative value sayings common in ANE literature, but here it is grounded in covenantal theology rather than mere pragmatism.

verses.flow: Better is a portion of herbs where love (ahavah) is than a fattened ox and hatred with it.
A man of wrath stirs up strife, but one slow to anger quiets contention.
The way of the sluggard is as a hedge of thorns, but the path of the upright is a raised highway.
A wise son makes a father glad, but a foolish man despises his mother.
Folly is joy to him who lacks heart, but a man of understanding walks straight.
Without counsel plans are frustrated, but with many advisors they are established.
A man has joy in the answer of his mouth, and a word in its season — how good it is!

words.g: to/for to be circumspect and hence
from Hades or the world of the dead

verses.flow: The path of life goes upward for the prudent, that he may turn away from Sheol below.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "path of life upward" (orach chayyim lema'alah, lit. "the way of life toward above") sets wisdom in a cosmological frame — wisdom leads away from Sheol (the realm of death below) toward life. This trajectory prefigures the resurrection hope and the NT promise of eternal life. TYPOLOGY: Christ declared himself "the way, the truth, and the life" (John 14:6), fulfilling the personified path of wisdom; the righteous man's upward path points to the ascension and glorification of the Son. (John 14:6; Phil 3:14)

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: YHWH tears down the house of the proud, but he establishes the boundary of the widow.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's tearing down the house of the proud while establishing the widow's boundary is a direct expression of the divine reversal theme (cf. Hannah's prayer in 1 Sam 2; the Magnificat in Luke 1:51-53). The "boundary of the widow" recalls the Mosaic legislation protecting the vulnerable (Deut 19:14; 27:17). YHWH himself acts as the divine Kinsman-Redeemer for those who have no human protector.

verses.flow: The thoughts of the wicked are an abomination to YHWH, but gracious words (imrei noam) are pure.
He who is greedy for unjust gain troubles his own household, but he who hates bribes will live.
The heart of the righteous ponders how to answer, but the mouth of the wicked pours out evil things.
YHWH is far from the wicked, but he hears the prayer of the righteous.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The distance of YHWH from the wicked is not merely relational but covenantal — it echoes the covenant curses of Deut 28 where YHWH's face is turned away. By contrast, YHWH's attentiveness to the prayer of the righteous reflects the Aaronic benediction (Num 6:25-26). This verse is the covenantal heartbeat of the book: relationship with YHWH is the axis of all blessing and cursing. (Ps 34:15-16; John 9:31; 1 Pet 3:12)

verses.flow: The light of the eyes gladdens the heart, and good news makes the bones fat.
The ear that listens to life-giving reproof will dwell among the wise.
He who ignores discipline despises his own soul, but he who listens to reproof acquires heart.
The fear of YHWH is the discipline (musar) of wisdom, and before honor is humility.
verses.note: HEBREW: "The fear of YHWH is the discipline (musar) of wisdom" — musar (H4148) encompasses instruction, correction, and chastening; it is the same word used of the father's discipline in 1:8 and 3:11. The final clause — "before honor is humility (anavah)" — forms an inclusio with the book's opening theme (1:7) and anticipates New Testament teaching. TYPOLOGY: The pairing of humility and honor is fulfilled in Christ: the one who humbled himself even to the cross was highly exalted (Phil 2:8-9). (Prov 1:7; Jas 4:10; Phil 2:8-9)

words.g: to/for ruddy i.e. a human being

verses.flow: The preparations of the heart belong to man, but the answer of the tongue is from YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: The Hebrew ma'arekh (Strong's H4633, "arrangement" or "preparation") refers to the ordering of thoughts in the heart. The contrast between human planning and YHWH's sovereign response highlights the limits of human autonomy. This verse opens a cluster of sayings (vv. 1-9) that emphasize divine sovereignty over human agency. THEOLOGICAL: This tension is not fatalism but a call to humility — man prepares, God determines.

verses.flow: All the ways of a man are pure in his own eyes, but YHWH weighs the spirits (ruachot).
verses.note: HEBREW: The word ruachot (spirits/breath) here refers to the inner motivations of man; YHWH is described as the one who "weighs" (toqen, H8254) them, implying perfect moral evaluation. THEOLOGICAL: This echoes the divine council motif of judgment where inner realities are fully transparent before YHWH, paralleling Proverbs 21:2 and 1 Samuel 16:7.

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal jussive 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: Roll your works upon YHWH, and your plans will be established.
YHWH has made everything for its purpose (ma'aneh), even the wicked for the day of evil.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word ma'aneh (H4617, "answer" or "purpose") here conveys that YHWH made all things with a corresponding purpose or reply — even the wicked exist within his sovereign design. THEOLOGICAL: This is one of Proverbs' most direct statements of divine sovereignty over evil; it does not render YHWH the author of wickedness but affirms that even evil is bounded within his purposes (cf. Romans 9:22).

verses.flow: Everyone proud of heart is an abomination (to'evah) to YHWH; hand to hand, he will not go unpunished.
By lovingkindness (hesed) and faithfulness (emet) iniquity is atoned for, and by the fear of YHWH one turns away from evil.
verses.note: HEBREW: Hesed (H2617, lovingkindness/covenant loyalty) and emet (H571, faithfulness/truth) form a classic Hebrew pairing found throughout the Psalms and Proverbs. The verb translated "atoned for" is yekuppar (from kapar, H3722), the same root used of the sacrificial atonement system. TYPOLOGY: This anticipates Christ's atoning work, in whom hesed and emet are perfectly embodied (John 1:14 — "grace and truth").

verses.flow: When the ways of a man please YHWH, he makes even his enemies to be at peace (shalom) with him.
Better is a little with righteousness than great revenues without justice.
The heart of a man plans his way, but YHWH establishes his steps.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Verse 9 forms the theological center of the opening section (vv. 1-9), affirming that YHWH's sovereignty does not eliminate human planning but governs its outcome. HEBREW: The verb yakhin ("establishes," H3559) is used covenantally elsewhere (2 Sam 7:12-13) of God's establishment of David's throne, giving this common wisdom saying a deeper resonance.

verses.flow: An oracle (qesem) is on the lips of the king; in judgment his mouth does not act treacherously.
verses.note: HEBREW: Qesem (H7081) normally means "divination" and is forbidden in the Law (Deut 18:10), but here it is used metaphorically for the authoritative, almost oracular weight of a king's judicial pronouncements. TYPOLOGY: The ideal king whose lips carry divine authority anticipates the Messiah, whose words are spirit and life (John 6:63; cf. Isaiah 11:2-4).

verses.flow: A just balance and scales are YHWH's; all the weights of the bag are his work.
To do wickedness is an abomination to kings, for by righteousness a throne is established.
Righteous lips are the delight of kings, and he who speaks uprightly is loved.

words.m: verb piel imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: The wrath of a king is a messenger of death, but a wise man will appease it.
In the light of a king's face is life, and his favor is like a cloud of the latter rain.
To acquire wisdom, how much better than gold! And to acquire understanding is to be chosen over silver.
The highway of the upright is to turn away from evil; he who guards his way preserves his life (nefesh).
Pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word gaavah (H1346, "pride" or "arrogance") describes an elevated self-assessment that usurps the place of YHWH. THEOLOGICAL: This proverb is among the most quoted biblical statements on pride, and its context in a chapter about kingship suggests that pride is the defining sin of illegitimate rule. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Pride is also the archetypal sin of fallen heavenly beings (cf. Isaiah 14:13-14; Ezekiel 28:17), making this warning cosmic in scope.

verses.flow: Better to be of a lowly spirit with the humble than to divide plunder with the proud.

words.g: in/with good in the widest sense

verses.flow: Whoever gives attention to the word will discover good, and blessed is he who trusts in YHWH.
The wise of heart is called discerning, and sweetness of lips increases persuasiveness (leqach).
Good sense (sekhel) is a fountain of life to its possessor, but the instruction of fools is folly.
The heart of the wise makes his mouth discerning, and on his lips it adds persuasiveness (leqach).
Pleasant words are a honeycomb, sweet to the soul (nefesh) and healing to the bones.
There is a way that seems upright before a man, but its end is the ways of death.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse repeats nearly verbatim Proverbs 14:12, a structural device emphasizing that the theme of self-deception is central to Proverbs' wisdom. The double occurrence underscores that the way seeming right to man is among the gravest dangers. HEBREW: The Hebrew yashar ("upright/straight") used of this deceptive way is the same root used of righteous conduct elsewhere, highlighting how thoroughly self-deception mimics virtue.

verses.flow: A laborer's appetite (nefesh) labors for him, for his mouth urges him on.
A worthless man (ish beliyya'al) digs up evil, and on his lips there is as a scorching fire.
verses.note: HEBREW: Ish beliyya'al (H1100 + H376, "man of worthlessness/man of Belial") is a technical term in the Hebrew Bible for a morally corrupt and destructive person. Beliyya'al later became a name for an adversarial spiritual being in Second Temple literature and the NT (2 Cor 6:15). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The use of beliyya'al as both a human descriptor and a demonic entity in the broader canon suggests a correspondence between human wickedness and spiritual corruption.

verses.flow: A perverse man spreads strife, and a whisperer separates close friends.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: A man of violence entices his neighbor and leads him in a way that is not good.
He who winks his eyes to devise perversities, he who purses his lips brings evil to pass.
Gray hair is a crown of glory; it is gained in a way of righteousness.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Gray hair as a crown of glory (ateret tiferet) ties honor to a righteous life rather than mere longevity; it is not age itself but age achieved through the way of righteousness that is honored. HISTORICAL: In the ANE, elders were revered as bearers of wisdom and divine blessing, making this proverb culturally resonant across the ancient world.

words.g: in/with a city in the widest sense

verses.flow: Better is the slow to anger than the mighty, and he who rules his spirit than he who captures a city.
The lot (goral) is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: Goral (H1486, "lot") refers to the physical casting of lots used for decision-making in ancient Israel (cf. Joshua's land allotment, Jonah 1:7, Acts 1:26). THEOLOGICAL: The proverb does not endorse lot-casting as a general practice but makes the theological point that even ostensibly random outcomes are governed by YHWH's sovereign will, bringing the chapter to a fitting doxological close.

verses.flow: Better is a dry morsel with quietness (shalom) than a house full of feasting with strife.
verses.note: HEBREW: "shalom" (H7965, שָׁלוֹם) here denotes completeness and tranquility, not merely peace. The verse contrasts a "dry morsel" (pat charevah, a crumb of bread) with a house of "slaughtered sacrifices" (zivchei-riv), suggesting feasting carried to excess. The wisdom tradition consistently prizes inner harmony over material abundance. (Matt 6:31-33)

verses.flow: A servant who acts wisely will rule over a son who causes shame, and among brothers he will share in the inheritance.
The refining pot is for silver and the furnace for gold, but YHWH is the one who tests hearts.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The image of YHWH as the divine Refiner who tests hearts places Him above any human or metallurgical process. Just as fire purifies silver and gold, YHWH's providential trials expose and purify the inner man. This is not merely moral testing but covenantal examination. (Ps 66:10; Mal 3:3; 1 Pet 1:7) TYPOLOGY: Christ as the ultimate Refiner fulfills this image in the eschatological judgment and in the sanctifying work of the Spirit. (Mal 3:2-3)

verses.flow: An evildoer gives heed to wicked lips; a liar gives ear to a destructive tongue.
Whoever mocks the poor reproaches his Maker; whoever rejoices at calamity will not go unpunished.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The proverb links mockery of the poor directly to reproach of YHWH as Creator (Maker = עֹשֵׂהוּ, 'oseihu), grounding ethics in creation theology rather than mere social convention. To despise an image-bearer is to despise the One in whose image he was made. (Prov 14:31; Gen 1:27)

verses.flow: Grandchildren are the crown of the aged, and the glory of sons is their fathers.
Excellent speech is not fitting for a fool; how much less are lying lips for a noble.
A bribe is like a magic stone in the eyes of the one who gives it; wherever he turns, he prospers.
Whoever covers over a transgression seeks love (hesed), but whoever repeats a matter separates close friends.
verses.note: HEBREW: "hesed" (H2617, חֶסֶד) carries the weight of covenant loyalty and steadfast love, not merely affection. "Covers a transgression" (mekhasseh-pesha) echoes the Levitical language of atonement — to cover sin is a redemptive act. Repeating a matter (shoneh) implies a betrayal of confidence that fractures relational bonds. TYPOLOGY: The one who covers transgression anticipates the atoning work of the Messiah, whose blood covers the sins of the covenant people. (1 Pet 4:8; Ps 32:1)

verses.flow: A rebuke goes deeper into a man of understanding than a hundred blows into a fool.
An evil man seeks only rebellion, and a cruel messenger will be sent against him.
Let a bear robbed of her cubs meet a man rather than a fool in his folly.

words.g: and good in the widest sense

verses.flow: Whoever returns evil for good — evil will not depart from his house.
The beginning of strife is like one who releases water; therefore, abandon the quarrel before it breaks out.
He who justifies the wicked and he who condemns the righteous — both of them alike are an abomination to YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The Hebrew uses the word "toevat YHWH" (תּוֹעֲבַת יְהוָה), "abomination of YHWH," the strongest possible expression of divine revulsion. Both the inversion of justice — acquitting the guilty and condemning the innocent — are equally detestable. This undergirds the entire biblical theology of righteous judgment. (Deut 25:1; Isa 5:23) TYPOLOGY: The unjust condemnation of the righteous Son of God (Matt 27:24) and the justification of sinners through his sacrifice (Rom 3:26) together fulfill and reverse this proverb at the deepest theological level.

verses.flow: Why is there a price in the hand of a fool to buy wisdom, when he has no heart for it?
A friend loves at all times, and a brother is born for adversity.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse presents the highest ideal of covenant friendship in the wisdom literature. "Loves at all times" (ahav b'khol-et) implies a love that transcends circumstance, while "born for adversity" (l'tzarah yivaled) suggests the brother's purpose is revealed in crisis. The language parallels covenant (berit) loyalty. (John 15:13) TYPOLOGY: The perfect fulfillment of this friendship is seen in Christ, who loves his own to the end and is born — incarnate — precisely to meet humanity's greatest adversity.

verses.flow: A man lacking heart strikes hands in pledge and gives surety before his neighbor.
Whoever loves transgression loves strife; whoever makes his door high seeks destruction.

words.g: in/with good in the widest sense
and to turn about or over

verses.flow: A man of crooked heart does not find good, and one with a perverted tongue falls into calamity.
He who begets a fool does so to his grief, and the father of a fool has no joy.
A joyful heart is good medicine, but a crushed spirit dries up the bones.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Lev sameach" (לֵב שָׂמֵחַ) — a "rejoicing heart" — is described as "gehah," meaning healing or medicine (H1456). The crushed or stricken spirit (ruach nekhe'ah) literally "dries the bone" (teyabesh-gerem), a visceral image of physical wasting from spiritual despair. The proverb unites psychosomatic reality with wisdom teaching, remarkable in its medical intuition. (Ps 32:3-4)

words.g: to/for to stretch or spread out

verses.flow: A wicked man takes a bribe from the bosom to pervert the paths of justice.
Wisdom is before the face of him who has understanding, but the eyes of a fool are on the ends of the earth.

words.m: preposition + verb qal participle active + suffix

verses.flow: A foolish son is vexation to his father and bitterness to her who bore him.
Also, to punish the righteous is not good, nor to strike the noble for their uprightness.
Whoever restrains his words has knowledge, and a man of understanding is of a calm spirit (ruach).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Restrain his words" (chosiikh amarav) from the root חָשַׂךְ (H2820), meaning to hold back or spare. The second clause, "calm (or cool) of spirit (ruach)" — yeqar-ruach — may be rendered "precious of spirit" (yeqar, H3368 = precious/rare) in many manuscripts, yielding "a man of understanding is of a rare spirit." Both readings are theologically significant. THEOLOGICAL: Verbal restraint is here counted as wisdom itself, forming a bridge to verse 28's observation that silence mimics understanding even in the fool.

verses.flow: Even a fool who keeps silent is considered wise; he who closes his lips is deemed a man of understanding.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This famous verse — sometimes called the "fool's wisdom" — demonstrates that wisdom is partly social and discernible: the fool who is silent cannot be distinguished from the wise. The proverb functions as both practical counsel and ironic observation. It has a long reception history, often compared to the maxim attributed to Lincoln or Twain. HISTORICAL: This verse reflects a broader ANE wisdom tradition in which silence is a mark of the trained scribe and sage, attested in Egyptian instructions such as the Instruction of Amenemope. (Eccl 5:2-3; Jas 1:19)

verses.flow: He who separates himself seeks his own desire; against all sound wisdom he breaks out.
A fool takes no delight in understanding, but only in uncovering his own heart.
When the wicked comes, contempt comes also, and with dishonor comes reproach.
The words of a man's mouth are deep waters; the fountain of wisdom is a flowing brook.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Deep waters" (mayim amuqqim, H6013) is a recurring wisdom metaphor for hidden, inexhaustible depth — used again in Prov 20:5. "Flowing brook" (nahal nobe'a) suggests an overflowing, life-giving stream, contrasting still mystery with active outpouring. The verse implies that true wisdom issues forth from an inner source of great depth.

words.g: to/for to stretch or spread out

verses.flow: It is not good to show partiality to the wicked, so as to thrust aside the righteous in judgment.
The lips of a fool enter into strife, and his mouth invites blows.
A fool's mouth is his ruin, and his lips are a snare to his soul (nephesh).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Soul" (nephesh, H5315) here denotes the whole self or life-breath — not merely an immaterial spirit but the entirety of a person's being. The fool's lips become a trap (moqesh, H4170) set against his own existence, emphasizing self-destructive consequences of careless speech.

verses.flow: The words of a whisperer are like delicious morsels; they go down into the innermost chambers of the belly.
He also who is slack in his work is brother to him who destroys.

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: The name of YHWH is a strong tower; the righteous man runs into it and is set on high.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "name of YHWH" (shem YHWH) encapsulates His revealed character, covenant identity, and saving power. To "run into it" images a besieged person fleeing to a fortified tower (migdal oz, H4026 + H5797). This verse is foundational to the biblical theology of refuge in YHWH, anticipating the NT theme of Christ as the one in whom believers are hidden and protected (Col 3:3; Prov 30:5).

words.g: and + as/like a wall of protection

verses.flow: A rich man's wealth is his strong city, and like a high wall it is in his own imagination.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Verse 11 stands in deliberate ironic contrast to verse 10. The rich man's city is imagined (maskil, "in his imagination" — lit. "in his own mind"), whereas YHWH's tower is objectively strong. The juxtaposition exposes the self-deception of trusting material security over divine refuge (Ps 49:6; Luke 12:19).

verses.flow: Before destruction a man's heart is haughty, but before honor is humility.
He who gives an answer before he hears — it is folly and shame to him.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: A man's spirit (ruach) sustains him in his sickness, but a crushed spirit — who can bear it?
verses.note: HEBREW: "Spirit" (ruach, H7307) in this context means the inner animating force of a person — courage, resolve, and vitality. A resilient ruach can carry a person through bodily affliction, but when the spirit itself is broken (nakeh ruach), the implied question "who can bear it?" is left unanswered, underscoring the severity of inner collapse. Compare the "broken spirit" language in Ps 34:18.

verses.flow: An understanding heart acquires knowledge, and the ear of the wise seeks knowledge.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: A man's gift makes room for him and leads him before the great.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: The first to state his case seems righteous, but then his neighbor comes and searches him out.
The lot puts an end to quarrels and decides between mighty ones.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The casting of lots (goral, H1486) was a common ANE and Israelite practice for discerning divine will or resolving disputes (Lev 16:8; Josh 18:6; Acts 1:26). The lot was not seen as random chance but as the providential determination of YHWH (Prov 16:33), making it an instrument of divine arbitration in human conflict.

verses.flow: A brother offended is harder to win than a strong city, and quarrels are like the bars of a citadel.
From the fruit of a man's mouth his belly is satisfied; with the yield of his lips he is satisfied.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal participle active + suffix

verses.flow: Death and life are in the power of the tongue, and those who love it will eat its fruit.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Death and life are in the power of the tongue" establishes the tongue as a cosmic instrument with eschatological weight. The Hebrew yad (H3027, "hand" or "power") of the tongue implies agency and dominion. James 3:5-10 echoes this passage directly, reflecting on the tongue's destructive and constructive potential. This verse grounds much of the NT theology of confession and witness.

words.g: in/with good in the widest sense

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: He who finds a wife finds a good thing and obtains favor (ratson) from YHWH.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: "He who finds a wife finds a good thing" uses the verb matsa' (H4672, to find, encounter), suggesting discovery of a divinely-ordered gift. "Favor from YHWH" (ratson, H7522 — delight, good pleasure) frames marriage as a covenantal blessing. The imagery of the ideal wife in Prov 31 casts her as a figure of Wisdom herself, and NT typology sees Christ as the bridegroom who finds and redeems His bride, the Church (Eph 5:25-27; Rev 19:7).

verses.flow: The poor man speaks with pleadings, but the rich man answers with harshness.
A man of many companions may come to ruin, but there is a friend who sticks closer than a brother.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "friend who sticks closer than a brother" (davaq, H1692 — to cling, cleave) uses the same root as Gen 2:24, where a man "cleaves" to his wife. This covenantal adhesion language points beyond human friendship to the loyal-love (hesed) of the covenant God. Christian readers have consistently read this as a type of Christ — the friend who lays down His life (John 15:13-15), surpassing all familial bond.

verses.flow: Better is a poor man who walks in his integrity than one who is crooked in his lips and is a fool.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Walks in his integrity" uses the root tamim (H8537), meaning wholeness or blamelessness, cognate with the word used of Noah (Gen 6:9) and Job (Job 1:1). The contrast between moral integrity and perverse speech reflects the Solomonic wisdom tradition's emphasis on the alignment of character and conduct. "Crooked in his lips" ('iqqesh sephatayw) denotes one whose words are deliberately twisted or deceptive.

verses.flow: Also, that the soul (nephesh) without knowledge is not good, and whoever makes haste with his feet sins.
The folly of a man twists his way, and against YHWH his heart rages.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse captures a profound human tendency — to blame God for the consequences of one's own folly. The verb za'aph (H2196), "rages" or "frets," implies an inward seething directed upward toward YHWH. Rather than acknowledging personal responsibility, the foolish man's heart turns against his Creator — an inversion of the fear of YHWH that is wisdom's foundation (Prov 1:7).

verses.flow: Wealth adds many friends, but the poor man is separated from his friend.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: A false witness will not go unpunished, and whoever breathes out lies will not escape.
Many court the favor of a noble, and everyone is a friend to a man who gives gifts.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd common plur + suffix

verses.flow: All the brothers of a poor man hate him; how much more do his friends go far from him! He pursues them with words, but they are not there.

words.g: in/with good in the widest sense

verses.flow: Whoever acquires heart (lev) acquires life (nephesh); he who guards understanding will find good.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Acquires heart" uses the Hebrew lev (H3820), the seat of the mind, will, and understanding in Hebrew thought — not merely emotion as in modern usage. To "acquire lev" is to cultivate wisdom, discernment, and moral reasoning. The parallelism with nephesh (soul/life) highlights that true self-love — properly ordered — leads one to pursue wisdom, a theme Paul echoes in Rom 12:2's "renewing of the mind."

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: A false witness will not go unpunished, and whoever breathes out lies will perish.
Luxury is not fitting for a fool; how much less for a servant to rule over princes.
The insight (sekel) of a man makes him slow to anger, and his glory is to pass over a transgression.
verses.note: HEBREW: Sekel (H7922), rendered "insight," denotes shrewd, practical discernment — the capacity to read a situation rightly and act accordingly. "Slow to anger" ('erek aph, lit. long of nose) is a phrase applied to YHWH Himself in Exod 34:6, so the wise man here mirrors a divine attribute. TYPOLOGY: This bears directly on the Messiah, who in His earthly ministry embodied patient forbearance in the face of transgression (Isa 53:7; 1 Pet 2:23).

verses.flow: The wrath of a king is like the growling of a lion, but his favor (ratzon) is like dew upon the grass.
A foolish son is ruin to his father, and the contentions of a wife are a continual dripping.
House and wealth are inherited from fathers, but a wife who acts wisely is from YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The verse draws a distinction between what human inheritance can provide — house and wealth — and what only YHWH can give: a prudent wife. The Hebrew maskelet (from sakal, H7919) means "one who acts wisely or with insight." This elevates the godly spouse beyond the transactional and situates her as a direct gift of divine providence, anticipating the portrait of the noble wife in Prov 31:10-31.

verses.flow: Sloth (atslah) casts into a deep sleep, and a slack soul (nephesh) will go hungry.
He who keeps the commandment keeps his soul (nephesh), but he who despises his ways will die.
Whoever is gracious to the poor lends to YHWH, and He will repay him for his deed.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This remarkable verse portrays YHWH as a debtor who receives a loan on behalf of the poor. The verb lavah (H3867), "lends," is the same root used for ordinary financial lending. The theological implication is staggering — generosity to the poor is credited to YHWH's account, and He personally guarantees repayment. Jesus echoes this principle in Matt 25:40: "as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me."

verses.flow: Discipline your son, for there is hope; and do not lift up your soul (nephesh) to put him to death.
A man of great wrath will bear the penalty, for if you deliver him, you will only do it again.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel imperative 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Listen to counsel and receive discipline, that you may be wise in your latter end.
Many are the plans in the heart (lev) of a man, but it is the counsel of YHWH that will stand.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The sovereignty of YHWH's counsel ('etsah, H6098) over human machinations is a recurring wisdom theme (Prov 16:9; Ps 33:10-11). The plural "plans" (machashavot) in the human heart stands against the singular, immovable "counsel" of YHWH. This is not fatalism but confidence in a purposeful God who governs history — a foundation for Messianic expectation and the fulfillment of redemptive purpose (Eph 1:11; Isa 46:10).

words.g: and good in the widest sense

verses.flow: What is desired in a man is his lovingkindness (hesed), and better is a poor man than a liar.
verses.note: HEBREW: Hesed (H2617) — one of the richest theological terms in the Hebrew Bible — denotes covenant loyalty, lovingkindness, steadfast love, and faithful devotion. Here it is presented as the most desirable quality in a human being, suggesting that the imitation of divine hesed is the hallmark of genuine humanity. THEOLOGICAL: The second half of the verse — preferring poverty to dishonesty — reaffirms the moral calculus of v.1, grounding ethics in character rather than circumstance.

verses.flow: The fear of YHWH leads to life, and he who has it will abide in satisfaction; he will not be visited by evil.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The fear of YHWH" (yir'at YHWH) is the central epistemological and ethical principle of the entire book (Prov 1:7; 9:10). Here it is directly linked to life (chayyim, H2416) and to a state of satisfaction (sava', H7646) — fullness, abundance. The promise that evil will not "visit" (paqad) the one who fears YHWH frames godly wisdom as a form of covenant protection, reflecting Deut 28's blessings for obedience.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: The sluggard buries his hand in the dish and will not even bring it back to his mouth.
Strike a scoffer and the simple will be made prudent; reprove one who has understanding and he will gain knowledge.
He who assaults his father and drives away his mother is a son who causes shame and brings reproach.
Cease, my son, to hear instruction — only to stray from the words of knowledge.
verses.note: HEBREW: This verse is deliberately ironic or sarcastic in its construction. The imperative "cease to hear instruction" is followed by a result clause showing the inevitable outcome — straying from knowledge. Some interpreters read it as a direct warning: "stop listening to instruction and you will stray." The Hebrew construction supports reading this as a biting conditional: further refusal to hear is tantamount to willfully choosing the path of the fool.

verses.flow: A worthless witness mocks justice, and the mouth of the wicked swallows iniquity.
Judgments are prepared for scoffers, and blows for the backs of fools.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The chapter closes with an affirmation of divine justice — "judgments are prepared" (konu shephatiym) for scoffers, a perfect passive implying divine ordination. The scoffer (lets, H3887), who mocks wisdom and reproof throughout Proverbs, will not escape accountability. "Blows for the backs of fools" echoes the lex talionis principle: those who refuse verbal correction will receive physical consequence. This final verse frames the entire chapter within a framework of moral accountability before YHWH.

verses.flow: Wine is a mocker, strong drink (shekar) a brawler, and whoever is led astray by it is not wise.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Wine" (yayin, H3196) and "strong drink" (shekar, H7941, likely barley beer or date wine) are personified as a "mocker" (lets, H3887) and "brawler" (homeh, H1993). The warning is not absolute abstinence but against being "led astray" (shagah, H7686), implying voluntary intoxication. THEOLOGICAL: Wisdom literature frames sobriety as prerequisite for discernment, linking it to the fear of YHWH as the foundation of wisdom throughout Proverbs.

verses.flow: The dread of a king is like the growling of a lion; whoever provokes him to anger forfeits his own life.
It is an honor for a man to keep back from strife, but every fool will be quarreling.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: The sluggard does not plow in autumn; he seeks at harvest and there is nothing.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: The counsel (etsah) in a man's heart is deep water, but a man of understanding will draw it out.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Counsel" (etsah, H6098) as "deep water" (mayim amuqqim, H4325/H6013) uses a striking metaphor for the hidden depths of human intention and wisdom. The "man of understanding" (ish tevunah) is one who can draw such wisdom out through skilled questioning and discernment. TYPOLOGY: Christ as the one in whom are hidden all treasures of wisdom (Col 2:3) is the ultimate fulfillment of this capacity to draw out the deepest counsel.

verses.flow: Many a man proclaims his own steadfast love (hesed), but a faithful man who can find?
verses.note: HEBREW: The word "proclaims" (yiqra, H7121) implies public declaration. "Steadfast love" (hesed, H2617) is covenantal loyalty — far richer than sentiment. The rhetorical question "who can find?" echoes Proverbs 31:10 and implies extreme rarity. THEOLOGICAL: The tension between claimed virtue and lived faithfulness is a recurring wisdom theme; only YHWH can evaluate the heart (v. 27).

verses.flow: A righteous man who walks in his integrity — blessed are his children after him.
A king who sits on the throne of judgment winnows all evil with his eyes.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The king who "winnows all evil with his eyes" anticipates the messianic king whose judgment is perfectly righteous (Isa 11:3-4; Rev 19:11-16). The throne of judgment (kisse din) is a royal-judicial image fulfilled in Christ, the son of David, who will judge the nations. THEOLOGICAL: Kingship in Israel carries inherent judicial responsibility — the king is YHWH's vice-regent for establishing justice.

verses.flow: Who can say, "I have made my heart clean; I am pure from my sin"?

words.g: an ephah or measure for grain
and an ephah or measure for grain

verses.flow: Differing weights and differing measures — both of them alike are an abomination to YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Differing weights" (even va'even, literally "stone and stone") and "differing measures" (eyphah va'eyphah, "ephah and ephah") — the doubling implies having two sets, one for buying and one for selling, constituting commercial fraud. "Abomination" (to'evah, H8441) is the strongest term of divine disgust in the Hebrew ethical vocabulary. HISTORICAL: Dishonest weights were condemned throughout the ANE; Deuteronomy 25:13-16 codifies this into law.

verses.flow: Even a young man is known by his deeds, whether his work is pure and upright.
The hearing ear and the seeing eye — YHWH has made both of them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Both the "hearing ear" and "seeing eye" are ascribed directly to YHWH as their maker (asah, H6213), grounding human sensory and rational capacity in divine creation. This confronts any dualistic diminishment of the body. TYPOLOGY: The one who gave ears and eyes is the one who truly hears and sees (Ps 94:9), an implicit rebuke of idols that cannot perceive (Ps 115:5-6).

verses.flow: Do not love sleep, lest you come to poverty; open your eyes and you will have plenty of bread.
"Bad, bad!" says the buyer, but when he goes away, then he boasts.
There is gold, and an abundance of corals (peninim), but lips of knowledge are a precious vessel.

words.g: and in up to or over against

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Take a man's garment when he has given surety for a stranger, and hold it in pledge when he gives surety for foreigners.

words.g: to fill or be full of

verses.flow: Bread gained by deceit is sweet to a man, but afterward his mouth will be full of gravel.
Plans are established by counsel (etsah); by wise guidance wage war.

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb qal participle active

verses.flow: Whoever goes about slandering reveals secrets; therefore do not associate with a simple babbler.

words.g: in/with the little man of the eye
in/with the little man of the eye

verses.flow: Whoever curses his father or his mother, his lamp will be put out in the darkness of night.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Curses" (meqallel, H7043) carries the sense of treating as contemptible or light. "His lamp will be put out" (yidak nero, H1846) uses the image of an extinguished lamp — in Hebrew culture the lamp symbolizes life, progeny, and dynastic continuation (1 Kgs 11:36). THEOLOGICAL: The fifth commandment (Exod 20:12) carries a covenantal blessing; its violation leads to covenantal curse, including the "darkness of night" — a figure for utter desolation.

verses.flow: An inheritance gained hastily at the beginning will not be blessed in its end.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Do not say, "I will repay evil"; wait for YHWH, and he will deliver you.
Differing weights are an abomination to YHWH, and false scales are not good.

words.g: and ruddy i.e. a human being

verses.flow: A man's steps are from YHWH; how then can a man understand his way?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "A man's steps (mits'adei gaver) are from YHWH" expresses divine providence over the human journey in its most granular detail. The rhetorical question "how can a man understand his way?" does not deny human agency but subordinates it to divine sovereignty. TYPOLOGY: This echoes Jeremiah 10:23 and points toward Christ as the "way" (John 14:6) — the one in whom divine and human paths are perfectly unified.

verses.flow: It is a snare to a man to say rashly, "It is holy," and after making vows to reflect.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: A wise king winnows the wicked and drives the wheel over them.
The breath (neshamah) of man is the lamp of YHWH, searching all his innermost chambers.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Breath" (neshamah, H5397) is the same word as in Genesis 2:7 where YHWH breathes life into Adam. It is distinct from ruach (spirit/wind) and refers to the inner animating breath uniquely given to humans. "Lamp of YHWH" (ner YHWH) suggests that human consciousness and conscience are divine instruments of moral illumination. THEOLOGICAL: This verse provides a key anthropological statement — the human inner life is a divine instrument for searching the "innermost chambers" (cheder, H2315), a figure for the hidden places of the soul. TYPOLOGY: The Spirit of Christ as the one who "searches all things, even the deep things of God" (1 Cor 2:10) reflects this lamp-image elevated to its fullness.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Steadfast love (hesed) and faithfulness (emet) preserve the king, and by steadfast love his throne is upheld.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The preservation of the king by "steadfast love" (hesed) and "faithfulness" (emet) anticipates the messianic king whose throne is eternally upheld by these very qualities (Ps 89:14; Isa 16:5). The pairing of hesed and emet is a covenantal formula appearing throughout the Psalter and fulfilled in Christ, who is "full of grace (hesed) and truth (emet)" (John 1:14).

verses.flow: The glory of young men is their strength, but the splendor of old men is their gray hair.
Blows that wound cleanse away evil, and strokes reach the innermost chambers.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: A king's heart is channels of water in the hand of YHWH; wherever He pleases, He turns it.
verses.note: HEBREW: "channels of water" (palgei-mayim, H6388) refers to irrigation channels that farmers redirect at will — a vivid ANE agricultural image. The point is sovereign: the most powerful human ruler is as easily redirected by YHWH as a farmer diverts a stream. THEOLOGICAL: This verse grounds royal theology in divine sovereignty, anticipating NT affirmations that governing authorities are under God's ultimate control (Rom 13:1). TYPOLOGY: The ultimate King whose heart is perfectly aligned with YHWH's is the Messiah (Ps 2:6-7; Isa 11:1-5).

verses.flow: Every way of a man is right in his own eyes, but YHWH weighs the hearts.
To do righteousness and justice is more acceptable to YHWH than sacrifice.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This proverb stands in the tradition of the prophets who subordinate ritual to ethical obedience (1 Sam 15:22; Isa 1:11-17; Mic 6:6-8). "Righteousness and justice" (tzedaqah u-mishpat) form a covenantal word-pair throughout the Hebrew Bible, describing the character of YHWH's own rule (Ps 89:14). TYPOLOGY: Christ embodies both perfectly, fulfilling the law's moral demand rather than its ceremonial shadow (Matt 5:17; Heb 10:8-10).

verses.flow: Haughty eyes and a proud heart — the lamp of the wicked — are sin.
verses.note: HEBREW: "lamp of the wicked" (ner resha'im) is a debated phrase. Some read it as a predicate — pride and haughtiness ARE the lamp (i.e., defining light) of the wicked — while others emend to "plowing" (nir). The MT reading as "lamp" is preferred: what the wicked consider their guiding light is itself sin. THEOLOGICAL: Pride heads the list of YHWH's abominations (Prov 6:16-17), and this verse grounds all wickedness in the orientation of the inner person.

verses.flow: The plans of the diligent lead surely to abundance, but everyone who is hasty comes only to poverty.
The getting of treasures by a lying tongue is a fleeting vapor, a snare of death.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: The violence of the wicked will drag them away, because they refuse to do justice.
The way of the guilty man is crooked, but the pure — his work is upright.
It is better to dwell in a corner of a rooftop than in a house shared with a contentious woman.
The soul (nefesh) of the wicked desires evil; his neighbor finds no favor in his eyes.
verses.note: HEBREW: nefesh (H5315) is the whole desiring self, often rendered "soul" but encompassing appetite, life-breath, and personhood. The verse diagnoses the root of wickedness as a disordered desire-center. THEOLOGICAL: The wicked man's nefesh is entirely bent toward evil, leaving no relational charity for the neighbor — an inversion of the Shema's call to love YHWH with all one's nefesh (Deut 6:5).

words.g: and + in/with to be circumspect and hence
words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb hiphil infinitive construct

verses.flow: When the scoffer is punished, the simple becomes wise; and when the wise is instructed, he gains knowledge.
The Righteous One observes the house of the wicked; He overthrows the wicked to their ruin.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The subject of "the Righteous One" (tzaddiq) is disputed — it may refer to a righteous man observing his wicked neighbor, or more likely to YHWH Himself as the supreme Righteous One who surveys and judges the wicked (Ps 11:4-7). The latter reading coheres with the book's frame that YHWH is the ultimate arbiter of justice. TYPOLOGY: Christ as the Righteous One (Acts 3:14; 1 John 2:1) will ultimately overthrow all wicked structures at His return (Rev 19:11-21).

verses.flow: Whoever stops his ears at the cry of the poor will himself cry out and not be answered.
A gift in secret subdues anger, and a bribe in the bosom, strong wrath.
When justice is done it is a joy to the righteous, but terror to the workers of iniquity.
A man who strays from the way of understanding will rest in the assembly of the dead (rephaim).
verses.note: HEBREW: rephaim (H7496) denotes the shades or spirits of the dead in Sheol (cf. Isa 14:9; Ps 88:10). The "assembly of the rephaim" (qahal rephaim) is the gathered dead in the underworld — a stark antithesis to the "assembly of the upright" (qahal yesharim, Ps 111:1). DIVINE_COUNCIL: The term rephaim also connects to ancient Ugaritic literature where the rpum are honored dead warriors; the biblical usage demythologizes them as mere shades with no power. THEOLOGICAL: To abandon wisdom's path is literally to take the road to death.

verses.flow: Whoever loves pleasure will be a poor man; whoever loves wine and oil will not grow rich.
The wicked is a ransom for the righteous, and the treacherous in place of the upright.
It is better to dwell in a land of wilderness than with a contentious and vexing woman.

words.m: verb piel imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Precious treasure and oil are in the dwelling of the wise, but a foolish man devours it.
Whoever pursues righteousness and loyal-love (hesed) will find life, righteousness, and honor.
verses.note: HEBREW: hesed (H2617) is YHWH's covenantal loyal-love — steadfast, faithful, enduring. It cannot be fully captured by "kindness" or "mercy" alone. The one who pursues both righteousness AND hesed imitates the very character of YHWH (Exod 34:6-7). TYPOLOGY: Christ is the fullest embodiment of hesed made flesh (John 1:14, "grace and truth"), and those united to Him are called to pursue His character (Mic 6:8).

words.g: in/with a city in the widest sense

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: A wise man scales the city of the mighty and brings down the stronghold in which they trust.
Whoever guards his mouth and his tongue guards his soul (nefesh) from trouble.
Proud, Haughty — Scoffer is his name — he acts with overflowing arrogance.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: The craving of the sluggard kills him, for his hands refuse to labor.
All day long he craves and craves, but the righteous gives and does not hold back.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: The sacrifice of the wicked is an abomination; how much more when he brings it with evil intent.
verses.note: HEBREW: "abomination" (to'evah, H8441) is the strongest term of divine revulsion in the Hebrew Bible, used frequently in Proverbs for what YHWH finds detestable. The intensification "how much more with evil intent" (zimmah, H2154 — a premeditated scheme) indicates that outward religious performance combined with wicked motive compounds guilt rather than canceling it. THEOLOGICAL: This verse anticipates the prophetic and dominical critique of hypocritical religion (Isa 1:13; Matt 23:14).

verses.flow: A lying witness will perish, but the man who listens will speak enduringly.
A wicked man hardens his face, but the upright — he discerns his ways.
There is no wisdom, and there is no understanding, and there is no counsel against YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse is one of Proverbs' most absolute theological assertions: no human wisdom, strategy, or counsel can succeed if it stands against YHWH. The triple negation ("no wisdom... no understanding... no counsel") is emphatic and total. HISTORICAL: The ANE world was filled with royal advisors, diviners, and sages whose counsel shaped nations; this proverb relativizes all such counsel before divine sovereignty. TYPOLOGY: Paul echoes this theme in 1 Cor 1:20-25, where God's "foolishness" in the cross overturns all human wisdom.

verses.flow: The horse is prepared for the day of battle, but the victory (teshuah) belongs to YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: teshuah (H8668) means deliverance, salvation, or victory — the same root as yeshua (salvation), the Hebrew name of Jesus. The "horse prepared for battle" represents the fullest human military readiness. THEOLOGICAL: Victory is not denied to human preparation, but its ultimate source is YHWH alone (Ps 20:7; 33:17). TYPOLOGY: The one whose name IS teshuah — Yeshua/Jesus — is the ultimate fulfillment: the victory that belongs to YHWH came through His Son (1 Cor 15:57; Rev 7:10).

verses.flow: A name (shem) of good repute is to be chosen over great riches; and favor (chen) is better than silver and gold.
verses.note: HEBREW: "shem" (H8034) means name or reputation; the verse establishes that a good name — one's moral standing and honor — surpasses material wealth. "Chen" (H2580, favor or grace) is the relational quality that commends one to others. THEOLOGICAL: The Solomonic tradition consistently holds that character precedes prosperity, echoing the covenantal emphasis that fidelity to YHWH produces honor (1 Sam 2:30).

verses.flow: The rich and the poor meet together; YHWH is the maker of them all.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The juxtaposition of rich and poor under one Creator undercuts social stratification. Both are equally creatures of YHWH, making economic exploitation a theological offense. TYPOLOGY: Paul draws on this egalitarian creational logic in Gal 3:28 and Col 3:11, grounding human equality in Christ the Creator.

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb niphal perfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb niphal perfect 3rd common plur

verses.flow: A prudent man sees danger and hides himself; but the simple pass on and are punished.
The reward of humility (anavah) and the fear (yirah) of YHWH is riches, and honor, and life.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Anavah" (H6038) is the noun form of humble or lowly — a deep inward posture of dependence, not mere social deference. "Yirah" (H3374, fear or reverence) is the foundational disposition of Proverbs (1:7). THEOLOGICAL: The triad of riches, honor, and life are the covenantal blessings promised to covenant fidelity (Deut 28:1-14), here obtained through the inner posture of humility.

verses.flow: Thorns and snares are in the way of the crooked; whoever guards his soul will keep far from them.
Train up a youth (naar) in the way he should go; even when he is old he will not depart from it.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Chanok" (H2596, train or dedicate) shares the root with Hanukkah — it carries the sense of initiating or dedicating something to its proper use. "Naar" (H5288) spans from infant to young man. THEOLOGICAL: The verse is not a guarantee formula but a wisdom principle: formation in covenant ways during the formative years creates a deep moral grain that persists. TYPOLOGY: Christ himself is described as growing in wisdom in precisely the formative pattern this verse envisions (Luke 2:52).

verses.flow: The rich rules over the poor, and the borrower is servant to the lender.
Whoever sows injustice will reap calamity, and the rod of his fury will fail.
A good eye (ayin tovah) — he shall be blessed, for he gives of his bread to the poor.
