verses.note: HEBREW: "Ayin tovah" (good eye) is the Hebrew idiom for generosity, the opposite of the evil eye (ayin ra'ah) which denotes miserliness (cf. Prov 23:6). The verse grounds benevolent giving in a disposition of seeing the world with abundance rather than scarcity. TYPOLOGY: Jesus employs the identical idiom in Matt 6:22-23, contrasting the good eye and evil eye in his teaching on wealth and kingdom generosity.

words.g: to drive out from a possession

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Drive out a scoffer and strife will go out, and quarreling and dishonor will cease.
He who loves purity of heart, and whose lips are gracious — the king is his friend.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: The eyes of YHWH keep watch over knowledge, but he overthrows the words of the treacherous.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The eyes of YHWH as guardian of knowledge evokes the divine council's supervisory role over truth and speech in the earth. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The image of YHWH watching and overturning false speech connects to the council's judicial function seen in Ps 82, where cosmic justice is administered against those who distort truth.

verses.flow: The sluggard says, "There is a lion outside! I shall be killed in the midst of the streets!"
The mouth of forbidden women (zarot) is a deep pit; he with whom YHWH is angry will fall into it.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Zarot" (H2114 fem. pl.) means strange or foreign women, used throughout Proverbs for the seductive figure who leads away from covenant wisdom. The pit (shuchah, H7745) is a trapping pit — language evoking the underworld and death. THEOLOGICAL: Falling into this pit is connected to divine anger, framing sexual immorality as a covenant violation with judicial consequence rather than merely a social problem.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Folly is bound up in the heart of a youth (naar); the rod of discipline will drive it far from him.
Whoever oppresses the poor to increase his own wealth, and whoever gives to the rich — only to want.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Incline your ear and hear the words of the wise, and apply your heart to my knowledge.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: For it is pleasant when you keep them within your belly, when all of them are ready on your lips.

words.m: verb hiphil perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: So that your trust may be in YHWH, I have made them known to you today — even to you.
Have I not written for you thirty sayings of counsel and knowledge,
verses.note: HEBREW: The word translated "thirty" (shloshim) is significant — many scholars identify verses 17-24:22 as the "Words of the Wise," structurally parallel to the Egyptian Wisdom of Amenemope (which also has thirty chapters). HISTORICAL: This parallel to Amenemope is one of the most discussed ANE parallels in wisdom literature, suggesting Solomonic scribes engaged with and reframed international wisdom through a Yahwistic theological lens.

words.m: preposition + verb hiphil infinitive construct + suffix
preposition + verb qal participle active + suffix

verses.flow: to make you know the certainty of true words, that you may give back words of truth to those who sent you?
Do not rob the poor because he is poor, and do not crush the afflicted at the gate;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The gate (sha'ar) was the locus of legal proceedings and commerce in ancient Israel. Oppression "at the gate" means exploiting legal and commercial systems to defraud the vulnerable — a systemic, institutionalized injustice. HISTORICAL: Prophetic literature repeatedly condemns this practice (Amos 5:12, Isa 29:21), indicating it was a persistent social pathology in the monarchy period.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: for YHWH will plead their cause and despoil (qaba') the life of those who despoil them.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Qaba'" (H6906) means to rob or plunder — YHWH uses the very action done against the poor as the measure of his own retributive action against oppressors. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH is cast as the go'el (kinsman-redeemer) of the poor, the one who pleads their legal case (rib, H7379). This is a profound statement of divine advocacy for the socially powerless.

verses.flow: Make no friendship with a man given to anger, and with a wrathful man do not go,

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: lest you learn his ways and take a snare for your soul.
Be not one of those who strikes hands in pledge, who puts up security for debts.
If you have nothing with which to pay, why should your bed be taken from under you?
Do not move an ancient boundary stone (gevul olam) that your fathers have set.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Gevul olam" (ancient boundary) refers to the stone markers delineating ancestral land allotments. Moving them was both theft and a desecration of the tribal inheritance structure established by YHWH's division of the land (Deut 19:14, 27:17). THEOLOGICAL: Land in Israel was not merely property but covenantal inheritance — to violate boundaries was to assault the order YHWH built into the nation's social fabric.

verses.flow: Do you see a man skilled in his work? He will stand before kings; he will not stand before obscure men.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The skilled craftsman who stands before kings evokes the artisan Bezalel (Exod 31:3), who was filled with the Spirit of God for skilled work. THEOLOGICAL: The verse closes the chapter by affirming that excellence of work — diligence expressed in craft — is itself a path of dignity and royal access, underscoring that the fear of YHWH, which produces wisdom, has tangible vocational fruit.

verses.flow: When you sit down to eat with a ruler, consider carefully what is before you,

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: and put a knife to your throat if you are a man given to appetite.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Put a knife to your throat" is a vivid hyperbolic idiom in Hebrew (שִׂים סַכִּין בְּלֹעֶךָ, sim sakkin be-lo'eka), warning against self-destructive appetite. "Given to appetite" (ba'al nefesh) literally means "master of soul/desire," using nefesh (H5315) in its sense of craving or appetite. The instruction to restrain oneself physically dramatizes the spiritual danger of uncontrolled desire.

verses.flow: Do not desire his delicacies, for it is bread of deceptions.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Bread of deceptions" (lechem kezavim) echoes the wisdom tradition's sustained contrast between genuine nourishment and illusory gain. The ruler's table may appear hospitable, but the food carries a hidden cost — obligation, manipulation, or moral compromise. Proverbs consistently warns that what appears pleasant can be spiritually lethal (cf. Prov 9:17).

verses.flow: Do not toil to acquire wealth; cease from your own understanding.

words.m: particle + verb qal imperfect 2nd masc sing
particle + verb hiphil imperfect 2nd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Will you set your eyes upon it? It is gone! For surely it makes itself wings like an eagle and flies toward the heavens.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb translated "set your eyes upon" (תָּעוּף עֵינֶיךָ, ta'uph eyneycha) literally means "will your eyes fly upon it?" — a rhetorical question implying the futility of fixating on wealth. The eagle imagery reinforces the speed and finality of wealth's disappearance (cf. Prov 27:24). THEOLOGICAL: Wealth is not evil in Proverbs, but trust in wealth is consistently condemned as a form of misplaced faith.

verses.flow: Do not eat the bread of a man who is evil of eye, and do not desire his delicacies,

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: for as one who calculates within himself, so is he. "Eat and drink," he says to you, but his heart is not with you.
verses.note: HEBREW: The key phrase "as one who calculates within himself" reflects the Hebrew שָׁעַר בְּנַפְשׁוֹ (sha'ar be-nafsho), meaning "to reckon or estimate in his soul/self." Some manuscripts and the LXX read this differently, suggesting "for as he thinks in his heart, so is he" — a reading that has become proverbial in its own right and is close to the Hebrew sense. The host's outward generosity masks inward calculation.

verses.flow: The morsel you have eaten you will vomit up, and you will waste your pleasant words.
Do not speak in the ears of a fool, for he will despise the wisdom of your words.
Do not remove an ancient landmark, and into the fields of the fatherless do not enter,
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Ancient Near Eastern law codes (including the Code of Hammurabi and Egyptian boundary stele inscriptions) treated landmark removal as a serious civic and religious offense. In Israel, the landmark (gevul) defined tribal and family inheritance granted by YHWH (Deut 19:14; 27:17). Moving it was tantamount to stealing the divine allotment of land. THEOLOGICAL: The joining of landmark protection with care for the fatherless reflects the covenant's concern for those without a human defender.

words.g: to be the next of kin

verses.flow: for their Redeemer (Go'el) is strong; He will plead their cause against you.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Redeemer" here is Go'el (גֹּאֵל, H1350), the kinsman-redeemer who holds legal responsibility to restore what has been taken from a vulnerable family member. THEOLOGICAL: Applying this title to YHWH (cf. Job 19:25; Isa 41:14) declares that He serves as the ultimate Go'el of the defenseless. TYPOLOGY: The Go'el institution anticipates Christ, who as the incarnate kinsman-redeemer reclaims what was lost to sin and death (Ruth 4; Gal 4:4-5).

words.m: verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Bring your heart to discipline (musar), and your ears to words of knowledge.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Do not withhold discipline (musar) from a youth; if you strike him with the rod, he will not die.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

words.g: from Hades or the world of the dead

verses.flow: You shall strike him with the rod, and his soul you shall deliver from Sheol.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Deliver his soul from Sheol" does not mean corporal punishment prevents literal death, but that discipline rescues a child from the path that leads to destruction. Sheol (שְׁאוֹל, H7585) in Proverbs represents the realm of death and the ultimate end of the foolish way. Proper discipline is therefore an act of redemptive love, not cruelty (cf. Prov 13:24; Heb 12:9-11).

verses.flow: My son, if your heart is wise, my heart also will rejoice.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd fem plur

verses.flow: And my innermost being (kelayot) will exult when your lips speak what is upright.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Innermost being" translates kelayot (כְּלָיוֹת, H3629), literally "kidneys," which in Hebrew psychology served as the seat of the deepest emotions and moral sensibility, analogous to how English uses "heart." The plural form emphasizes the intensity of the joy. The teacher's moral investment in the student is total — not merely intellectual but visceral and deeply personal.

verses.flow: Let not your heart envy sinners, but rather be in the fear of YHWH all the day.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "The fear of YHWH" (yir'at YHWH) is the controlling theme of the entire book (Prov 1:7; 9:10; 31:30). Set in contrast to envy of sinners, it reorients the student's perspective from horizontal comparison to vertical orientation. The phrase "all the day" (kol ha-yom) stresses the comprehensive, uninterrupted character of covenant devotion — not episodic religion but sustained posture of the soul.

verses.flow: For surely there is a future (acharit), and your hope will not be cut off.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Future" translates acharit (אַחֲרִית, H319), meaning "latter end, outcome, posterity." It carries eschatological overtones, pointing to what ultimately remains when the present moment fades. THEOLOGICAL: This verse functions as a counter-argument to envy: the sinner's apparent prosperity has no acharit, but the righteous possess a hope (tiqvah, H8615) that is indestructible. (cf. Prov 24:14; Jer 29:11).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing
conjunction + verb piel imperative 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Hear, my son, and be wise, and direct your heart in the way.
Do not be among those who drink wine to excess, among those who are gluttonous with meat,
for the drunkard and the glutton will come to poverty, and slumber will clothe a man with rags.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Listen to your father who begot you, and do not despise your mother when she is old.
Buy truth and do not sell it — wisdom and discipline (musar) and understanding.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The command to "buy truth" (emet, אֱמֶת, H571) and "not sell it" frames wisdom as a treasure requiring costly acquisition and faithful retention. The triad of wisdom (chokmah), discipline (musar), and understanding (binah) recapitulates the book's core vocabulary of moral formation. Truth here is not merely propositional but relational and covenantal — YHWH's own character expressed in reliable, enduring instruction.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: The father of a righteous man will greatly rejoice, and he who begets a wise son will be glad in him.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal jussive 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: Let your father and your mother rejoice, and let her who bore you be glad.

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Give me your heart, my son, and let your eyes observe my ways,
for a prostitute is a deep pit, and a foreign woman is a narrow well.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "foreign woman" (ishah zarah, H2114) is a recurring figure in Proverbs 1-9 and here reappears as a "narrow well" — imagery evoking concealment, entrapment, and false promise of refreshment. Her seductive appeal is contrasted throughout the book with the open, life-giving invitation of Lady Wisdom (Prov 9:1-6). TYPOLOGY: The contrast between the two women anticipates the apocalyptic contrast between the Whore of Babylon and the Bride of Christ (Rev 17-19; 21).

words.g: in/with ruddy i.e. a human being

verses.flow: She too lies in wait like a robber, and increases the treacherous among men.
Who has woe? Who has sorrow? Who has contentions? Who has complaints? Who has wounds without cause? Who has redness of eyes?
verses.note: STRUCTURE: This verse launches one of the most rhetorically elaborate passages in all of Proverbs (vv. 29-35), structured as a dramatic poem on the devastation of drunkenness. The six rhetorical questions (anaphora) build an indictment through accumulation: woe, sorrow, contention, complaint, causeless wounds, and bloodshot eyes — a comprehensive portrait of the drunkard's life. This literary artistry underscores Proverbs' use of beauty itself as a carrier of moral formation.

verses.flow: Those who linger over wine, those who go to taste mixed wine.
Do not look at wine when it is red, when it sparkles in the cup, when it goes down smoothly.
At the last it bites like a serpent, and stings like an adder.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Bites like a serpent" (nachash, נָחָשׁ, H5175) and "stings like an adder" (tziph'oni, צִפְעֹנִי, H6848) use two distinct Hebrew snake terms to intensify the image of delayed but lethal consequence. The serpent connection is theologically resonant, recalling the Edenic deceiver whose appeal was pleasant to the eyes but deadly in its effect (Gen 3:1-6). Wine's seduction follows the same ancient pattern of beauty concealing destruction.

verses.flow: Your eyes will see strange things, and your heart will speak perverse things.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc sing

words.g: a sea or large body of water

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb qal participle active

verses.flow: And you will be like one who lies down in the heart of the sea, or like one who lies down on the top of the mast.

words.m: verb hiphil perfect 3rd common plur + suffix
verb qal perfect 3rd common plur + suffix
verb piel imperfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: "They struck me, but I was not hurt; they beat me, but I did not feel it. When shall I awake? I will seek it yet again."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The closing words of the drunkard ("I will seek it yet again") are among the most chilling in all of Proverbs — a portrait of addiction's self-defeating cycle rendered in the drunkard's own voice. The chapter thus ends not with an exhortation but with a dramatic monologue, allowing folly to condemn itself. This rhetorical strategy — letting the fool speak — underscores Proverbs' conviction that wisdom and folly each reveal themselves by their fruits.

verses.flow: Do not be envious of evil men, and do not desire to be with them,
for their heart meditates on violence, and their lips speak of trouble.
By wisdom a house is built, and by understanding it is established;
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The triad of wisdom (chokmah), understanding (binah), and knowledge (da'at) in vv. 3-4 mirrors the divine attributes by which YHWH founded the earth (Prov 3:19-20). This construction imagery anticipates the NT portrait of Christ as the master builder through whom all things were created (Col 1:16-17; 1 Cor 3:10-11). The 'house' language connects to the household of God theme in Hebrews (Heb 3:3-6).

words.g: to fill or be full of

verses.flow: and by knowledge the rooms are filled with all precious and pleasant riches.
A wise man is strong (gevar), and a man of knowledge increases strength (koach);
verses.note: HEBREW: The opening phrase is debated; the MT reads 'a wise man is strong (be'oz)' — some manuscripts and LXX read 'a wise man is better than a strong man.' The word gevar (man, warrior) derives from the root gavar, meaning to prevail or be strong, adding a martial nuance. koach (strength) refers to raw physical or vital power. The combination positions wisdom as a superior form of military might.

verses.flow: for by wise guidance you wage your war, and in an abundance of counselors there is victory.
Wisdom is too high (ramot) for a fool; in the gate he does not open his mouth.
verses.note: HEBREW: ramot is plural, possibly an abstract plural meaning 'heights' or 'lofty things.' The phrase 'in the gate' refers to the city gate as the civic and judicial center of ancient Israelite life, where elders and leaders convened to settle disputes and conduct public affairs (Ruth 4:1-2; Amos 5:12). The fool has no voice in such councils because he lacks wisdom.

verses.flow: Whoever devises evil will be called a master of schemes.

words.g: to/for ruddy i.e. a human being

verses.flow: The devising of folly is sin, and the scoffer is an abomination to mankind.
If you faint in the day of adversity, your strength is small.
Deliver those who are being taken away to death, and those who are stumbling to the slaughter — hold them back.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse issues a remarkable ethical imperative to intervene on behalf of those unjustly condemned to death. It anticipates the prophetic call to defend the vulnerable (Isa 1:17; Jer 22:3) and finds its ultimate expression in Christ's own work of redemption — delivering humanity from the slaughter of sin and death (Heb 2:14-15). The verse has been applied in Christian tradition to advocacy against all unjust killing.

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

words.g: to/for ruddy i.e. a human being

verses.flow: If you say, "Behold, we did not know this," does not he who weighs the heart perceive it? And he who keeps your soul, does he not know it? And will he not repay man according to his work?
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The description of YHWH as the one who 'weighs the heart' (tochen libot) is a striking affirmation of divine omniscience and moral accountability. The verb tachan (to weigh, examine) appears in Prov 21:2 as well, where YHWH weighs the heart against human self-justification. This anticipates Paul's warning that God will judge the hidden things of men (Rom 2:16) and the NT affirmation that nothing is hidden from God (Heb 4:13).

words.g: and a dripping i.e. of honey

verses.flow: Eat honey (devash), my son, for it is good, and the drippings of the honeycomb are sweet to your palate;

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: so know that wisdom is to your soul: if you find it, there is a future (acharit), and your hope will not be cut off.
verses.note: HEBREW: acharit (future, end, latter portion) is a key wisdom term denoting the ultimate outcome or destiny of a person's life. It appears in contrast to the 'no acharit' of the wicked in v. 20, forming a deliberate literary antithesis. The same term is used in messianic contexts (Jer 29:11; Num 24:14) to denote the eschatological hope. Finding wisdom thus carries the weight of securing one's ultimate destiny.

words.g: a couch or place of repose

verses.flow: Do not lie in wait, O wicked man, against the dwelling of the righteous; do not plunder his resting place;

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: for the righteous man falls seven times and rises again, but the wicked stumble in calamity.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The 'seven times' of the righteous man's falling is a Hebraic idiom for completeness — the righteous may fall repeatedly and completely, yet YHWH restores them. This is the ground of perseverance theology. The contrast with the wicked who 'stumble in calamity' without recovery reflects the two-ways theme running through Proverbs and the Psalms (Ps 1:6). NT parallel: the just shall live by faith, enduring through all falls (Rom 1:17; Heb 10:38).

words.g: and + in/with to totter or waver
words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb niphal infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: Do not rejoice when your enemy falls, and let not your heart be glad when he stumbles,

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb hiphil sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: lest YHWH see it and it displease him, and he turn away his wrath from your enemy.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The notion that YHWH may 'turn away his wrath' from the enemy as a consequence of the righteous man's improper gloating is significant: it reveals YHWH's sovereign superintendence of retributive processes, which are not mechanistic but relational and responsive. This is consistent with the prophetic picture of YHWH deliberating within his divine council over the fates of nations (1 Kgs 22:19-23; Jer 23:18).

verses.flow: Do not fret because of evildoers, and do not be envious of the wicked,
for there will be no future (acharit) for the evil man; the lamp of the wicked will be put out.
verses.note: HEBREW: The 'lamp' (ner) of the wicked being extinguished is a recurring death and judgment metaphor in Proverbs (13:9; 20:20) and Job (18:5-6). In the ANE, the burning lamp in a household symbolized life, prosperity, and dynastic continuity. Its extinguishing therefore denotes not merely physical death but the total cessation of one's lineage and legacy.

verses.flow: Fear YHWH, my son, and the king, and do not join with those who do otherwise (shonim),
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The pairing of YHWH and the human king as dual objects of fear reflects the covenantal and political theology of ancient Israel, in which the king was YHWH's vicegerent (Ps 2:6-7; Rom 13:1-7). The term shonim (those who are different, who change, who do otherwise) may refer to political revolutionaries or religious apostates — those who agitate against established divine and human order.

verses.flow: for disaster from them rises suddenly, and who knows the ruin that will come from both of them?
These also are sayings of the wise: Partiality in judgment is not good.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix
verb qal imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: Whoever says to the wicked, "You are righteous" — peoples will curse him, nations will denounce him;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The public declaration of the wicked as righteous constitutes a perversion of justice that brings communal curse — notably the curse falls not merely on the individual judge but is acknowledged by 'peoples' and 'nations,' indicating a universal moral order. This universalism in Proverbs reflects the creation-order theology by which wisdom governs all humanity, not Israel alone (Prov 8:15-16). Cf. Isa 5:20's woe on those who call evil good.

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb hiphil participle active

words.g: in/with good in the widest sense

verses.flow: but those who rebuke the wicked will have delight, and a good blessing will come upon them.
Whoever gives an honest answer kisses the lips.
verses.note: HEBREW: The image of 'kissing the lips' in response to an honest answer is culturally rich — the kiss in the ANE could signify honor, covenant ratification, and intimate loyalty (cf. Ps 2:12). The verse thus elevates truthful speech to the level of a covenant act of love, connecting it to the hesed (loyal-love) ethic that pervades wisdom literature. An honest word is an act of deep relational fidelity.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Prepare your work outside; make it ready for yourself in the field, and afterward build your house.

words.m: conjunction + particle + verb piel perfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Do not be a witness against your neighbor without cause, and do not deceive with your lips.
Do not say, "I will do to him as he has done to me; I will repay the man according to his work."
I passed by the field of a sluggard (atsel), and by the vineyard of a man lacking heart (lev);
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The closing poem (vv. 30-34) is a first-person experiential observation (mashal), a form well-attested in Egyptian wisdom literature such as the Instruction of Amenemope. The use of empirical observation as a vehicle for moral instruction is characteristic of the international wisdom tradition that Israel shared and transformed under YHWH's revelation. The field and vineyard were the primary economic units of subsistence agriculture in ancient Canaan.

words.g: to pull down or in pieces

verses.flow: and behold, it was all overgrown with thorns; its surface was covered with nettles, and its stone wall was broken down.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: Then I saw and considered it; I looked and received instruction (musar).
verses.note: HEBREW: musar (instruction, discipline, correction) is one of the central terms of Proverbs, appearing in the book's programmatic statement (1:2-3). It implies not mere intellectual learning but formative correction that shapes character. The sage's observation of the sluggard's field becomes an act of moral formation — wisdom is acquired not only from texts but from attentive perception of the created order (cf. Prov 6:6-11).

verses.flow: A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest,

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: and poverty will come upon you like a robber, and want like an armed man.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The repetition of this couplet from Prov 6:10-11 functions as a refrain, sealing the instruction with rhetorical emphasis. The 'armed man' (ish magen — lit. man of a shield/weapon) portrays poverty as an aggressive, unavoidable assailant — not a passive condition but an active consequence of sloth. In the NT, spiritual sloth is similarly condemned as an opening for spiritual adversity (1 Pet 5:8; Rom 13:11-14).

verses.flow: These also are proverbs of Solomon, which the men of Hezekiah king of Judah copied out.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "men of Hezekiah" (c. 715–686 BC) represent a royal scribal guild who compiled and transmitted Solomonic wisdom, attesting to the literary activity of Hezekiah's court. This editorial note provides a rare window into the canonical process of Proverbs, placing this collection (chs. 25–29) among the later-assembled Solomonic materials. Hezekiah's scribal reforms parallel his broader religious renewals described in 2 Kings 18–20.

verses.flow: It is the glory of God to conceal a matter, but the glory of kings is to search out a matter.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between divine concealment and royal inquiry reflects a theology of hiddenness — God's glory is manifest in mystery (cf. Deut 29:29), while kings bear the image-bearing vocation to govern through wisdom and investigation. HEBREW: The word for "glory" (kabod, H3519) carries the sense of weighty honor and splendor. This verse establishes an epistemological asymmetry between Creator and creature that runs throughout wisdom literature.

verses.flow: As the heavens for height, and the earth for depth, so the heart of kings is unsearchable.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Take away the dross from silver, and a vessel comes forth for the refiner;

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: take away the wicked from before the king, and his throne will be established in righteousness (tsedeq).
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The removal of the wicked and the establishment of the throne in tsedeq (righteousness, H6664) anticipates the Messianic King whose throne is founded on justice and righteousness (Isa 9:7; Ps 45:6). HEBREW: Tsedeq denotes conformity to a standard — here, the covenant standard of YHWH. The purification motif (vv. 4–5) echoes priestly and royal purification themes fulfilled in Christ the King-Priest.

verses.flow: Do not exalt yourself in the presence of the king, and do not stand in the place of great men;

words.m: preposition + verb hiphil infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: for it is better that it be said to you, "Come up here," than that you be put lower in the presence of a noble whom your eyes have seen.
Do not go out hastily to contend, lest you know not what to do in the end of it, when your neighbor puts you to shame.
Argue your cause with your neighbor himself, and do not disclose the secret of another;

words.m: verb piel imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: lest he who hears it bring shame upon you, and your ill repute have no end.
A word fitly spoken is like apples of gold in a setting of silver.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "apples of gold in a setting of silver" uses the word maskiyyot (H4906), meaning "carved work" or "setting/figure," perhaps referring to filigree lattice-work. The image evokes exquisite craftsmanship applied to speech, honoring the power of the rightly timed word. THEOLOGICAL: This verse reflects the broader wisdom conviction that speech is a moral and aesthetic act, not merely functional communication (cf. Prov 15:23; Col 4:6).

verses.flow: Like a gold ring or an ornament of gold is a wise reprover to a listening ear.

words.m: preposition + verb qal participle active + suffix

verses.flow: Like the cold of snow in the time of harvest is a faithful messenger to those who send him; he refreshes the soul (nephesh) of his masters.
verses.note: HEBREW: Nephesh (H5315) here means the animating life-force or inner being — the messenger's faithfulness literally revives the vitality of those he serves. HISTORICAL: "Snow in time of harvest" likely refers to cold water brought from snowy mountain sources (e.g., Lebanon), used to cool drinks, since snow itself would be unusual at harvest in lowland Canaan. The image stresses the refreshing relief that reliability brings.

verses.flow: Like clouds and wind without rain is a man who boasts of a gift he does not give.
With patience a ruler may be persuaded, and a soft tongue will break a bone.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The paradox of a "soft tongue" breaking a bone captures wisdom's counter-intuitive power — patient, measured speech overcomes resistance that force cannot. HEBREW: "Patience" renders the Hebrew arek appayim (lit. "long of nose/nostrils"), the idiom for slow anger, the same quality attributed to YHWH in His covenant self-disclosure (Exod 34:6). The proverb thus models divine patience as the pattern for human persuasion.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 2nd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb hiphil perfect 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: If you have found honey, eat only enough for you, lest you be sated with it and vomit it.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix
conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Let your foot be seldom in your neighbor's house, lest he become weary of you and hate you.
A man who bears false witness against his neighbor is like a war club, and a sword, and a sharp arrow.
Like a bad tooth or an unsteady foot is confidence in a treacherous man in a time of trouble.
Whoever sings songs to a heavy heart is like one who takes off a garment on a cold day, like vinegar poured upon a wound.

words.m: verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix
verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: If your enemy is hungry, give him bread to eat, and if he is thirsty, give him water to drink;
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: Verses 21–22 are directly quoted by Paul in Romans 12:20, grounding the ethic of enemy-love in the wisdom tradition of Israel. The instruction to feed and water one's enemy anticipates Jesus's teaching in the Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5:44) and demonstrates that the ethics of the New Covenant are rooted in the Old Testament wisdom of YHWH.

verses.flow: for you will heap burning coals on his head, and YHWH will reward you.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Heaping burning coals" (geḥalim, H1513) upon an enemy's head is debated — most likely it refers to producing in the enemy a burning sense of shame and conviction that leads to repentance, rather than a curse of destruction. HEBREW: The reward (shillam, from shalam, H7999) that YHWH provides links enemy-love to covenant faithfulness — YHWH himself is the guarantor and rewarder of such costly grace.

verses.flow: The north wind brings forth rain, and a backbiting tongue brings forth an angry face.
It is better to dwell in a corner of a housetop than in a house shared with a quarrelsome wife.
Like cold water to a thirsty soul (nephesh) is good news from a far country.
verses.note: HEBREW: Nephesh here (H5315) emphasizes the deep interior thirst of the whole person, not merely physical dehydration. The simile of cold water elevates good news (shemu'ah tovah) to a life-sustaining category. THEOLOGICAL: The proverb reflects Israel's theology of the spoken word as life-giving — an anticipation of the Gospel as "good news" (euangelion) refreshing those alienated and far off (Eph 2:17; Isa 52:7).

verses.flow: Like a muddied spring or a polluted fountain is a righteous man who gives way before the wicked.
It is not good to eat much honey, nor is it glory to seek one's own glory.

words.g: in/with a city in the widest sense

verses.flow: A man without self-control is like a city broken into and left without walls.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The image of a city without walls (without defenses) applied to a man without self-control (ruach, H7307 — lit. "no restraint over his spirit") frames personal virtue in political-military terms. HEBREW: The word motzar (H4691), "restraint" or "siege-work," reinforces the defensive imagery. TYPOLOGY: The Spirit-controlled life (Gal 5:22–23) fulfills what the wisdom tradition here demands — self-mastery as the wall that preserves the integrity of the soul.

verses.flow: Like snow in summer and like rain in harvest, so honor is not fitting for a fool.
Like a fluttering sparrow, like a darting swallow, so a curse without cause does not alight.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb "alight" (נוּד, nud, Strong's H5110) carries the sense of a bird landing or settling. The proverb affirms that an undeserved curse — one without moral or covenantal grounding — has no power to take effect. This reflects the Hebrew understanding that words of cursing operate within a moral-causal order governed by YHWH, not by arbitrary magic.

verses.flow: A whip for the horse, a bridle for the donkey, and a rod for the back of fools.
Do not answer a fool according to his folly, lest you also become like him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Verses 4 and 5 form a famous antithetical pair that has puzzled interpreters from the Talmud onward. The apparent contradiction is intentional — a literary device (antithesis) that demands wisdom in discernment. The reader must judge the situation: sometimes silence is wisdom, sometimes engagement is necessary. This mirrors the broader Wisdom literature's call for situational discernment (hokhmah, חָכְמָה) rather than mechanical rule-following.

verses.flow: Answer a fool according to his folly, lest he become wise in his own eyes.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The tension between v.4 and v.5 is pedagogically deliberate. The Book of Proverbs as a whole teaches that no single principle applies universally without wisdom. James 1:5 urges believers to ask YHWH for this very wisdom to navigate such tensions. The fool's self-deception is the greater danger — silence that allows it to go unchallenged can be its own form of harm.

verses.flow: He cuts off his own feet, he drinks down violence — one who sends a message by the hand of a fool.
Like the legs of a lame man that hang useless, so is a proverb in the mouth of fools.
Like one who binds a stone in a sling, so is he who gives honor to a fool.
Like a thorn that goes up into the hand of a drunkard, so is a proverb in the mouth of fools.
Like an archer who wounds everyone, so is he who hires a fool or hires those passing by.
Like a dog that returns to his vomit, so a fool who repeats his folly.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: This verse is quoted directly in 2 Peter 2:22, where Peter applies it to false teachers who return to their former sinful patterns after initial profession of the truth. The image of the dog returning to vomit underscores the degeneracy of apostasy from the covenant community. The Greek Septuagint rendering closely follows the Hebrew, confirming the textual stability of this proverb.

verses.flow: Do you see a man who is wise in his own eyes? There is more hope for a fool than for him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Self-conceit (being "wise in one's own eyes") is identified throughout Proverbs as a form of spiritual blindness more dangerous than outright foolishness. The phrase "wise in his own eyes" (חָכָם בְּעֵינָיו, hakham b'einav) echoes the warning of Proverbs 3:7 ("Be not wise in your own eyes") and is connected to the primal sin of Genesis 3, where the forbidden fruit was perceived as desirable "to make one wise." Pride masquerading as wisdom is the deepest folly.

verses.flow: The sluggard says, "There is a lion in the road! A lion is in the open square!"
As a door turns on its hinges, so a sluggard turns on his bed.

words.m: preposition + verb hiphil infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: The sluggard buries his hand in the dish; it wearies him to bring it back to his mouth.
The sluggard is wiser in his own eyes than seven men who answer with discretion.
Like one who seizes a dog by the ears is he who passes by and meddles in a quarrel not his own.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The image of seizing a dog by the ears reflects daily life in the ancient Near East, where dogs were typically semi-wild scavengers, not domesticated pets. To grab such an animal by the ears was to invite an immediate and vicious bite. The proverb warns against unnecessary intervention in disputes belonging to others — meddling that invites personal injury without gain.

verses.flow: Like a madman who throws firebrands, arrows, and death,

words.m: conjunction + verb qal perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: so is the man who deceives his neighbor and says, "I was only joking!"
For lack of wood a fire goes out, and where there is no whisperer, strife ceases.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The proverb highlights the causal power of speech in the social fabric. "Whisperer" (נִרְגָּן, nirgan, Strong's H5372) denotes a slanderer or gossip who fans the flames of social conflict. The metaphor of fire and fuel maps precisely: remove the fuel (the whisperer) and the fire (strife) extinguishes itself. This principle is foundational to the New Testament ethics of speech (Eph 4:29-31; James 3:1-12).

verses.flow: As charcoal to burning coals and wood to fire, so is a contentious man for kindling strife.
The words of a whisperer are like delicious morsels; they go down into the inner chambers of the belly.
Like silver dross overlaid on earthenware are burning lips and a wicked heart.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "silver dross" (סִיגִים כֶּסֶף, sigim keseph) refers to the impure slag skimmed off molten metal — a worthless residue given the appearance of value when applied as a glaze over cheap pottery. The image powerfully captures hypocrisy: beautiful exterior language ("burning lips") concealing a morally corrupt interior ("wicked heart"). The LXX reads slightly differently, but the MT's metallurgical metaphor is striking and coherent.

verses.flow: An enemy dissembles with his lips and harbors deceit within himself;
when his voice is gracious, do not believe him, for seven abominations are in his heart.
Though his hatred is covered with deception, his wickedness will be exposed in the assembly.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Assembly" (קָהָל, qahal, Strong's H6951) carries covenantal weight — it is the same word used for the gathered assembly of Israel before YHWH. The proverb implies that no deception ultimately conceals itself; exposure comes in the public assembly, anticipating the eschatological judgment where all things hidden are revealed (Luke 12:2-3; 1 Cor 4:5).

verses.flow: Whoever digs a pit will fall into it, and he who rolls a stone, it will return upon him.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The principle of retributive justice embedded in this proverb — the evil one devises falling upon himself — echoes throughout Scripture: Haman hanged on his own gallows (Esther 7:10), the Psalmist's cry that the wicked fall into their own pit (Ps 7:15-16), and ultimately the cross, where the powers of darkness overreached and were defeated by the very death they orchestrated (Col 2:14-15). The moral order of creation bends toward justice.

verses.flow: A lying tongue hates those it crushes, and a flattering mouth works ruin.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Crushes" (דַּכָּיו, dakkav) carries the sense of those who are bruised, crushed, or brought low — the victims of the liar's manipulations. The proverb closes the chapter on the theme of destructive speech, forming an inclusio with the earlier warnings about the whisperer and the flatterer. "Flattering mouth" (פֶּה חָלָק, peh halaq, lit. "smooth mouth") refers to speech designed to manipulate through pleasing words, contrasted with truthful speech throughout Proverbs.

verses.flow: Do not boast about tomorrow, for you do not know what a day may bring forth.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The prohibition on boasting about tomorrow reflects the wisdom tradition's affirmation of divine sovereignty over time and human contingency. The phrase "what a day may bring forth" (Hebrew: yeled yom, lit. "what the day births") acknowledges that events are in YHWH's hand, not man's (cf. James 4:13-15, which echoes this proverb explicitly). This verse forms part of Proverbs' broader polemic against self-reliance and presumption.

words.m: verb piel jussive 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Let another praise you, and not your own mouth; a stranger, and not your own lips.
A stone is heavy, and sand is weighty, but a fool's provocation is heavier than both.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word rendered "provocation" is ka'as (Strong's H3708), meaning vexation, grief, or anger-inducing aggravation. The comparative structure intensifies the point: physical burdens (stone, sand) are manageable, but the emotional and social weight of a fool's chronic irritation surpasses them. The saying reflects ancient Israel's social realism about the communal damage caused by fools.

verses.flow: Wrath is cruel, and anger is overwhelming, but who is able to stand before jealousy?
Better is open rebuke than hidden love (ahavah).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Hidden love" (ahavah setorah) — ahavah (Strong's H160) is the standard Hebrew word for love; setorah (from satar, to conceal) modifies it. The contrast is striking: open (galu) rebuke is better than a love that conceals itself and never corrects. True covenant-love (hesed) speaks the truth even when painful (cf. Gal 6:1).

verses.flow: Faithful are the wounds of a friend, but the kisses of an enemy are profuse.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The paradox of "faithful wounds" (ne'emanim patze'e ohev) — the wounds of a friend who loves are trustworthy, i.e., they serve genuine good. The "profuse kisses" (ata'rot) of an enemy are excessive and deceptive. This verse anticipates the Judas typology: betrayal comes through a kiss (Matt 26:48-49), while true friendship bears painful honesty.

verses.flow: A sated soul (nephesh) treads underfoot a honeycomb, but to a hungry soul every bitter thing is sweet.
verses.note: HEBREW: Nephesh (Strong's H5315) here means "soul" in its appetitive, embodied sense — the whole desiring self. The verse turns on the relativity of satisfaction: fullness destroys appetite even for what is excellent (the honeycomb, symbol of finest sweetness), while hunger transforms even bitterness into sweetness. Spiritually, this warns against spiritual satiation that makes the word of YHWH seem unappealing.

verses.flow: Like a bird wandering from its nest, so is a man who wanders from his place.
Oil and incense make the heart rejoice, and the sweetness of a friend's counsel is from the counsel of the soul.
Do not forsake your friend and your father's friend, and do not go to your brother's house in the day of your calamity; better is a neighbor nearby than a brother far off.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel imperative 2nd masc sing
conjunction + verb hiphil cohortative 1st common sing

verses.flow: Be wise, my son, and make my heart glad, that I may answer him who reproaches me.
A prudent man (arum) sees danger and hides himself, but the simple pass on and are punished.

words.g: and in up to or over against

words.m: verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Take the garment of him who puts up security for a stranger, and hold it in pledge when he gives surety for a foreign woman.
Whoever blesses his neighbor with a loud voice, rising early in the morning, it will be counted as a curse to him.
A continual dripping on a day of heavy rain and a contentious woman are alike;
to restrain her is to restrain the wind, and his right hand grasps oil.

words.g: in/with to be sharp or severe
in/with to be sharp or severe

verses.flow: Iron sharpens iron, and one man sharpens the face of another.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Iron sharpens iron" (barzel be'varzel yachedh) is one of the most quoted proverbs in practical wisdom. The word yachedh (sharpens, from chadad) implies bringing to a point or edge. The second clause literally reads "a man sharpens the face (panim) of his companion" — the face being the seat of expression and engagement. True friendship and mentorship involve this productive friction (cf. Heb 10:24-25).

verses.flow: Whoever tends a fig tree will eat its fruit, and whoever guards his master will be honored.

words.g: the ruddy i.e. a human being
to/for ruddy i.e. a human being

verses.flow: As water reflects face to face, so the heart of man reflects the man.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verse uses the preposition ke ("as/like") in a comparative simile: as water-face to face, so man's heart to the man. The Hebrew is terse and has generated interpretive debate — the most natural reading is that one's inner heart (lev, Strong's H3820) reflects one's true self, just as water gives back an exact likeness. It is an affirmation of the moral transparency of the heart to those who look carefully.

words.g: Hades or the world of the dead
the ruddy i.e. a human being

verses.flow: Sheol and Abaddon are never satisfied, and never satisfied are the eyes of man.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: Sheol and Abaddon (Strong's H11) are paired cosmic realities — the realm of the dead and the place of destruction respectively. Their appearance together (cf. Job 26:6; Rev 9:11, where Abaddon names the angel of the abyss) reflects the ancient Israelite cosmology in which the underworld has a structured existence. The insatiability of death mirrors the insatiability of human desire, drawing a dark moral parallel between cosmic appetite and human covetousness.

verses.flow: The crucible is for silver, and the furnace is for gold, and a man is tested by his praise.
verses.note: HEBREW: The crucible (matzreph, Strong's H4715) and the furnace (kur, Strong's H3564) are metallurgical images for refining and testing. The third clause — "a man is tested by his praise" (ve'ish le'phi mahalalo) — is distinctive. Mahalalo means "his praise" or "what he is praised for." A man's character is revealed by what wins his approval or by how he responds to being praised. Both interpretations are valid and complementary (cf. 1 Pet 1:7).

verses.flow: Though you crush a fool in a mortar with a pestle among crushed grain, yet his folly will not depart from him.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The mortar-and-pestle image depicts the futility of attempting to reform a deeply entrenched fool by external force or discipline alone. The word for folly (ivveleth, Strong's H200) points not to mere ignorance but to moral-spiritual obduracy — the refusal to receive correction. This verse guards against naive optimism about human nature apart from divine transformation (cf. Jer 17:9).

words.g: a collective name for a flock

verses.flow: Know well the face of your flocks; set your heart to your herds,
for riches are not forever, nor does a crown endure to all generations.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The transience of both wealth ("riches are not forever," ki lo le'olam choshen) and royal power ("nor does a crown endure to all generations") frames the agricultural instructions of vv. 23-27 in a covenantal-creational context. Only diligent stewardship of what YHWH has given sustains life across time. The saying anticipates the NT's warnings about earthly treasure (Matt 6:19-21) and points toward the eternal kingdom of Messiah as the only enduring reign.

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal perfect 3rd masc sing

words.g: and to gather for any purpose
words.m: conjunction + verb niphal perfect 3rd common plur

words.g: a mountain or range of hills

verses.flow: When the grass is gone and the new growth appears, and the herbage of the mountains is gathered,
the lambs will provide your clothing, and the goats the price of a field,
and there will be enough goats' milk for your food, for the food of your household, and maintenance for your maidens.
The wicked flee when no one is pursuing, but the righteous are bold (batach) as a lion.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Bold" renders the root batach (Strong's H982), often translated "trust" or "confidence." The lion imagery (ari, H738) conveys majestic, fearless assurance. The contrast between the wicked's irrational fear and the righteous man's lion-like confidence is a hallmark of wisdom theology — inner moral integrity produces outward fearlessness. (Lev 26:17, 36)

words.g: and + in/with ruddy i.e. a human being

verses.flow: By the transgression of a land its princes are many, but by a man of understanding and knowledge, so it endures.
A poor man who oppresses the weak is a driving rain that leaves no food.
Those who forsake the Torah (instruction) praise the wicked, but those who keep the Torah strive against them.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Torah" (H8451, torah) here means not merely the Mosaic Law but divine instruction in its fullest sense. The verb translated "strive against" is yitgaru (from garah, H1624), meaning to contend, quarrel, or engage in conflict. Those who keep Torah are actively opposed to wickedness — wisdom is not passive. THEOLOGICAL: The link between Torah-keeping and opposition to the wicked reflects covenantal fidelity; departing from Torah results in moral inversion where evil is praised.

verses.flow: Evil men do not understand justice, but those who seek YHWH understand all of it.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Seek YHWH" (darash YHWH, H1875 + H3068) is a covenantal expression of wholehearted inquiry and dependence. The assertion that such seekers "understand all of it" (kol yabinu) is a bold epistemological claim: true knowledge of justice is rooted not in natural reason alone but in the fear of YHWH. (Prov 1:7, 1 Kgs 3:9)

verses.flow: Better is a poor man who walks in his integrity than he who is crooked in his ways, though he be rich.

words.g: and + and to tend a flock

verses.flow: A son who keeps the Torah is a discerning son, but he who is a companion of gluttons shames his father.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Whoever multiplies his wealth by interest and profit gathers it for him who is gracious to the poor.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The terms "interest" (neshek, H5392) and "profit" (tarbit, H8636) refer to the two forms of usury prohibited in Torah (Lev 25:36-37). Neshek ("a bite") was interest deducted in advance; tarbit was profit added at repayment. THEOLOGICAL: The reversal of ill-gotten gain — wealth flowing ultimately to the charitable — reflects the covenantal principle that YHWH governs the redistribution of justice across generations.

verses.flow: He who turns away his ear from hearing the Torah, even his prayer is an abomination.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: A startling inversion — the man who refuses to hear Torah finds even his prayers classified as an abomination (toevah, H8441), the strongest term of divine revulsion in the Hebrew Bible. This places willful rejection of divine instruction in the category of idolatry and moral perversion. TYPOLOGY: The principle finds NT resonance in Christ's teaching that those who do not forgive cannot receive forgiveness; covenant posture determines reception of mercy. (John 9:31, Jas 4:3)

words.g: in/with good in the widest sense

verses.flow: Whoever leads the upright astray in an evil way will himself fall into his own pit, but the blameless will inherit good.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: A rich man is wise in his own eyes, but a poor man with discernment sees through him.

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb qal infinitive construct

verses.flow: When the righteous triumph, there is great glory, but when the wicked rise, men hide themselves.
He who conceals his transgressions will not prosper, but he who confesses and forsakes them will find compassion (rachamim).
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse encapsulates the biblical doctrine of confession and repentance with extraordinary clarity. "Confesses" (yadah, H3034) carries the sense of publicly acknowledging, throwing one's hands up in surrender to truth. "Forsakes" (azav, H5800) demands action, not merely sentiment. TYPOLOGY: This proverb anticipates the NT pattern of confession leading to forgiveness (1 John 1:9). The word "compassion" (rachamim) is the plural of racham, the womb-love of God.

verses.flow: Blessed is the man who fears (yare) always, but he who hardens his heart will fall into calamity.
verses.note: HEBREW: The "fear" here is yare (H3372), covenantal awe and reverence, not merely dread. The verb "hardens" uses the root qashah (H7185), applied to Pharaoh's heart in the Exodus narrative (Exod 7:3), linking this proverb to the archetypal pattern of hardened rebellion leading to destruction. THEOLOGICAL: Perpetual, God-ward fear functions as a spiritual immune system against moral catastrophe.

verses.flow: A roaring lion or a charging bear is a wicked ruler over a poor people.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The lion (ari, H738) and bear (dov, H1677) were apex predators of the ancient Near East, known from Mesopotamian royal iconography as symbols of destructive power. Rulers in ANE culture were often depicted as lions; the proverb subversively applies this imagery to the wicked ruler as a predator against his own people rather than their protector.

verses.flow: A prince who lacks understanding is also a great oppressor, but he who hates unjust gain will prolong his days.

words.g: in/with blood of man or an animal

verses.flow: A man who is laden with the guilt of human blood will flee to the pit; let no one support him.
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase "laden with the guilt of human blood" renders the idiom 'ashuk b'dam nefesh — oppressed or burdened with the blood of a soul (nefesh, H5315). The command "let no one support him" (al yitmekehu) is a stark social decree: the blood-guilty man is to receive no communal shelter or assistance in his flight. THEOLOGICAL: This reflects the lex talionis principle and the sanctity of human life as image-bearers (Gen 9:6).

verses.flow: Whoever walks in integrity will be delivered, but he who is crooked in his ways will fall all at once.
Whoever works his land will have plenty of bread, but he who follows empty pursuits will have plenty of poverty.
A faithful man will abound with blessings, but whoever hastens to be rich will not go unpunished.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Faithful man" translates ish emunim (H529), a man of steadfast faithfulness and reliability — the same root as emunah (faith/fidelity). The contrast with the one who "hastens to be rich" (bahal, H926, to be rash or alarmed) reveals that covetous urgency is a form of faithlessness. TYPOLOGY: The faithful steward who abounds with blessing anticipates the parable of the talents (Matt 25:21).

verses.flow: To show partiality is not good, yet for a piece of bread a man will transgress.

words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: A man with an evil eye hastens after wealth and does not know that poverty will come upon him.
Whoever rebukes a man will afterward find more favor than he who flatters with the tongue.
Whoever robs his father or his mother and says, "It is no transgression," is a companion to a man who destroys.
A man of wide appetite stirs up strife, but he who trusts in YHWH will be enriched.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Wide appetite" or "greedy soul" renders rachav nefesh (H7342 + H5315) — literally "broad of soul/appetite," a metaphor for insatiable desire that creates relational conflict. Contrast with the "enlarged heart" given by wisdom. The verb translated "enriched" (yedushen) comes from deshen (H1878), to grow fat or prosperous — an agricultural image of abundance. THEOLOGICAL: Trust in YHWH is presented as the antidote to the social chaos generated by self-serving desire.

verses.flow: Whoever trusts in his own heart is a fool, but he who walks in wisdom will be delivered.
He who gives to the poor will not lack, but he who hides his eyes will get many a curse.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The promise of sufficiency to the giver and curse to the withholder reflects covenantal economics — generosity to the poor is not optional charity but an expression of covenant faithfulness. "Hides his eyes" (master einav) is a deliberate act of willful blindness to need. TYPOLOGY: The pattern anticipates the generosity ethic of the early church (Acts 2:44-45) and Christ's teaching that giving to the poor is giving to him (Matt 25:40).

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb qal infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: When the wicked rise, men hide themselves, but when they perish, the righteous increase.

words.g: the nape or back of the neck

verses.flow: A man who, being often reproved, stiffens his neck shall suddenly be broken, and that without remedy.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Stiffens his neck" (qasheh-oref, H7185+H6203) is a recurring idiom in the Hebrew Bible for stubborn, willful resistance to correction or divine authority (cf. Deut 9:13; Neh 9:16). "Without remedy" (ein marpe, H4832) appears also in 2 Chr 36:16 describing the hardened generation before the Babylonian exile — a stark warning that protracted rebellion forfeits the possibility of restoration. THEOLOGICAL: The structure of this proverb mirrors the covenantal consequence pattern: repeated rejection of reproof hardens the will until divine judgment falls suddenly and irremediably (cf. Prov 6:15; Rom 2:5).

words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb qal infinitive construct

verses.flow: When the righteous increase, the people rejoice; but when a wicked man rules, the people groan.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This verse establishes a direct connection between the moral character of leadership and the welfare of the populace, a principle foundational to biblical political theology. The verb rendered "groan" (anach, H584) describes the cry of Israel under Egyptian oppression (Exod 2:23), linking the suffering caused by wicked rule to slavery-like conditions. TYPOLOGY: The righteous ruler who brings joy foreshadows the Messianic King whose reign brings ultimate rejoicing to his people (Ps 72:1-7; Isa 9:6-7).

words.g: and + and to tend a flock

verses.flow: A man who loves wisdom brings joy to his father, but a companion of prostitutes wastes his wealth.

words.g: to pull down or in pieces
words.m: verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: A king by justice establishes a land, but a man who takes bribes tears it down.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Takes bribes" (ish terumot, H8641) is literally "a man of heave-offerings" or "a man of contributions" — a cutting irony: the term terumot is used for sacred priestly offerings, here co-opted for corrupt tribute. THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between justice (mishpat) that builds a nation and exaction that tears it down reflects the covenantal principle that righteous governance is stewardship under YHWH's kingship (Deut 17:14-20). TYPOLOGY: The ideal king who establishes the land by justice anticipates the Messiah of whom it is said, "Of the increase of his government and of peace (shalom) there will be no end" (Isa 9:7).

verses.flow: A man who flatters his neighbor spreads a net for his feet.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: In the transgression of an evil man there is a snare, but the righteous man runs and rejoices.
The righteous man knows the cause of the poor; the wicked man does not understand such knowledge.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Knows the cause" (yodea din, H3045+H1779) — da'at here denotes active, engaged understanding that issues in advocacy, not mere intellectual cognizance. The "cause" (din) is a legal term for the right or legal case of the poor. THEOLOGICAL: Knowledge of the poor's cause is presented not as optional virtue but as the defining mark of the righteous person, consistent with the prophetic tradition that true covenant fidelity includes defending the vulnerable (Isa 1:17; Jer 22:16; Amos 5:12).

verses.flow: Scoffers set a city aflame, but wise men turn away wrath.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: When a wise man has a dispute with a foolish man, the fool rages and laughs, and there is no rest.

words.g: blood of man or an animal

verses.flow: Bloodthirsty men hate the blameless, but the upright seek his life.
verses.note: HEBREW: The second clause is textually debated. The MT reads literally "and the upright seek his life (soul)" — the antecedent being the blameless man. This is often emended, but the MT can be read as the upright seeking to preserve the life of the blameless man whom the bloodthirsty hate. THEOLOGICAL: The hatred of the wicked for the blameless is a pervasive Wisdom theme (Ps 37:32; John 15:18-19), rooted in the enmity between the seed of the woman and the seed of the serpent (Gen 3:15).

words.m: verb piel imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: A fool gives full vent to his spirit, but a wise man holds it back and quiets it.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Gives full vent" (yotsi kol-rucho, H3318+H3605+H7307) — literally "sends forth all his spirit/breath." The term ruach (H7307) here denotes inner disposition and emotional energy. The wise man "holds back" or "stills" it (yeshabechenah, H7623). THEOLOGICAL: The contrast between unbridled and disciplined expression of the inner life is a central Wisdom concern, pointing toward the Spirit-formed character described in the New Covenant (Gal 5:22-23).

verses.flow: If a ruler listens to falsehood, all his servants will be wicked.
The poor man and the oppressor meet together; YHWH gives light to the eyes of both of them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The meeting of the poor man and the oppressor (literally "man of oppressions," ish tokafot) under YHWH's providential governance is a striking assertion of divine sovereignty over social inequity. DIVINE_COUNCIL: YHWH's giving of light to the eyes of both — the oppressed and the oppressor — asserts that all human life derives its illuminating principle from the one God who governs all nations and all classes (cf. Job 31:13-15; Prov 22:2). This counters any polytheistic framework where distinct deities govern distinct social tiers.

verses.flow: A king who judges the poor with faithfulness — his throne will be established forever.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The throne established "forever" by faithful judgment of the poor echoes the Davidic covenant promise (2 Sam 7:16; Ps 89:36-37) and its ultimate fulfillment in the Messianic King (Ps 72:2,4; Luke 1:32-33). THEOLOGICAL: The qualifier "with faithfulness" (be'emet, H571) frames just judgment of the poor not as political strategy but as covenantal fidelity — the king as YHWH's vice-regent is obligated to reflect divine justice (hesed u'emet) in his administration.

verses.flow: The rod and reproof give wisdom, but a child left to himself brings shame to his mother.
verses.note: HEBREW: "The rod" (shevet, H7626) is the same word used for a ruler's scepter and a tribal designation, connoting structured authority. "Left to himself" (meshulab, H7971 passive) literally "sent away" or "released without restraint." THEOLOGICAL: Biblical parental discipline is grounded in the nature of human fallenness — the child's will is not naturally inclined toward wisdom and must be shaped through corrective guidance (cf. Prov 22:15; Heb 12:5-11).

verses.flow: When the wicked increase, transgression increases, but the righteous will look upon their downfall.

conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Discipline your son, and he will give you rest; yes, he will give delight to your soul.
Where there is no vision (chazon), the people cast off restraint, but blessed is he who keeps the Torah (torah).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Vision" (chazon, H2377) denotes prophetic revelation — the word of YHWH delivered through his prophets, not merely general foresight or strategic planning. "Cast off restraint" (yipara, H6544) echoes Exod 32:25, used of Israel's chaos at the golden calf incident when Moses was absent. "Torah" (H8451) here may refer broadly to divine instruction rather than narrowly to Mosaic law. THEOLOGICAL: This verse draws a direct line between active prophetic revelation and social order — where the living Word of YHWH is absent, human communities unravel. TYPOLOGY: The keeping of Torah as the path of blessing anticipates Christ's affirmation that he came not to abolish but to fulfill the law and the prophets (Matt 5:17-19).

verses.flow: By words alone a servant is not disciplined, for though he understands, there will be no response.
Do you see a man who is hasty in his words? There is more hope for a fool than for him.
Whoever pampers his servant from youth — in the end he will be an heir.
verses.note: HEBREW: The final word of this verse, "manon" (H4497), is a hapax legomenon — appearing only here in the entire Hebrew Bible — making its precise meaning uncertain. Ancient versions differ: LXX reads "he will become a servant," the Syriac Peshitta reads "he will cause grief." A plausible reading based on cognate languages and context is that the pampered servant eventually claims inheritance rights, disrupting the household order. HISTORICAL: The treatment of household servants in the ANE context was governed by codes such as Hammurabi's, which recognized that elevated servant status could lead to legal complications regarding inheritance.

verses.flow: A man of wrath stirs up strife, and a hot-tempered man abounds in transgression.

words.m: verb hiphil imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: A man's pride will bring him low, but a lowly spirit will obtain honor.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The inversion principle — pride brings low, humility brings honor — is axiomatic in Hebrew Wisdom and pervasive in prophetic literature (Isa 2:12-17; 57:15). TYPOLOGY: This proverb receives its fullest expression in the person of the Messiah, who humbled himself and was therefore exalted (Phil 2:6-11), and in his teaching that "everyone who exalts himself will be humbled, and everyone who humbles himself will be exalted" (Luke 14:11). James 4:6 and 1 Pet 5:5 echo the same Wisdom tradition.

verses.flow: Whoever shares with a thief hates his own soul; he hears the oath but discloses nothing.
The fear of man lays a snare, but whoever trusts in YHWH will be set on high.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Fear of man" (cherdath adam, H2731+H120) denotes the anxious dread of human opinion or power that paralyzes moral action. "Lays a snare" (yiten moqesh, H5414+H4170) — the snare (moqesh) is the same term used for the idolatrous traps that ensnare Israel in the conquest narratives (Exod 23:33). "Set on high" (yesugab, H7682) carries connotations of being placed in an inaccessible, fortified height — a refuge beyond the reach of human threat. THEOLOGICAL: Trust in YHWH rather than human power is the recurring call of Psalms and the prophets (Ps 118:6-9; Isa 31:1-3; Jer 17:5-8), and this proverb encapsulates the fundamental choice between human and divine security.

verses.flow: Many seek the face of a ruler, but it is from YHWH that a man's judgment comes.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: While many seek access and favor from earthly rulers (the face of a ruler, pene-moshel), ultimate justice and vindication belong to YHWH alone. This is a significant assertion of divine sovereignty over all human judicial and political processes. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The implicit claim here is that even the decrees of human rulers are subordinate to YHWH's governance — a theme consistent with the divine council framework in which YHWH retains final authority over all nations and their rulers (Ps 82:8; Dan 4:17; Prov 21:1).

verses.flow: An unjust man is an abomination to the righteous, and one who is upright in the way is an abomination to the wicked.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The closing verse of the chapter presents an absolute and irreconcilable antithesis between the righteous and the wicked — each finds the other to be an abomination (toevah, H8441), the strongest Hebrew term of moral revulsion, used elsewhere for idolatry (Deut 7:25) and sexual immorality (Lev 18:22). TYPOLOGY: This mutual abomination reflects the cosmic enmity of Gen 3:15 and the separation that the Messiah himself declared he came to bring (Matt 10:34-36), as alignment with truth necessarily creates antagonism with those aligned with falsehood.

verses.flow: The words of Agur son of Jakeh. The oracle. The declaration of the man to Ithiel, to Ithiel and Ucal:
verses.note: HEBREW: The term "oracle" (massa, Strong's H4853) can mean both "burden" and "utterance/oracle," suggesting a weighty prophetic declaration. Agur and Ithiel are otherwise unknown figures, though some rabbinical traditions identify Agur allegorically with Solomon. The phrase "to Ithiel and Ucal" may alternatively be read as a despairing cry: "I am weary, O God, I am weary and worn out" (reading 'iti el and ukhal as verbs), following some modern textual analyses of the consonantal Hebrew.

verses.flow: Surely I am more brutish than any man, and I do not have the understanding of a man;
I have not learned wisdom, nor do I have knowledge of the Holy One.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "the Holy One" (ha-Qadosh) uses the singular form, in contrast to the more common plural form seen in Job and Isaiah. Agur's confession of ignorance before the Holy One echoes Job's humbling before YHWH (Job 38-40), establishing that true wisdom begins with the acknowledgment of creaturely limitation before divine transcendence.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: Who has ascended into heaven and come down? Who has gathered the wind in his fists? Who has bound the waters in a garment? Who has established all the ends of the earth? What is his name, and what is the name of his son — if you know?
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: This verse echoes the divine challenge speeches of Job 38-39 and anticipates the incarnation language of the New Testament. "Who has ascended into heaven and come down?" is quoted in John 3:13 in reference to the Son of Man. The phrase "what is his son's name?" is theologically remarkable in a wisdom text, hinting at a divine sonship that transcends mere royal metaphor. Some divine council interpreters see here an awareness of the heavenly hierarchy with YHWH and his unique Son. (John 3:13, Job 38:4, Deut 32:8)

verses.flow: Every word of God (Eloah) is refined (tserufah); he is a shield to those who take refuge in him.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word "refined" (tserufah, Strong's H6884) is the same term used for the smelting of precious metals, indicating that every divine word has been purified of all dross and impurity. The word translated "Eloah" is the singular poetic form of God, used frequently in Job and in elevated hymnic or wisdom contexts, underscoring the personal and awesome nature of the divine being addressed. (Ps 18:30, 2 Sam 22:31)

words.m: conjunction + verb niphal sequential perfect 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Do not add to his words, lest he reprove you and you be found a liar.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This prohibition against adding to God's words anticipates the canonical closing formula of Deuteronomy 4:2 and Revelation 22:18-19, forming a significant bookend in the theology of Scripture's sufficiency and integrity. To be "found a liar" before God echoes the concern for truthfulness before the divine witness central to covenant speech. (Deut 4:2, Rev 22:18)

verses.flow: Two things I ask of you; do not deny them to me before I die:

words.g: to pluck off or pull to pieces
words.m: verb hiphil imperative 2nd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Falsehood and lying words remove far from me; give me neither poverty nor riches — feed me with the bread of my portion,

conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing

conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 1st common sing

verses.flow: lest I be full and deny you and say, "Who is YHWH?" or lest I be poor and steal and profane the name of my God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Agur's prayer reflects a remarkable balance of covenantal concern: both prosperity and poverty carry spiritual dangers. The phrase "who is YHWH?" echoes Pharaoh's defiance in Exodus 5:2, making the denial of YHWH in times of abundance the gravest possible spiritual failure. This tension between material blessing and covenant faithfulness is a central theme in Deuteronomy. (Exod 5:2, Deut 8:10-14)

verses.flow: Do not slander a servant to his master, lest he curse you and you be held guilty.
There is a generation that curses its father and does not bless its mother.
There is a generation that is pure in its own eyes, yet is not washed from its filth.
There is a generation — oh how lofty are their eyes, and their eyelids are lifted high!

words.g: from ruddy i.e. a human being

verses.flow: There is a generation whose teeth are swords and whose jaw-teeth are knives, to devour the poor from the earth and the needy from among mankind.
The leech has two daughters: "Give," and "Give." Three things are never satisfied; four things never say, "Enough!":
verses.note: STRUCTURE: Verses 15-33 employ a sustained numerical (x, x+1) literary pattern common in ancient Near Eastern wisdom literature (cf. Ugaritic texts and Amos 1-2). This "graded numerical saying" creates rhetorical suspense, with the final item in each list bearing the greatest emphasis. The leech's two daughters named "Give" and "Give" introduces the theme of insatiable appetite that runs through the following lists.

words.g: Hades or the world of the dead

verses.flow: Sheol, and the barren womb, the earth that is never satisfied with water, and fire that never says, "Enough!"

verses.flow: The eye that mocks a father and scorns to obey a mother — the ravens of the valley will pick it out, and the young eagles will eat it.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The graphic image of eye-plucking ravens serves as a stark covenantal curse upon those who dishonor parents, recalling the fifth commandment (Exod 20:12) and its promised blessing. Dishonoring parents in the Old Testament is an offense of covenant-breaking severity (Deut 21:18-21), and the imagery of unburied carrion represents the ultimate covenantal shame — dying without proper burial rites. (Exod 20:12, Deut 21:18-21)

words.m: verb qal perfect 1st common sing + suffix

verses.flow: Three things are too wonderful for me; four things I do not understand:

words.g: a sea or large body of water

verses.flow: the way of an eagle in the sky, the way of a serpent on a rock, the way of a ship in the heart of the sea, and the way of a man with a young woman.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word "way" (derek, Strong's H1870) is the unifying term across all four mysteries — each "way" leaves no trace, no permanent mark, embodying the concept of things whose process is hidden from human observation. The "way of a man with a young woman" (almah, Strong's H5959) uses the same word as Isaiah 7:14, the famous virgin/young woman passage, though here it plainly means a young woman of marriageable age without the prophetic weight of the Isaiah context.
