conjunction + verb qal sequential perfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: This is the way of an adulterous woman: she eats and wipes her mouth and says, "I have done no wrong."
Under three things the earth trembles; under four it cannot bear up:
a servant when he becomes king, and a fool when he is filled with food,
an unloved woman when she gets a husband, and a maidservant when she displaces her mistress.
Four things on earth are small, yet they are exceedingly wise:

words.m: conjunction + verb hiphil sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: the ants are a people not strong, yet they prepare their food in the summer;

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur

verses.flow: the rock badgers (shaphan) are a people not mighty, yet they make their houses in the rocks;
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "rock badger" or hyrax (shaphan, Strong's H8227) is a small, furry mammal native to the rocky cliffs of the Levant and classified as ceremonially unclean in Leviticus 11:5. Despite its weakness and unclean status, its wisdom in choosing secure dwelling places in rocky crags makes it a fitting example of compensating for natural disadvantage through shrewd positioning — a recurring theme in Israelite wisdom.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: the locusts have no king, yet all of them march in rank;
the lizard you can seize with your hands, yet it is in the palaces of kings.
Three things are stately in their stride; four are stately in their going:
the lion, which is mightiest among beasts and does not turn back before any;
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The lion as the mightiest and most fearless of beasts carries rich messianic resonance. In the blessing of Jacob (Gen 49:9), Judah is a lion's cub, and in Revelation 5:5 the Messiah is "the Lion of the tribe of Judah." The lion's refusal to turn back before any foe typologically anticipates the unrelenting royal courage of the Davidic Messiah. (Gen 49:9, Rev 5:5)

verses.flow: the strutting rooster, the he-goat, and a king when his army is with him.
If you have been foolish in exalting yourself, or if you have been devising evil — put your hand on your mouth!

words.g: blood of man or an animal

verses.flow: For the churning of milk produces curds, and the wringing of the nose produces blood, and the churning of anger produces strife.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verse employs a triple use of the verb mits (Strong's H4330, "to press/churn/wring"), creating a powerful rhetorical climax through repetition. The final proverb anchors the entire chapter's wisdom in practical cause-and-effect observation: just as physical processes inevitably produce their outcomes, so the "pressing" or provoking of anger (aph, meaning both "nose" and "anger" in Hebrew, Strong's H639) will just as inevitably produce strife. The wordplay on "nose/anger" is deliberate and untranslatable in English.

words.m: verb piel perfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: The words of Lemuel, king of Massa, which his mother taught him:
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Lemuel" and "Massa" are connected — Massa was an Arabian tribal region (Gen 25:14), suggesting this chapter may preserve wisdom from a non-Israelite king whose mother instructed him, reflecting the ancient Near Eastern tradition of royal instruction literature (cf. Egyptian "Instruction of Amenemhat"). The oracle genre (massa) also carries prophetic weight.

verses.flow: What, my son? And what, O son of my womb? And what, O son of my vows?
verses.note: HEBREW: The triple rhetorical question — "mah" (what?) repeated — intensifies the maternal urgency. "Son of my vows" (bar-nedarai) indicates the child was consecrated to God, paralleling Hannah's vow concerning Samuel (1 Sam 1:11). This personal dedication frames the entire instruction as sacred charge.

verses.flow: Do not give your strength to women, nor your ways to that which destroys kings.

words.g: and + to/for probably to be heavy
words.m: conjunction + preposition + verb qal participle active

verses.flow: Not for kings, O Lemuel, not for kings to drink wine, nor for rulers to crave strong drink;

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing
conjunction + verb piel imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: lest they drink and forget what has been decreed, and pervert the cause (din) of all the afflicted.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Din" (Strong's H1779) means cause, judgment, or legal case. The warning is that drunken rulers pervert justice for the afflicted (aniyim). THEOLOGICAL: Scripture consistently ties kingship to covenant justice; a king who neglects the poor breaks his fundamental mandate (Ps 72:1-4).

verses.flow: Give strong drink to one who is perishing, and wine to the one whose soul is bitter;

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperfect 3rd masc sing

verses.flow: let him drink and forget his poverty, and remember his misery no more.
Open your mouth for the mute, for the cause of all the sons of the passing away.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Sons of the passing away" (bene chaloph) is a difficult phrase — some render it "sons of desolation" or "sons of those appointed to die," likely referring to those condemned, orphaned, or without legal advocates. The king is charged to be their voice before the court.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal imperative 2nd masc sing

verses.flow: Open your mouth, judge righteously, and defend the cause of the poor and the needy.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The royal charge to "judge righteously" and "defend the cause of the poor and needy" anticipates the messianic King whose reign is marked by justice and equity (Isa 11:4; Ps 72:4, 12-14). Christ fulfills this as the advocate (parakletos) who defends the spiritually destitute (1 John 2:1).

verses.flow: An excellent wife (eshet chayil) — who can find her? For her worth is far above rubies (peninim).
verses.note: HEBREW: "Eshet chayil" (Strong's H802 + H2428) — literally "woman of valor/strength/ability." The same word "chayil" is used of mighty warriors and men of excellence (e.g., Boaz in Ruth 2:1 is called "gibbor chayil"). "Peninim" (rubies or coral, Strong's H6443) denotes the rarest of precious things. STRUCTURE: Verses 10-31 form an acrostic poem, each verse beginning with a successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet (aleph through tav), framing this woman as the summation of all wisdom.

verses.flow: The heart of her husband trusts in her, and in gain he shall not lack.

words.m: verb qal perfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

words.g: in/with good in the widest sense

verses.flow: She does him good, and not harm, all the days of her life.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: She seeks wool and flax, and she works with the delight of her hands.
She is like the ships of a merchant; from afar she brings her bread.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd fem sing
conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: And she rises while it is yet night, and gives food to her household, and a portion (choq) to her maidens.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Choq" (Strong's H2706) means a prescribed portion, statute, or allotted share — the same word used for divine decrees. Her administration of her household mirrors the order and structure of divine governance, an echo of wisdom's role in creation (Prov 8:27-30).

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd fem sing + suffix

verses.flow: She considers a field and acquires it; from the fruit of her hands she plants a vineyard.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel sequential imperfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: She girds her loins with strength, and makes her arms strong.
She tastes that her merchandise is good; her lamp does not go out by night.

words.g: and the hollow hand or palm

verses.flow: She puts her hands to the distaff, and her palms hold the spindle.
She spreads out her palm to the poor, and she stretches out her hands to the needy.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Her open palm to the poor (dal) and needy (evyon) directly enacts the covenant ethic demanded of Israel (Deut 15:7-11). The "woman of valor" is not merely domestic — she embodies the hesed of YHWH extended into the community.

verses.flow: She is not afraid of snow for her household, for all her household are clothed in scarlet.
She makes coverings for herself; her clothing is fine linen and purple.

words.m: preposition + verb qal infinitive construct + suffix

verses.flow: Her husband is known in the gates, when he sits among the elders of the land.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: She makes linen garments and sells them, and supplies sashes to the merchant.

words.m: conjunction + verb qal sequential imperfect 3rd fem sing

verses.flow: Strength and honor (hadar) are her clothing, and she laughs at the time to come.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Hadar" (Strong's H1926) means splendor, majesty, or ornament — a word frequently used of YHWH's own glory (Ps 96:6; 104:1). Her clothing of strength and honor inverts the vanity of external adornment (v. 30), clothing her instead in qualities that belong to the divine character.

verses.flow: She opens her mouth with wisdom (chokmah), and the teaching (torah) of lovingkindness (hesed) is on her tongue.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Chokmah" (Strong's H2451) is the central theme of the entire book of Proverbs, personified as a woman in chapters 1-9. That the eshet chayil speaks chokmah identifies her as the embodiment of Lady Wisdom herself. "Torah of hesed" is a remarkable phrase — her very speech is covenantal instruction rooted in loyal love. TYPOLOGY: As Wisdom is personified and later identified with Christ in whom are hidden all treasures of wisdom (Col 2:3; 1 Cor 1:30), the eshet chayil who speaks chokmah and hesed prefigures the community shaped by the Messiah.

verses.flow: She watches over the ways of her household, and does not eat the bread of idleness.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word rendered "idleness" is "atslut" (Strong's H6103), meaning sluggishness or sloth — the antithesis of chayil (valor/diligence). Proverbs consistently contrasts the sluggard with the wise; here the woman of valor is the polar opposite of the fool who sleeps through life (Prov 6:9-11).

words.m: conjunction + verb piel sequential imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix
conjunction + verb piel sequential imperfect 3rd masc sing + suffix

verses.flow: Her children rise up and call her blessed; her husband also, and he praises her:
"Many daughters have done valiantly, but you surpass them all."
Charm is deceitful, and beauty is vanity (hevel); but a woman who fears YHWH — she shall be praised.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Hevel" (Strong's H1892) — the same word used throughout Ecclesiastes for "vanity/vapor/breath" — is here applied to physical beauty. "Yirat YHWH" (fear of YHWH) is the foundational principle of all wisdom in Proverbs (1:7; 9:10), making this verse the theological climax of the entire book. THEOLOGICAL: The book opens with "the fear of YHWH is the beginning of wisdom" and closes with a woman who fears YHWH — an intentional bookend that reveals the whole of Proverbs as instruction in what it means to fear YHWH embodied in a human life.

words.m: conjunction + verb piel imperfect 3rd masc plur + suffix

verses.flow: Give to her from the fruit of her hands, and let her works praise her in the gates.
verses.note: STRUCTURE: The acrostic concludes with tav, the final letter of the Hebrew alphabet, suggesting completeness and totality. To praise her "in the gates" — the seat of civic and judicial authority — is the highest public honor. TYPOLOGY: As the fruit of her hands brings her praise at the gates, so the works of the redeemed community, built by Wisdom-Incarnate, will be brought into the New Jerusalem (Rev 21:24-26), the ultimate city of the King.
